THE BOOK OF JOB

The Bible is a universal book; so, it has a universal appeal to all races of men and women alike, of any age, in all walks of life. For this reason its sixty six books reach out through more than twenty five centuries and appeal to a variety of people in a variety of places. It is not "one drop of truth in an ocean of truth," but rather all of the oceans condensed into one drop. If five hundred Bible readers, chosen at random from five different countries, were asked to name their favorite book in the Bible (or what they considered to be the greatest book in the Bible), most of them would respond with Romans, John, or the book of Revelation. Martin Luther (1483--1546) was partial to [Romans ]{.class_3759}and [Galatians. ]{.class_3759}Sam Jones (1847--1906) and John Wesley (1703--1791) loved the books of [Proverbs ]{.class_3759}and [James. ]{.class_3759}Most prophetic scholars are partial to [Daniel ]{.class_3759}and [Revelation. ]{.class_3759}A few Christians would choose the [Gospel of Luke ]{.class_3759}or the [First Epistle of Peter. ]{.class_3759}The unsaved man will usually choose [Matthew, ]{.class_3759}and the Pentecostal and Holiness groups will name [The Acts of the Apostles ]{.class_3759}nine times out of ten. Although [I Thessalonians ]{.class_3759}is the first book that a new convert should read to get grounded in New Testament Christian fundamentals, only a handful of people pay much attention to that book. Although [2 Corinthians ]{.class_3759}is the greatest book in the Bible on the ministry, most ministers prefer [The Acts of the Apostles. ]{.class_3759}A few hardy souls will stand by [Hebrews, ]{.class_3759}and occasionally one meets a Christian who picks an Old Testament book such as [Isaiah ]{.class_3759}or the [Psalms.]{.class_3759}

But the Book of Books for a [literary man ]{.class_3759}is [The Book of ]{.class_3759}Job. Furthermore, it is the book of books for any man who thinks deeply and feels deeply about [life. ]{.class_3759}Only [Psalms ]{.class_3759}pictures the heartcry of the soul as vividly, and only the book of [Proverbs ]{.class_3759}touches the wellsprings of wisdom which crop up in [The Book of Job. ]{.class_3759}Of [Job ]{.class_3759}Victor Hugo (1802--1885) has said, "This book is perhaps the greatest masterpiece of the human mind." Philip Schaff wrote, "It is without predecessor or rival." Thomas Carlyle said of it, "Nothing has been written of equal literary merit." Dean Bradley says: "It stands alone in the Bible, alone in the literature of the world as the very flower of inspired Hebrew poetry" [(Biblical Expositor, ]{.class_3759}Exell, Baker). Watson cites Samuel Davidson as saying: "Like a star of the first magnitude the brilliant genius of the writer of Job attracts the admiration of man" [(Expositor's Bible, ]{.class_3759}Vol. II, Eerdmans). Lord Tennyson (1809--1892) said that it was "the greatest poem of ancient or modern times," and Luther held that Job is "magnificent and sublime as no other book of scripture." And beyond these literary opinions stand two outstanding facts of history:

[1. The Book of Job ]{.class_3759}is the first real "book" ever written. Its predecessors---The [Eloquent Peasant ]{.class_3759}(1800 B.C.), [The Gilgamesh Epic ]{.class_3759}(2000 B.C.), [Ipuwer ]{.class_3759}(2000 B.C.), [Baba ]{.class_3759}(1600 B.C.), [The Priest of Sneferu ]{.class_3759}(2900 B.C.), [Phaht Ptahhotep ]{.class_3759}(2600 B.C.), and the so called [Book of the Dead ]{.class_3759}(3400 B.C.) all have four things in common:

a\. A lack of material on prophesy that comes to pass in history.

b\. A lack of scientific data beyond the time of their composition.

c\. A lack of plot and dramatic development.

d\. A lack of discussion concerning the first main problem that mankind has (and has always had and will always have): WHY DO THE RIGHTEOUS SUFFER?

Alongside the [Babylonian Tablets ]{.class_3759}(2400 B.C.), the [Tablets from Nippur ]{.class_3759}(2220 B.C.), [Ikhnaton ]{.class_3759}(1375 B.C.), [Amenemope]{.class_3759} (1400 B.C.), and the writings of Plato, Thales, Parmenides, Plotinus, and Heraclitus, [The Book of Job ]{.class_3759}stands out like Mt. Everest among the foothills of the Ozarks.

[2. The Book of Job ]{.class_3759}contains (among many other things) statements on the physical sciences unknown until after the time of Christopher Columbus (1492); prophetic utterances that will be fulfilled in the next thirty years; revelations on the spirit world that no Buddhist, Taoist, Brahmist, or Confucionist ever found; revelations on life after death fouftd nowhere in [The Book of the Dead; ]{.class_3759}spiritual illustrations of conversion and dedication found in the New Testament; types of Christ's atonement and mediatorship; and the answer to many problems of suffering and affliction that have no answer in the works of Plato, Socrates, Aristotle, Marx, Freud, Darwin, etc. When all is said and done, the first real book ever written (intra  or extra Canonical) sets the pace for every book that follows it, at least where those books deal with the basic human problems of suffering, affliction, and death.

"[Ye have heard of the patience of Job" ]{.class_3760}(James 5:11), for Job is one of the three men (Ezek. 14:20) whom the Lord picked when He wanted to show the world what a righteous man was. Job 31, for example, reveals a personal righteousness which goes far beyond the bare demands of the Ten Commandments.

Since we have sufficiently covered the bloopers of the [NASV ]{.class_3759}in our Commentaries on [Acts, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, ]{.class_3759}and [Colossians, ]{.class_3759}it will not be necessary here to list any more of the errors in that absurd translation. However, to keep abreast of the Laodicean Apostasy (1900--1980), we have chosen the [Living Bible ]{.class_3759}(1971) and the [New English Bible ]{.class_3759}(1970) as representatives of the modern so called "Bibles," and we will make occasional references to them in our comments. The [Living Bible, ]{.class_3759}as the [New American Standard "Bible," uses ]{.class_3759}the double tongued dodge of the Logical Positivists to sell. The book is plainly a [paraphrase ]{.class_3759}of the Bible, and Taylor admits this in his introduction; however, the front cover of the book says "The Living Bible paraphrased." [Then what is the Living Bible? You ]{.class_3759}see how these birds work? You are led to believe that what you are reading is [The Living Bible, ]{.class_3759}but the author has confessed (in small print inside!) that his work is only a paraphrase: A paraphrase of [WHAT?]{.class_3759} Well, the cover says that it is a paraphrase of [The Living Bible! ]{.class_3759}Observe: [The Living Bible---paraphrased. ]{.class_3759}Now this is so characteristic of the Laodicean approach that no one should be surprised. It fits nicely into the vendetta carried on by Fundamentalists and Conservatives since 1880 to overthrow the real "Living" Bible---the [AV ]{.class_3759}(1611). Readers of our series would recognize the double tonguing, double standard, two faced flimflam in advertising the book. Neal and Custer (Bob Jones University) do it constantly when they talk about "THE Greek text"---meaning [half a dozen ]{.class_3759}Hesychian texts printed by Nestle, Souter, Westcott, Hort, Weiss, Aland, et al. The Lockman Foundation is also quite adept at this type of thing; they advertise the [New American Standard VERSION ]{.class_3759}as the [New American Standard BIBLE. ]{.class_3759}Very typical. In the case of Taylor's work, you are left to believe that [The Living Bible ]{.class_3759}is Taylor's paraphrase of the "Living Bible!" What is the "Living Bible?" It isn't Taylor's [paraphrase ]{.class_3759}or the cover "should have said "The Living Bible---a paraphrase." But no, instead we see "The Living Bible---paraphrased."

Himmler, Goebbels, Eichman, Hitler, Hoess, Goering, and Dietrich mastered this type of ambiguity more than forty years ago, and the concentration camps of Nazi Germany were fed, staffed, operated, and occupied by the use of this type of terminology (see the Preface to [The Mark of the Beast, ]{.class_3759}1959). The outstanding thing about every translation on the market since 1884 has been this deliberate lying (see frontispiece to [The New Scofield Reference Bible), ]{.class_3759}couched in terminology the average man would not be able to analyze, since modern man no longer studies Latin and Greek roots and since the average college graduate of 1960--1980 has less than half the grasp of language and grammar that a high school graduate had between 1920 and 1940.

Taylor's work has sold over 8,000,000 copies, thanks to a vigorous promotion job conducted by Billy Graham. It is fitting, then, that we should include it in our commentary on [Job ]{.class_3759}and compare it with its corrupt counterparts---the [RSV, RV, ASV, ]{.class_3759}and the [Septuagint, ]{.class_3759}as well as the commentaries by Clarke, Lange, Jamieson, Fausset, Brown, Dummelow, and others. Taylor is an A 1 example of the modern premillennial "Fundamentalist" who plays down the wrath of God (see pp. 424, 436 in [The Living Bible, ]{.class_3759}1971), destroys cross references to [advanced revelation,]{.class_3759} (pp. 441, 445), compromises with the Jesuit Bible of 1582 (pp. 761, 994), and fails to find the truth when it is directly in front of his face (pp. 428, 777). The insertion of expressions such as "bitch" for ["rebellious woman" ]{.class_3760}(1 Samuel 20:30) and "going to the toilet" (1 Kings 18:27) for[ "or he is pursuing,"]{.class_3760} means little or nothing, as the word for ["dung"]{.class_3760} (Phil. 3:8) was not updated, nor were a dozen other Anglo Saxon expressions. Readers of Kenneth Taylor should always remember that he never published any "Living Bible ," What he did was publish his own [paraphrase ]{.class_3759}of the [Holy Bible,]{.class_3759} but he would not dare tell anyone this on the COVER of his work; so it slides by nicely as [The Living Bible. ]{.class_3759}It is not a Bible at all; it is a paraphrase of [the]{.class_3759} Bible. This is an entirely different thing from "The Living Bible---paraphrased," for the grammatical structure of that last phrase indicates that [some other book ]{.class_3759}was paraphrased, not the one you were reading. But as we said before, the Christian public is much too gullible and ignorant these days to follow this sleight of hand. They buy the book thinking they have a "Bible." It is no more a "Bible" than is an [ASV ]{.class_3759}or [Alice in Wonderland.]{.class_3759}

The title of Taylor's work is quite similar to the horseplay that goes on in modern schools of philosophy and "advanced thought." (Wiener, Margenau, Reichenbach, et al.). A fine analysis of this horseplay will be found in the book by Francis Schaeffer entitled [The God Who Is There, ]{.class_3759}(1968). The long and short of it is that following Hegel, Sartre, Dada, Gaugin, Van Gogh, Pollock, Miro, Cezanne, Mondrian, Camus, Kierkegaard, Klee, Einstein, Heidegger, Bernstein, Henry Miller to their terminus, produces an[ agnostic insanity ]{.class_3759}where words lose their meaning. The men listed above represent the "meaningless" school of philosophy as it is manifested in art, literature, science, religion, and music. As Schaeffer has well noted: When they say "data," they might just as well say "blip." The word "data" ceases to mean anything in the school of Logical Positivitism (Comte, Russell, Mach, et al.), exactly as harmonics, balance, shadow, perspective, composition, tone, chords, phrasing, dictions, timing, grammar, and plot cease to have any meaning in the avant garde of modern music, art, and literature. After nearly one hundred years of juggling terms, using words indiscriminately, mangling chords, smearing the wrong colors together, ignoring perspective and proportion, distorting tones and values, and mishandling "data" and "evidence," it is now quite proper in Christian circles to lie openly. [The Living Bible ]{.class_3759}is not "paraphrased"---it is a paraphrase. [The New Scofield Reference Bible ]{.class_3759}is [not ]{.class_3759}the [Authorized Version ]{.class_3759}(although the frontispiece states that it is), and the [NASV ]{.class_3759}is no more the [New American Standard BIBLE ]{.class_3759}than Barth, Niebuhr, Tillich, Alan Richardson, and John Robinson are Bible believing Christians.

In this commentary, our consistent working premise will continue to be that the [Authorized Version ]{.class_3759}of 1611 is the infallible word of the Living God, preserved without proven error. We accept it as the ultimate absolute (in the material realm) by which all other absolutes (or relatives) are to be judged. We simply lay it alongside the work of any philosopher, musician, doctor, lawyer, scientist, Greek scholar, archaeologist, Hebrew scholar, reviser, commentator, teacher, priest, pope, bishop, preacher, or educator; and where they disagree with it, we discard them.

We will not waste a lot of time correcting the Fundamental and Conservative scholars on the [Book of Job, ]{.class_3759}since, to them, [Job ]{.class_3759}is primarily a book of devotional and spiritual matter and accordingly they have largely ignored the prophetic passages in it. Nearly anyone can write a good commentary on Job as long as he sticks to the [devotional ]{.class_3759}and [spiritual ]{.class_3759}matters, for they are stated so forcibly and so clearly that there really is not much room for a variety of interpretations. We may say, therefore, that the majority of commentaries on [Job ]{.class_3759}written by Conservatives and Fundamentalists are "good reading" or "edifying." More than this we cannot say, for it will be more than apparent when we get to Job 26, 37, 38, 40, and 41 that 99 percent of the Conservatives have rejected the words of the Holy Spirit as blatantly and as arrogantly as did Tillich, Niebuhr, Ralph Sockman, Bishop Pike, or Eugene Carson Blake. As we have said on a score of occasions, the basic attitude of the modern Conservative scholar toward the [authority ]{.class_3759}of the Bible is not much different from the attitude of any atheist or Satanist.

We will approach this great book as we approach all of the sixty six books of the [King James Authorized Holy Bible: ]{.class_3759}that is, with complete confidence and trust in the text [as it stands ]{.class_3759}(1 Thess. 2:13) and with complete lack of confidence or faith in any "godly" scholar who thinks he is smart enough to meddle with it. We insert the word "godly," for this is the contemporary bunko used by destructive critics who profess to believe the "fundamentals" of the faith; they are all obsessed with the wild idea that a man can tear up as much of the Bible as suits him as long as he has a reputation for being "godly." (Readers who have studied our Commentaries on [Matthew, Exodus, Acts, Revelation, ]{.class_3759}and [Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, and Colossians ]{.class_3759}are well aware of the fact that the adversaries of the [AV]{.class_3759} (1611) cannot answer any of the problems posed to them by the authority of the [King James ]{.class_3759}text. The rebuttal so far (from 1969 to 1978) has been name calling (which is perfectly all right with us), slander and gossip (help yourself; it is a free country), generalized statements that avoid the textual problems being discussed, and contemptuous sneers such as "Oh that!" or "Oh him!" These open admissions of ignorance and lack of concrete evidence pretty well show the student what he is dealing with; so, we shall take the "accredited scholarship" of such "qualified authorities" with the same amount of seriousness that they take ours. After all, they have had nearly nine years now to read in detail how the [King James Bible ]{.class_3759}has corrected [the men who taught them edge, ]{.class_3759}and if all they can muster in nine years is "Oh that radical extremist" or "Oh that fanatical crackpot," they don't really know enough about the Bible or the Author of the Bible to discuss anything Biblical. In this commentary, we will continue to correct the numerous errors of the men [who wrote the textbooks ]{.class_3759}for the faculty members of BJU, Pensacola Christian College, Grace Bible Institute, Liberty University, etc. This is not difficult to do, and we are not boasting about it. After all, we have an infallible Book of absolute truth, and with it we can correct Keil, Lange, Delitzsch, Excell, Driver, Davidson, Umbreit, Ewald, Peake, Budde, Good, Meyer, Dillman, Duhm, Marx, Siegfield, and Schlottmann quite nicely without exerting any special effort.

We do this not through blind prejudice or "fanatical extremism," but only after carefully examining the ideologies, philosophies, criticisms, conjectures, studies, researches, analyses, objections, variances, and alterations made by these men who think they are smart enough to correct the Bible. Our assumptions are as sound as any that Einstein (or Lorenz, Miller, Fermi, et al.) made in the field of physics, and a great deal more sound than any the United Nations or the Roman Catholic Church have made in the field of international politics in the last five hundred years.

1\. We assume that God is alive and well.

2\. We assume that He is concerned about His material creation.

3\. We assume that He has communicated Himself to this creation by the same means that men communicate with each other---by WORDS.

4\. We assume that a God who is powerful enough to [verbally inspire ]{.class_3759}a perfect revelation is powerful enough to [preserve it without error.]{.class_3759}

5\. We assume that this revelation must be [easily accessible ]{.class_3759}to sinners who are seeking the truth.

Beyond this we deal with facts, not assumptions (see [The Monarch of the Books, ]{.class_3759}1973, and [The Christian's Handbook of Manuscript Evidence, ]{.class_3759}1970).

If others are still in darkness searching for [the truth ]{.class_3759}(2 Cor. 4:4), we are not. And if those who profess to have found the Incarnate Truth are still searching for the written truth (John 17:17), we are not. We have the written truth and the Incarnate Truth, and although our dogmatism and convictions may infuriate and inflame the entire body of Liberal and Conservative scholarship till they are mad enough to kill, it will not alter either truth. We have never had (nor will we ever have, by the grace of God) to make any alterations or adjustments with Liberal or Conservative scholarship where they have hidden behind a "tolerant agnosticism" in order to supplant the absolute truth with their own "broad minded" ignorance. If any man be ignorant, Paul says ["let him be ignorant" ]{.class_3760}(1 Cor. 14:38), and we will apply that verse just as quickly to a Ph.D. in a fundamental seminary, with thirty years of Greek grammar, as to an unsaved D.D. on the [RSV ]{.class_3759}revision committee. Ignorance is ignorance no matter what your profession is (see Hills, [King James Version Defended, ]{.class_3759}1973, pp. 84--85).

Since ["thy word is a lamp\...and a light"]{.class_3760} (Psa. 119:105), we are about to receive Divine illumination from the source of all truth, the Author and Finisher of our Salvation, the Giver of every good and perfect gift, and the Beginning and the Ending of all knowledge and wisdom. With such an Instructor we shall not lose any sleep over the pious drivel of deceived sinners who have disguised their hatred for Truth with such terms as "scientific exegesis," "critical editions," "unquestioned scholarship," "monumental studies," and "vast technical resources." Who needs a pocket flashlight when he has a portable 200 watt lamp? We will believe what God has said, as He said it, in the context in which He placed it; and where four thousand "dedicated geniuses" or "godly scholars" alter these truths, we shall overlook them as cheerfully and as quickly as Charles Atlas would overlook a crippled old man who accidently stepped on his toes. After all, we have the [Author]{.class_3759} of the Book, and we have [the ]{.class_3759}Book. He has told us to ["search the scriptures" ]{.class_3760}(John 5:39), and He has placed the word "nigh unto us" (Romans 10:8). With such an orientation we have no more need of the destructive scholarship of Keil, Delitzsch, Gesenius, Harkavy, Umbreit, Fausset, Maurer, Moffat, and Mr. Good than we have need of a belly full of strychnine.

These men (plus many others in the Scholars Union) are at a real disadvantage anyway when dealing with scriptural problems, for they have no absolute authority but their own brains. When they correct our remarks (or God's Book), they have only the fallible findings of foolish sinners to work with. We have a "straightedge" that never wavers and never varies in measurement. We shall judge all of the Hebrew texts (DeRossi, Kennicott, and the Masoretic texts) and Greek texts ([Vaticanus, Sinaiticus, ]{.class_3759}etc.) by the [King James ]{.class_3759}(1611), and where they err, we shall correct them with implicit faith, believing that God made no mistakes in preserving His infallible word, without proven error.

Ministerial students who are still being tricked into attending schools that "stand without apology for the absolute authority of the Bible" (Bob Jones University, Wheaton, Pensacola Christian Colelge, Baptist Bible, Hyles-Anderson, Tennessee Temple, etc.) will soon learn that there are not two major Christian universities or colleges in America which believe anything of the kind. "The Bible" about which these men speak is a nonexistent, extinct, non available, hypothetical "original"---which they would correct as quickly as they corrected the [AV ]{.class_3759}(1611) if they ever found it. The trick in modern Christian education is to maintain a [double standard ]{.class_3759}(see booklet in color on [The Monarch of the Books), ]{.class_3759}so that the school itself can play god as the final authority where the two standards [conflict. ]{.class_3759}This explains the peculiar mystical voodoo that goes on in publications like [Faith ]{.class_3759}magazine, [The Biblical Viewpoint, ]{.class_3759}and the [Sword of the Lord, ]{.class_3759}which says in effect: Christian scholarship is the final authority, and [we ]{.class_3759}will tell you what is "reliable" and what is not "reliable" until you get as smart as we are, and then you too can tell someone else what to believe and what not to believe, until they are as smart as you are.

But to those of us who have seen the old Monarch of the forest trample down (and mutilate) the work of the commentators (see Commentaries on [Exodus, Minor Prophets, Genesis,]{.class_3759} and[ Proverbs ]{.class_3759}through eighteen centuries, all of this is wasted time and discussion. We know the Book is quite able to judge [any sinner ]{.class_3759}no matter how smart he thinks he is, and just because he is saved and "godly" or "dedicated" doesn't mean that his old nature has been "eradicated." Flesh is still flesh, and when it has rotted in the grave, the [AV ]{.class_3759}(1611) will still be running things. The contemporary hallucination that "thousands of good men" searching "thousands of manuscripts" have produced "reliable translations" since 1800, the equivalent of saying that "thousands of Germans" reading "thousands of books" produced the Third Reich (1933--1945). When all is said and done, you can trace the founding and development of the Third Reich to fifteen men, and ten of these followed someone else's orders. "The thousands of godly, dedicated devoted men" (to cite the propaganda of John R. Rice), who replaced the Reformation text with such drivel as the [ASV ]{.class_3759}(1901) and the [NASV]{.class_3759} (1971), only emulate forty teachers who were stupid enough to follow the apostate leadership of nine men: Origen, Augustine, Griesbach, Jerome, Westcott, Hort, Tischendorf, Schaff, and Nestle. When the blind (A. T. Robertson, J. G. Machen, Wuest, Thieme, and Walvoord) follow the blind, they both fall into a ditch no matter how "devoted" and "dedicated" they are. In this commentary, we assume that God is true and every man is a liar, an ["every man,"]{.class_3760} means anybody you met, spoke to, read after, conversed with, sat under, taught, saw, or heard about anywhere in the last six millennia. We assume that the Holy [Bible ]{.class_3759}is quite able to correct any sinner who tampers with its contents, and just because a sinner happens to sport a D.D. or an M.A. from a Fundamental or Conservative seminary is no concern of ours; nor is it of any consequence to the One Who wrote the Bible---the Lord God Almighty (Psa. 119:99--100). Where the "qualified authorities" disagree with the Holy Bible we shall correct them as quickly as we would correct our children, and we certainly will do it without any regard for their degrees or reputations.

[The Book of ]{.class_3759}Job has 42 chapters, 1,070 verses, and 10,102 words. There is one chapter for each month of the Great Tribulation, and Job is on the ground seven days and nights (Job 2:13), one for each year in Daniel's Seventieth Week. By sheer good fortune (!) the Book of Job falls into [144 logical divisions, ]{.class_3759}and these make up the six larger divisions: Chapters 1--2; 3--14; 15--21; 22--31; 32--37, and 38--42. By sheer good fortune (!) there turns out to be 144,000 "servants of God" sealed in the Tribulation (Rev. 7), and the number "six" is so prominent in connection with the Antichrist that no comment is necessary. However, the 144,000 virgin Jews from the twelve tribes of Israel---commonly mistaken for "spiritual Israel" or "Jehovah's Witnesses," etc.---show up twice in the Great Tribulation (Rev. 7, 14), and since Job is one of the greatest books in the Bible on Daniel's Seventieth Week, we should not be surprised to find these logical divisions in the discourse. (Ah so! We are off to a flying start, I see, and already the Scholars Union is hollering bloody murder about "chapter numbering" and Premillennialism, but fortunately we don't have to pay any attention to this uproar because\...!)

The word ["Job"]{.class_3760} means "one persecuted" (as Israel will be in the Tribulation), and Job is in Edom (see Lam. 4:21) where Israel will be in the Tribulation (Isa. 63:1)\
and\....

Ah so! Take a long look back and see if you can see anybody following us in the exhaust! Didn't we just lose thirty five cars on the back stretch? And I see that "Hebrew scholarship" is written on the side of thirty of them!

The one who persecutes Job (Job 1:6--9) will persecute Israel in the Tribulation (Rev. 12:13); so, naturally Job's "captivity" is restored at the end of the Book (Job 42:10), like Israel's will be returned at the end of the Tribulation. Since this will involve a resurrection (Ezek. 37:12--13; Acts 23:6--7), Job's dead children are resurrected at the end of the book (Job 42:13), in spite of four hundred commentators who say that they were not. (Isn't it beautiful out here on the mountain peaks, with no traffic and not a car in sight either way as far as you can see? It doesn't take much speed to lose the "pack," does it? I mean, there is nothing like a believing look at the English text to out pace any unbelieving look at the "original manuscripts," is there?)

[The Book of Job is ]{.class_3759}(plainly) one of the greatest books in the Bible on the sufferings of Israel in the Great Tribulation just ahead.

To show the dearth of material there is when dealing with the prophetic portions of the book, we should notice that even the premillennial scholar William Biederwolf (a great admirer of C. I. Scofield) could not find more than ONE reference in the whole book to the Second Advent. In Biederwolf's [Millennium Bible ]{.class_3759}(reprinted[ ]{.class_3760}as [The Second Coming Bible)]{.class_3759} we are told that Job 19:25 is the only reference concerned with the Second Advent. How Biederwolf missed the other fifty plus references is a mystery that can be explained only by his rejection of the[ ]{.class_3760}[Authorized ]{.class_3759}text as the final authority. And Barton Payne is just as shallow and superficial in his work[ ]{.class_3760}[Encyclopedia of Biblical Prophecy. ]{.class_3759}Dr. Payne found ten prophetic references in[ ]{.class_3760}[Job. ]{.class_3759}There are more than twenty in the last nine chapters.

In addition to descriptions of the Antichrist, the Devil, the movements of troops in the Tribulation, the landmarks for the Second Advent, the condition of Israel at this time, the phenomena accompanying the rapture, and the Battle of Armageddon, there will be found the expressed desires of men seeking death in the Tribulation, post Tribulation plagues, and demon worship during this period. The only way for the Conservative scholar to miss all these references is to deliberately "low rate" the[ ]{.class_3760}[AV ]{.class_3759}(1611)[ ]{.class_3759}and refuse to accept its statements at face value. Those of us who are accused of "seeing things not in the text" are prosecuted only by arrogant, egotistical stuffed shirts who have already prejudicially condemned the[ ]{.class_3760}[Authorized Text ]{.class_3759}and replaced it with their own blind nonsense. In their spiritual blindness they cannot see much beyond their own noses. There are actually more references to the Second Coming of Christ in [Job ]{.class_3759}than there are in the Gospels of [John ]{.class_3759}and [Mark ]{.class_3759}combined.

We now turn briefly to the critical data.

The most outstanding thing about the [Book of Job is ]{.class_3759}the furor it causes among Liberals, Fundamentalists, Gnostics, Agnostics, Conservatives, and Atheists in regards to the [date ]{.class_3759}of writing and the [authorship. You ]{.class_3759}see, the human author of the Book is given in the first person singular in Job 32:16, and he uses a historical narrative referring to his audience as "they" in the same context. For this reason, Job 32:16 has been [altered ]{.class_3759}in every translation on the market, and it has been [rejected ]{.class_3759}by every major commentator since 1800 as a "reliable" passage---even though it is in a "reliable" translation, you know! (To quote such misguided brethren as Sumner, Vernon McGee, Rice, and Robertson.) A book as old as the [Book of Job ]{.class_3759}cannot be allowed to have an author who is known; so, in keeping with the Liberal school of Bible rejecting apostates, the Fundamentalists and Conservatives of 1800--1980 have preserved the fable that "no one knows who wrote it." They do this by erasing (or changing) Elihu's speech in Job 32:16. Not even Fausset can stand the pressure of the Bible rejecting liberals in Germany and England; so, to alibi his removal of the authorship, he states that Elihu was not "competent" enough to have written it (Commentary, p. ix). The "leading authorities" have simply agreed that "Job" remain without a known author. This enables the "leading authorities" to date the Book at any time they desire, with conjectures running from Patriarchal times (2000--1600 B.C.) and Mosaic times (1600--1300 B.C.) to Solomon's reign (1000 B.C.) or Post exilic times (520--400 B.C.). If Elihu wrote the Book, all hope of moving the Book forward past the times of Jacob and Esau is gone. This will not do in scholastic circles, for the integrity of pagan literature would then be at stake, and pagan literature must maintain its religious standing at any cost. Watson [(Expositors Bible, ]{.class_3759}Vol. 11, p. 694) says that "no one before Moses would have been that elaborate and dealt with so many perplexities." Samuel Cox says it has to be later than Solomon because "man's ideas about God" evolve and follow the evolutionary system of Charles Darwin; no one before Solomon could have had "Job's concept of God." Another argument which Cox produces is the "conclusive evidence" that [Job ]{.class_3759}could not be written before the time of Solomon since Job does not appear as one of the heroes of faith in Hebrews 11, which runs from Adam to David. But for that matter, how did the writer of Hebrews miss [Judah ]{.class_3759}(Gen. 44:18--25), [Phineas ]{.class_3759}(Num. 25:6--10), [Caleb ]{.class_3759}(Josh. 15:13--20),[ Jonathan ]{.class_3759}(1 Sam. 14:1--5),[ ]{.class_3759}and [Shem]{.class_3759} (Gen. 9:26). And if being "destitute, afflicted, and tormented" (Heb 11:37) does not describe Job, who does it describe? Howard Hughes?

Watson admits that there is a "striking resemblance" between Job and [The Book of the Dead, ]{.class_3759}but this only complicates matters, for [The Book of the Dead ]{.class_3759}has no plot, no prophetic material provable in history, and no answers for the main problem that man has. If [Job ]{.class_3759}followed [The Book of the Dead, ]{.class_3759}it followed closely, which would again put the date of the writing long before the time of Moses; and if [The Book of the Dead ]{.class_3759}copied portions of [Job, ]{.class_3759}then [The Book of the Dead ]{.class_3759}was not the first "book" written. But why play "Puss in the Corner?" What man who has read [The Book of the Dead ]{.class_3759}(supposedly written around 3400 B.C.) does not know that it wasn't a book at all? It was a collection of pieces of scraps of paper which were placed in several dozen coffins.

Renan says that Elihu added his part to the book after the book was written. Many of the commentators say that it is "post exilic" because it came to "the Hebrew thinkers of the exile" (600--400 B.C.). According to Samuel Cox and Meredith Kline ([Wycliffe Commentary]{.class_3759}) the writer has to be writing at the time of Solomon ([Biblical Illustrator, ]{.class_3759}Baker Book House, p. ix), unless the writer is Solomon himself. "The grandeur and spontaneity of the book" has to come before the "eschatological contribution of the prophets." (You understand that dogmatic statements like this are made after nullifying fifty eschatological statements [in the book!) ]{.class_3759}If Cox and Kline knew how much "eschatological contribution" was in [The Book of Job, ]{.class_3759}they would faint.

[The New Bible Commentary ]{.class_3759}is quite typical of the approach made to the book by the majority of dead orthodox apostates: The writer, here (Heavenor), tells us that the date and historical background of [Job ]{.class_3759}are of less importance than nearly any poem in literature (citing Oesterly and Robinson), and he agrees with William Neil that Moffatt had a good translation because Moffatt worked from the assumption that "originally" Job made sense before things "happened" to the text. (This is quite representative of some of the baloney one may find published by some of the "reputable" Christian publishing companies in America.)

Collaborating to low rate the first real "book" ever written are Umbreit, Maurer, Jamieson, Fausset, Brown, Durnmelow, Davidson, Moffatt, Cheyne, Gleason Archer, Bernard Ramm, etc., and many other "evangelicals." C. I. Scofield (old [Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_3759}, p. 569),[ ]{.class_3759}however, maintains at least the early date of the book. Bullinger backs out and moves the date up to 1516 B.C. where Moses can take credit for the authorship. Adam Clarke puts the book this side of the law and cites numerous references (Job 31:9, 16, 26, 32) to prove that the Mosaic Law was an issue in Job's life and conduct. Others have given the credit to "an unknown poet," a "literary genius," or a "post exilic scribe," etc. Those familiar with the omnibus of literature on Biblical Criticism, Biblical Theology, and Biblical Introduction (Bengel, Semler, Fell, Bentley, Paulus, Baur, et al.) know what to make of this type of "scholarship": Yogurt.

If we are to go by the indefinite ("broad minded"), agnostic ("humble") theorizers (those "open to suggestion"), who are relativists ("tolerant scholars"), we would have 0 believe that an "unknown" wrote half of [Isaiah, ]{.class_3759}an "unknown" wrote [Revelation, ]{.class_3759}an "unknown" wrote [Matthew, ]{.class_3759}and an "unknown" wrote [Genesis; ]{.class_3759}and eventually, that the God of Truth is still the "UNKNOWN GOD" of Acts 17. There comes a time---at least in the light of Divine revelation---when a "charitable agnosticism" becomes an unholy ignorance. After all, if you don't want to believe Job 32:16, you have your liberties as any infidel living in a free country but don't try to convince those of us who believe the Bible that you have any more sense than the ignoramuses who taught you. Why make us come to you to assimilate your ignorance when we already have access to material that you don't have? Don't propagate your stupidity, child; a proselyte to ignorance is the last thing the Christian church needs today!

But the ignoramuses figured the body of Christ needed their vast stupidity back in the days of the "fathers" as much as it is needed now. The authors of the LXX (150---350 A.D.) found the book so difficult that almost a quarter of it is missing in their post Christian manuscripts. Duhm thinks that Chapters 1 and 2 (and 42) are "fragments of a popular book"; Davidson dated the book at 597 B.C., during the captivity; Ptolemy and Gesenius misplace Uz more than two hundred miles by putting it north of the Arabian desert; Rawlinson moves it three hundred miles east into Ur of the Chaldeas; and a bird's eye view of the other critics reveals that Job was an Arabian, an Egyptian, a Syrian, an Israelite, an Idumean, or a Hamite, who lived in the days of Abraham, Moses, Solomon, David, Ahasuerus, or Nebuchadnezzar. Ah well, variety is the spice of life.

Now, there is considerable proof from the text of [The Book of Job ]{.class_3759}that the writer was a [contemporary ]{.class_3759}of the three "comforters" who came to Job. Since this is apparent (see Job 32:1--16), Bullinger has wisely chosen to omit Elihu in his notes on the date and authorship of Job [(Companion]{.class_3759} [Bible, p. ]{.class_3759}666!). Dispensationalists should be thunderstruck to see that everyone's name in the book is listed by Bullinger except the [author. ]{.class_3759}Observe: Esau, Keturah, Issachar, Abraham (in tracing the ancestry of the characters). Eliphaz, Bildad, Zophar, Teman, Edom, Job, Naamah, Joseph, and Moses. Where did Elihu go?

Out to lunch? Now, again, this is quite typical of the approach of any destructive critic to the [AV ]{.class_3759}(1611) text. Surrounded by reams of notes on Greek and Hebrew roots, manuals and lexicons on Hebrew, Arabic, Coptic, Syriac, and Chaldean words, plus piles of translations and Targums, bales full of critical editions and Commentaries, bins stocked with manuscripts and "paraphrases," and secular and historical data that would choke Philip Schaff, the modern scholar [cannot even find the major characters in the book he is discussing. ]{.class_3759}Aside from Job, God, and Satan, the main character in the [Book of Job ]{.class_3759}is Elihu. His speech runs six straight chapters, and this discourse is [the longest discourse in the entire book; ]{.class_3759}not even Job speaks more than six chapters (Job 26--31). How did Bullinger fail to notice that [Elihu ]{.class_3759}was present with Bildad, Zophar, and Eliphaz?

Easy: He was just as anxious to get rid of the authorship of the book as the worst German Rationalist and English Deist who ever lived (1700--1900). The fact that Bullinger was a premillennial "Fundamentalist" meant nothing when it came to believing the [King James ]{.class_3759}text.

Elihu is the author by his own admission (Job 32:16) and by his own exclusion (Job 42:1--10). \[In the latter section you will find only the "three friends" mentioned (Job 42:7).\] The approximate time of the writing of the Book, then, is settled immediately, but this would leave nothing for the "qualified authorities to write and argue about! So you are sidetracked from Job 32:16 by every scholar and commentator since 1800. Scholastic repute must be maintained at all cost, and if the cost of believing Job 32:16 is the discarding of five hundred pounds of paper with conjectures written on it, the Bible will just have to go and the five hundred pounds of pulp will have to stay!

Against Elihu's authorship stand the following "arguments" presented by Fundamentalists, Atheists, Agnostics, Conservatives, Catholics, and Evangelicals:

1\. The language is "too advanced" for 1900--1600 B.C. (See [The Interpreter's Bible ]{.class_3759}of the NCCC, or any work by Briggs, the great nineteenth twentieth century Liberal).

2\. There are too many references to the Exodus and[ ]{.class_3760}Pharaoh and the Mosaic Law (Job 13:24; 4:9; 15:24; 26; 18:15; 12:17, 24; 7:25; 8:17, 18, 19; 8:8, 10; 20:17; 29:6; 15:27; 6:4, and 16:13). (Eighty percent of those listed were future premillennial prophecies which the scholars missed since 90 percent of them were amillennial or postmillennial in their perversions of the truth.)

3\. The content is not "primitive" enough (see the comparative columns of scripture set up by Adam Clarke \[pp. 7--11\] to prove that Solomon wrote the book or at least translated it). Only Lightfoot (1880)---a religious Liberal---gives the credit to Elihu, but Lightfoot immediately adds that Elihu lived along after the time of Moses and that he added the speeches of Job 32--42 to a book that he was writing as though he had been present, when actually he [wasn't. ]{.class_3759}We will encounter this "addition" business later when we come to the last chapters in the [Book of Job.]{.class_3759}

Of the three arguments given above we may say:

A. It is a religoius dogma of all [evolutionists]{.class_3759} that nothing can be as advanced as "modern man," and the fanatical extremes, to which the Graf-Wellhausen school carried this monkey business, are so well known as to be trite. Many of this school ascribed the first five books of the Bible to 1000--400 B.C. because "the language was too advanced" or the "concept of the priesthood was too developed" or because the "high ethical ground of monotheism had not yet been reached," etc. Robert Dick Wilson (A [Scientific Investigation of the Old Testament, ]{.class_3759}Moody, 1959) answered these kiddy games more than fifty years ago, and it is a waste of time to review the ground covered (see Hills, [The King James Version Defended, ]{.class_3759}1973, pp. 70--82). The truth of the matter is that neither you, nor your constituents know how well language had advanced in the district of Teman following the Tower of Babel where the "languages" had been confused. Furthermore, you don't know that Elihu's production was not translated into Hebrew by Moses (Exod. 2:10). Furthermore, you are a blank fool to keep judging the Bible by the secular evidence which some Bible rejecting jerk unearthed, when the rule always has been (and still is): [judge secular evidence by the BOOK. ]{.class_3759}The Bible is not subject to the depraved [guesses ]{.class_3759}of scientific [relativists ]{.class_3759}who don't even know where they are going when they die! Nonsense! The scientific relativists, international socialists, empirical physicists, positive thinkers, archaeological researchers, and logical positivists are subject to it (Heb. 4:12, 13). (Get your thinking straightened out before you begin to mess with a Book that will be here after Heaven and Earth have passed away.)

B. The references to the Exodus are imagined. You see, in their zeal to confine the [Book of Job ]{.class_3759}to the historical past and limit its applications to [spiritual ]{.class_3759}lessons regarding suffering, the majority of scholars ignored the fact that the book also pictures the Antichrist (Job 40:15), Daniel's Seventieth Week (Job 24), the Devil (Job 41), the Advent (Job 37:1--5), and the restoration of Israel (Job 42). Exodus 15 is a type of the rapture (see Exod. 15 and comments in that Commentary), and where "JOB" alludes to these matters, the Holy Spirit is pointing the way out loud and clear; that is, if you believe the Bible. The references to the Mosaic law (Job 20, 29, 31) are also imagmary and are based on the evolutionary hypothesis of Strauss, Astruc, Renan, DeWette, Graf, etc.---that no one [before Moses ]{.class_3759}could have had that much knowledge about ethical principles and sacrifices. If Adam Clarke, for example, had looked at Exodus 18:12 and Genesis 8:20 (or 12:8), he certainly would never have thought that Job's burnt offerings and sacrifices were "post Mosaic." By the same token, a heathen king (Gen. 20:5) doesn't have any trouble at all with Job 31:9, and Moses had not yet been born when Abimelech tells Abraham off (Gen. 20:8--11). Adam Clarke (Preface to his commentary, p. ii--xi) has imagined that pre Mosaic people were ignorant of these matters. Romans 1:19 and 2:14 should have corrected his thinking.

C. The old argument about the "style of writing" is as old as the claptrap the Scholars Union gave us on the [Book of Proverbs ]{.class_3759}(see its Preface). The "style of writing" matches the writings of Arabian poets who spent much time on philosophical questions dealing with judgment and fate. The style is a dramatic or epic poem, and it sets the pace for the works of Homer, Virgil, Ovid, Horace, and the Bhagavad Gita. If you cannot find a matchmeet to "Job" this early in the history of literature (1800--1700 B.C.), it is because God the Holy Spirit determined that the first real "book" written would be written by [Him ]{.class_3759}and that it would deal with the first, primary, basic, root trouble of mankind---the [relation of suffering to sin. ]{.class_3759}So, in their maddened rush to try to find counterparts or similitudes to the book (before 1500 B.C.), the scholars run into one dead end after another. God never intended that there would be any real competition for anything which He delivered to mankind, least of all the first thing that He [wrote.]{.class_3759}

Turning to the [objective evidence ]{.class_3759}on the positive side, we have four good reasons for believing that Elihu wrote the book long before 1700 B.C.

1\. A comparison of the chronologies found in Exodus (see that Commentary) would indicate that Abraham was born much earlier than the date (1996 B.C.) given in Bullinger's chronology. Larkin fixed it at 2111 B.C., which would be 115 years earlier than Bullinger. If these 115 years are added to the chronology on Esau and and his sons (Gen. 36), the ["Temanite" ]{.class_3760}of Job 2:11 could be "comforting" Job (Gen. 36:15) in 1780 B.C.; for Teman was Esau's grandson, and Jacob (Esau's brother) had grandsons before 1700 B.C. The date given by Ussher in the margin of Genesis 35--37 is 1800--1780 B.C., and this date can hardly be improved upon in view of the "430 years" in Egypt with their attendant problems (see Exodus 1:8--10 in that Commentary).

2\. All the characters in the Book of Job are patriarchal in their settings. Bildad the Shuhite (Job 2:11) is connected with Shuah (Gen. 25:2). ["Eliphaz the Temanite" ]{.class_3760}got his name from Genesis 36:10--11, and he is clearly allied with Edorn in Jeremiah 49:7 and Amos 1:11, 12. The brother of "Huz" (Gen. 22:21) is also found in this context (Jer. 25:23), and we should not fail to notice the constant references to Jeremiah. Jeremiah has 52 chapters (4 times 13), and the word ["Jeremiah" ]{.class_3760}means "to cast out"; that expression occurs thirteen times in the book. Jeremiah is clearly a prophetic foreview of Revelation 11:1--4. The Saviour who warned women about having children at this time (Matt. 24:19) told Jeremiah not to get married or have any children (Jer. 16:2). Zophar is not so easy to identify as Eliphaz and Bildad; however, the ["land of Uz" ]{.class_3760}settles things for Zophar, for the ["land of Uz"]{.class_3760} is spoken of in the same breath (by Jeremiah!) with Edom (Lam. 4:21; Jer. 25:20). In Genesis 36:28 you will find that Uz himself (after whom the land was named) was a ["Horite"]{.class_3760} who lived in the land of Edom (Gen. 36:20, 21). The setting, therefore, of the [Book of Job ]{.class_3759}is in Edom---[not Sinai]{.class_3759}---and the characters are the contemporaries of Esau's sons---not [Moses.]{.class_3759}

Completely overlooked by most of the major commentators are the constant references to a previous age in which Adam lived and in which there was a FLOOD (Job 8:8; 21:12; 22:15, 16; 20:7--15; 31:33; 15:19). Where these verses are mentioned, the commentator makes no comments on the chronological significance of the items. Hasn't it ever occurred to anyone as strange that the speakers of the [Book of Job ]{.class_3759}still have Adam and the [deluge ]{.class_3759}fresh in their minds when speaking of transgressors and transgressions?

If the writing was "post-Mosaic," why wasn't Pharoah named? Wouldn't he have been the "chief sinner" in any account dating from the Exodus? Furthermore, why do we keep hitting creation themes in the book (Job 25:5; 26:7--14, 38:4--10; 15:15, etc.)? And why do the ["sons of God" ]{.class_3760}(Job 1, 2, 38) of Genesis 6 take up space; they are not mentioned anywhere in Joshua, Judges, or 1 Samuel.

4\. Finally, to settle the matter of dating once and for all, why is there no reference to ["the law of Moses" ]{.class_3760}(as such) anywhere in the book? Did you read Joshua or Nehemiah? If you did, do you realize that the Hebrew scriptures contain the expression ["the law of Moses" ]{.class_3760}or the ["book of the Law" ]{.class_3760}[nine times ]{.class_3759}in the period following the conquest of Palestine (after the Exodus) and more than [fifteen times ]{.class_3759}in the post exilic period? The word "torah" (Hebrew for "law") occurs more than [one hundred times ]{.class_3759}between Exodus 12:49 and Malachi 4:4, yet the only time the word occurs in Job (Job 22:22) it is a reference to something which God could say [directly to Job, ]{.class_3759}exactly as God finally [said ]{.class_3759}it to Job (Job 41, 42). Again, if Job were the son of Issachar (Gen. 46:13), as Bullinger suggests, how is it that he cannot remember the name of his grandfather or great grandfather? Do you mean to tell me that the[ ]{.class_3760}sons of Jacob did not know about the Abrahamic blessing (Gen. 12:1--4) that would have prevented the calamities that came upon Job? Why doesn't Elihu mention this matter? Obviously because Elihu is descended from Buz (Gen. 22:21), the second son of Nahor, Abraham's brother---not [Abraham.]{.class_3759}

So, if we leave the text as it sands (Job 32:16), we have a book written on the spot by an eyewitness named [Elihu,]{.class_3759} and this book will give us a firsthand account of the heart and core of the drama of life. ["Job"]{.class_3760} deals with the roots of human nature; when you sit where Job sits (Job 2:8), in the condition that he is in, philosophy, politics, science, education, art, music, and literature dissolve into muddled abstractions that can neither alleviate nor solve the basic problems of suffering. It is appropriate that the first and oldest real "book" ever written should deal with this situation, for pain and suffering are the common lot in life. And even where the privation and the agony do not reach the peak that Job experiences, still disease, suffering, privation, bereavement, tears, misunderstandings, rebuke, worry, anguish, pain, and death are the common lot of man (Job 14:1--2). This world is still "a vale of tears," and science and education have done nothing---absolutely nothing---to convert it into anything else. The intellectual schmucks who talk about the "advancements in medicine" and "scientific technology" all forget to tell you that pain killers and hospital care and specialized treatment are luxuries that less than 2 percent of the world's population can afford. The [world's problems ]{.class_3759}are a great deal vaster than the problems of [upper ]{.class_3759}middle class America.

As proof of this, we have chosen for our "devotional meditations" on the [Book of Job ]{.class_3759}a different set of "commentaries" than one would normally choose. These "commentaries" have been chosen from the practical experience of "modern man" in the twentieth century, and they show more clearly than ever that neither science nor education has improved man's [general lot ]{.class_3759}more than by giving him a few pieces of junk to play with that he did not have in the eighteenth century.

After prayerfully reading the [Book of Job ]{.class_3759}through seventy seven times and giving careful attention to all of the critical remarks of Duhm, Peake, Bullinger, Dillmann, Delitzsch, Kline, Heavenor, Taylor, Dodd, Scofield, English, Umbreit, Maurer, Ewald, Schlotmann, Gesenius, Siegfried, Durnmelow, Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown (and anyone else the Bible faculty at BJU borrowed their material from), we have decided to let that monumental "Hebrew scholar" Richard Wurmbrand do some of our commenting. Along with Brother Wurmbrand, we thought that the German writers Theodore Pliever (Stalingrad) and Willie Heinrich [(The Crack of Doom) ]{.class_3759}might have a great deal more to contribute than the jaded guesswork of "Hebrew authorities" who wasted their lives translating the [NASV ]{.class_3759}(or the old one). And no commentary on [Job ]{.class_3759}would be really "scholarly" unless Haralan Popov and Mr. Stein [(Treblinka) ]{.class_3759}made some contributions. We have also decided to shelve Davidson, Umbreit, Fausset, Keil, Driver, Gesenius, and the faculty members of Wheaton, Moody, Fuller, and Dallas in favor of Eugene Kogon [(The Theory and Practise of Hell) ]{.class_3759}and the survivors of the Bataan "Death March." From time to time we may give space for the comments of Mrs. Salter (Escambia Rest Home), although she never finished high school, and we may grant some space to George Williams (Panama City, Fla.), who never went to college. Either of these people could handle the material in Job better than any Ph.D. teaching Old Testament at any Christian school in America. Martha and Bertha Siegel (Baltimore, Maryland), who dwell in the tenement district of downtown Baltimore, will also have something to contribute to "sound exegesis." For comic relief, we have selected the [New English Bible ]{.class_3759}and the [American Standard Version ]{.class_3759}of 1901 for comparison. Heavenor ([The New Bible Commentary]{.class_3759}),[ ]{.class_3759}Meredith Kline (Westminster Theological Seminary), and Dummelow will give us the typical view of the dead orthodox Conservatives, and [The Pulpit Commentary ]{.class_3759}will furnish us with the background for the destructive works done on the Book since 1800.

As we have already said, "Where these men (and any others) correct the [AV ]{.class_3759}text with their polite 'rathers' (see any Commentary) and 'betters' and 'more accurates,' we shall retaliate with some 'nevers' and 'balonies.'" The [AV ]{.class_3759}text of 1611 is quite able to correct the comments of every scholar since 1800; so, we shall simply use it as an authoritative standard by which to judge the mistakes of every Bible faculty of every Fundamental school since that time.

In reading the Commentary, the Bible student should give special attention to the following items:

1\. Constant attacks made by the scholars on passages which, if left as they stand, would give [scientific information ]{.class_3759}in advance of the time of the apostles (Job 26, 38).

2\. United attacks on texts which God in the past has honored in soulwinning messages or funeral messages (Job 19:26; 1:21, etc.).

3\. Universal unbelief and infidelity on the references of [Behemoth ]{.class_3759}and [Leviathan ]{.class_3759}in Job 40:15 and 4 1: 1.

4\. Universal rejection of Job's belief in the bodily resurrection (Job 42:13), which will occur at the end of Daniel's Seventieth Week (Dan 12:1--6), in the exact context of Job 42:1, when Israel's captivity is literally "turned" (Job 42:10).

5\. A fourfold meaning in many of the verses dealing with suffering as they first apply to Job himself, then to Christ in His atonement, then to the lost in Hell, and then to Israel in the Tribulation (Job 16, 18, 30).

6\. Complete ignorance on the part of every [major ]{.class_3759}scholar as to the prophetic implications of the Book. This ignorance is quite universal, and it causes the major commentators and translators to alter sources of passages simply because they themselves are blinded by wilful ignorance and fail to see the application.

7\. The uniformity of opinion by the main characters on two things: God [will bless ]{.class_3759}righteousness, and He will [judge ]{.class_3759}wickedness.

Readers who investigate the commentaries (any thirty at random) will discover that the scholars (any thirty at random) have tried to organize the drama so that the three comforters of Job take on standard "types" of critics: i.e., the traditionalist, the dogmatist, etc. When Ewald says that the three comforters are "poetic personifications of representative personalities" [(Biblical Illustrator, p. xiii), ]{.class_3759}he is talking through his stack (original Hebrew for "hollow head," from an unused Chaldean root for "tube talk"). When Fausset speaks of Eliphaz as a "theologian chilled by his creed," he sets up Eliphaz as a religious dogmatist, and this "type" has stuck to Eliphaz to this day. A careful reading of chapter 15 will show that Eliphaz is as much a "traditionalist" as Bildad or Zophar, and Bildad's speech of chapter 25 shows that he is anything but a "traditionalist." Everyone in the Book talks the same way on occasion, and if you are looking for some great difference in the approach of any of the "comforters," you are fooling yourself. Job often uses the very philosophy of his buddies, and we will note where this occurs. [Disaster is never an organized effort. ]{.class_3759}Much of what Job's comforters say is true whether it be "traditional" or not. When all is said and done, not even Elihu adds a great deal of truth to the preceding discussions. It is God Himself who breaks forth as the morning and sheds light on the darkness (Job 38, 2 Cor. 4:1--8), and, aside from Elihu's understanding of self righteousness, Elihu continues in the same refrain as his consorts.

You see, agonizing pain and Satanic attack under hideous circumstances are not subject to IBM "formats." You don't organize the thoughts of anyone in Job's condition, and the heated exchanges between him and his friends, after chapter 7, are not the products of calculated plans, coming from "phased programs"; they are the emotional development of a debate over matters so personal and so weighty that not one man in the group flys on a chartered course for long. You could just as well call the three ["physicians of no value" ]{.class_3760}(Job 13:4), Science, Education, and Religion, as try to make them stereotyped "personalities" to match the baloney served out on the Stoa by Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle. The verbal exchanges in Job are heated, violent, personal, and insulting; and many times they approach blasphemy, and this is exactly as it should be. [Disaster is never an organized affair.]{.class_3759}

The Bible will never gild the death's head of rotting "humus" or garland the fallen nature of Adam's sons. In Job's condition, there is no such thing as "organized thought" or "intelligent rebuttal." Job strikes back with everything at his disposal, and before chapter 32 is reached, his friends are grabbing at anything they can hit him with! If you are looking for a "Pilgrim's Progress" in Job, you are going to be disappointed. Pain and suffering have their own language, and misfortune and bereavement rarely bother to set up "formats" for their feelings. Disorganization and confusion are the characteristics of Hell, infantry combat, civil war, and the distraught mind of any man tortured within an inch of his life.

Don't try to stereotype anyone in the [Book of Job; ]{.class_3759}they are beyond your reach.

In Job 42:7, 8, there was no reference made to the lengthy speeches of Job 4--31. This is apparent by the fact that God says that Job had spoken the thing which was right," and yet [not two chapters before that ]{.class_3759}the Lord had accused him of speaking the thing which was "wrong" (Job 40:7--8)! What Job had said that was "right" is found in Job 42:3--6, and evidently the three "comforters" made no such confession; yet they had been in the same boat with Job and didn't know it! (We shall comment more on this when we arrive at Job 42.) Suffice it to say that Elihu omits any reference to himself in the last chapter as he has also spoken that which was "right" when he proclaimed the words of Job 33:12; 34:10; 34:19; 34:23; 36:3, and 37:24. The one place where Elihu differs in his analysis of God's dealings is that Elihu recognized that [self righteousness ]{.class_3759}is as great a sin as the other transgressions of "wicked men." After all, "self" is the root (2 Tim. 3:2), and although none of us would dare cast a stone at Job (John 8:7), or at any man like him in that position, we still must not forget that "all flesh is grass" and that God is God: He is the only One who is completely righteous. We would tremble to accuse a man like Job of being self righteous, but that was the problem (see Job 40:1--14), and a revelation of the Holiness of God (Job 42:1--6) was what it took to solve [that ]{.class_3759}problem.

Oh marvelous Book! Here in the midst of the agonies of the damned, where friends, family, property, health, and even motives for living have been destroyed, the Lord will not let up on "the pupil"! In the midst of a rotting disease, sleepless nights, worm-covered flesh, fever, nightmares, and the taunts of "fair weather friends," the Lord God still takes time out to point to the real problem we have---SELF! Let the self righteous take warning. Let the "godly, dedicated, separated Christian" watch well to his going! God is no respecter of persons: Stephen is filled with the Spirit, so he is murdered; Paul is filled with the Spirit, so he is lashed 195 times; Christ was the Godhead incarnate, so He was crucified. John Huss (1371--1415) was as "godly" as the last fifty Christians you just met, so they burned him at the stake.

In the dark dungeon (1948--1956) below the Romanian Police Station, Richard Wurmbrand danced before God and preached sermons aimed at no one but the Lord Himself. In the pitiful heartcries of his soul, at the point of distraction and insanity, we can see again the ancient drama unfolding: Why do the righteous suffer? The cries of Job on the ash heap state the basic fundamental problem of life itself: If there is a God, why doesn't He let me see Him, and why does He allow me (or my friends) to receive this kind of treatment if He is a good God? No Atheist or Agnostic can afford to bypass the [Book of Job. ]{.class_3759}Since the ash heap (Job 2:8) was usually found in the city dump (Matt. 5:22), Job's torments picture not only the sufferings of Christ and of Israel, they picture the abandoned soul of a man in Hell (Job 30:17--20) who trusted his own righteousness instead of God's (Rom. 10:1--5). In short, the Book of Job is a far-reaching book touching every major doctrine in either Testament where these doctrines deal with the problems of pain, self, suffering, death, and judgment. And down through the centuries, every man (saved or lost) is brought face to face with that terrible cry: ["Have pity upon me, have pity upon me, O ye my friends; for the hand of God hath touched me," ]{.class_3760}(Job 19:21).

Let us now "remove" to the ["land of Uz" ]{.class_3760}in ["the land of Edom" ]{.class_3760}and come to know our Lord better in that barren silence of suffering, where no airplanes, motorcycles, fences, walls, TV's, boats, cassettes, Holiday Inns, furniture, air conditioners, radios, banks, Pak a Saks, stores, bills, lawsuits, football stadiums, highways, cars, or shopping centers can prevent us from attending to the real problems of life.

Out here we shall not be distracted by a thousand things that come from America's "high standard of living." Job and his audience of six (Bildad, Eliphaz, Zophar, Elihu, God, and the Devil) will not be taking "coke breaks" or going to the teletype for the "latest news." Out here in the dry and rocky landscape of Selah (Petra) there will be no interruptions from washers and dryers breaking down or carburetors getting fouled up with sludge.

Off with the frills and the ornaments. Away with the trappings and decorations of the twentieth century. ["Stand thou still a while, that I may shew thee the word of God" ]{.class_3760}(1 Sam. 9:27).

Sooner or later you and I, or our families or our friends, will be going out to face the "ash heap"'in one form or another, and we couldn't prepare ourselves for the ordeal any better than by first paying a [visit to the "land of Uz."]{.class_3760}[ What we need is here. ]{.class_3759}Let us not deceive ourselves for a moment into thinking that we are going to cheerfully claim all the high ground that Paul claimed in Galatians and Ephesians and practice it (as he did in 2 Cor. 11) when our time comes. "Our time" may include the loss of wife and children, which Paul knew nothing about, or "our time" may include a crippling, loathsome disease which Paul had not experienced. You see, Job lost more materially than Paul ever had. Job experienced all of these things, and when God includes him with Noah and Daniel (Ezek. 14:14), He pays him one of the highest compliments that the Creator could give to a creature (see Luke 7:28). Therefore, no Christian in America could find a more profitable book in the Bible to study than Job, for American Christians (as a whole) are more materially minded than the lost masses of Africa and Asia.

Arise, my soul. Elihu is about to speak under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit (Job 32:8). Let us give him our undivided attention as he recites the Book of books, the poem of poems, the epic drama of a sinner's prestige, persecution, poverty, patience, and preservation.

[]{#IMh58iSMTFKKU0J9qyaeHw189.xhtml}

CHAPTER 1

1:1 There was a man in the land of Uz, whose name was Job; and that man was perfect and upright, and one that feared God, and eschewed evil.

2 And there were born unto him seven sons and three daughters.

3 His substance also was seven thousand sheep, and three thousand camels, and five hundred yoke of oxen, and five hundred she asses, and a very great household; so that this man was the greatest of all the men of the east.

4 And his sons went and feasted in their houses, every one his day; and sent and called for their three sisters to eat and to drink with them.

5 And it was so, when the days of their feasting were gone about, that Job sent and sanctified them, and rose up early in the morning, and offered burnt offerings according to the number of them all: for Job said, It may be that my sons have sinned, and cursed God in their hearts. Thus did Job continually.

The opening sentence speaks of a historical character who lived in a real place on this earth, at a particular time in history. ["In the land of Uz"]{.class_3760} (vs. 1) indicates a land named after a son of Aram (Gen. 10:23) who was the son of Shem. This land is plainly in Idumea or the land of Edom (Arabia, Petraea), and the approximate location may be marked by the city called "Petra," which lies south of the Dead Sea in the land of Edom. It is to this city that the Jews will flee in the Tribulation, and its peculiar meaning ("Rock") is prophetically connected with the references to the Lord God as the ROCK of Israel (see Psa. 18:2; 31:3; 42:9; 71:3, etc. in the Old Testament). ["Uz"]{.class_3760} will also be found to be a son of [Dishon]{.class_3759}, of the family of [Seir ]{.class_3759}(Gen. 36:28), and also (according to Bullinger) the ["Huz"]{.class_3760} of Genesis 22:21. However, the land probably received its name before the Huz of Genesis 22:21 was born. The [Pulpit Commentary ]{.class_3759}(Vol. 7, p. 1) and Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown hold that Uz was northeast of Palestine and nearer to Damascus and Haran. This conjecture is made in the face of the Biblical statement that Uz is in Edom (Lam. 4:21) and that it is in a list of nations which surround Palestine in a circle (Jer. 25:20), between Pharaoh (Jer. 25:19) and the Philistines.

The location is undoubtedly Edom, and this is where a Jewish remnant will be gathering manna at night (Lam. 5:9, Micah 7:14) when Jesus Christ returns up the ["king's high way"]{.class_3760} (Num. 21:22).

The ridiculous [Septuagint ]{.class_3759}of 330--500 A.D. (not B.C.--- there is no such thing as a B.C. [Septuagint]{.class_3759}) says that Job was in the land of "Ausis" (Greek: [Ausitidi]{.class_3759}), and even though the [ASV ]{.class_3759}and [NASV ]{.class_3759}committees believed in a "B.C. Septuagint" (while using the A.D. Catholic manuscripts to correct the [AV]{.class_3759}), they did not dare use this obvious corruption of Job 1:1.

"[Whose name was Job"]{.class_3760} (vs. 1). Job was a real man according to Ezekiel 14:20 and James 5:11. Whether or not he was the "fifth from Abraham" (the "Jobab" of 1 Chron. 7:1---which was spelled ["Jashub"]{.class_3762}) or whether he was the second king of Edom (spelled ["Jobab"]{.class_3762} in Gen. 36:33) is immaterial. He was a real man and he lived in a real land, and (as we have remarked in our Preface) there is little doubt about the general time of his earthly sojourn; he is living somewhere between 1800--1600 B.C. If Job was the third son of Issachar (Bullinger), he would have been only twenty years old in 1706 B.C., and he would have been 210 years old at his death around 1516 B.C. We prefer the earlier date for two reasons:

1\. Bullinger (as Scofield, English, R. A. Torrey, John Rice, and the Lockman Foundation) often yielded to the scholastic pressure of higher criticism and the phony baloney ("findings") of "science," when he corrected the [King James Bible.]{.class_3759}

2\. There is no reason at all for making the date of Abraham's birth 1996 B.C., unless one is trying to split up the 430-year "sojourning" of Exodus 12:40 into two periods of 215 years each to meet the demands of the mythological Septuagint. Clarence Larkin has a much more probable dating for Abraham's birth, but this date is nearer to 2100 B.C. To think that the Job of the [Book of Job]{.class_3759} was Issachar's son (see Gen. 46:13) is really stretching things; Issachar brought his sons into Egypt and none of them got out, as far as we know, until Moses led the exodus in 1491 B.C. What would one of Jacob's boys be doing over in Edom after the instructions given in Genesis 27:37 and 36:8? And how did he get to be ["the greatest of all the men of the east"]{.class_3760} (vs. 3) in a country where "the dukes of Edom" were running things, [in a land that God gave to Esau]{.class_3759} (Num. 20:18)?

Adam Clarke is the first man into the ring to cover up the significance of the Book; he tells us that the word ["Job"]{.class_3760} means "sorrowful," or "one who weeps" (Commentary, p. 23). This is a deliberate attempt to hide doctrinal truth. The Hebrew radicals (Aleph, Yod, Vau, and Beth) mean "one persecuted." Job (as Israel) was persecuted by the Devil (see Job 1:6--21), and, therefore, he stands in the scripture as an exact prototype of Israel's troubles, as they are found in Revelation 12.

"[And that man was perfect"]{.class_3760} (vs. 1). Notice how this word is hardly ever used in scripture with reference to sinlessness. In 2 Kings 20:3 and 1 Chronicles 28:9 the "perfect heart" is said to be "deceitful above all things" (Jer. 17:9). In 2 Chronicles 16:9 and Isaiah 38:3 the same type of thing occurs. In Luke 1:3, Luke does not mean to say that he knew every detail about everything; in Matthew 5:48 the reference is to being impartial; in 2 Chronicles 8:16 He is talking about a material building; in Luke 13:32 a sinless man is already talking (!); in Hebrews 13:21 no one is sinless in everything they do, for ["there is not a just man]{.class_3762} [upon the earth, that\...sinneth not"]{.class_3762} (Ecc. 7:20); and in Philippians 3:12, Paul says that he is not ["already perfect,"]{.class_3762} although some Christians, including himself, are perfect (Phil. 3:15)! "Perfect," in the Bible, means "to fit, adjust, complete, finish, or to make accurate, or to make exact." "Sinlessness" is not found in one case that we have listed above. To prove this, notice what Job says about "perfectness" (Job 9:20--21) when he connects the word with "sinless perfection." Job was not sinless, for he was self-righteous (see Job 40:8), and his "uprightness" (Job 1:1) couldn't save him from self-righteousness; it hardly ever does in anyone!

However, the "man of integrity" (Job 2:3 and comments) is marked by four characteriestics; he [fears God ]{.class_3759}(Prov. 1:7 and comments), he [eschews evil ]{.class_3759}(Job 1:1), he [walks uprightly ]{.class_3759}(Job 31:1--15 and comments), and he is "perfect," in the sense of outward obedience to the rules, regulations, laws, and social mores of his day and time. "Eschewed" (vs. 1) is the old English for "to turn aside from," or---as one holiness preacher put it: "They is some things that is better eschewed than [chewed]{.class_3759}, and terbaccer is one of 'em!" The old English is from [Coverdale ]{.class_3759}(1535), and when one must choose between [Coverdale ]{.class_3759}and E. S. English ([New Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_3759}), [Coverdale ]{.class_3759}is the best bet.

Verse 2 (see notes on Job 42:13) and verse 3 are self- explanatory; Job is a ["man\...of the east"]{.class_3760} (vs. 3), so he is a Shemite (see Gen. 10:21 and comments).

Unlike the patriarchs who came from Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, Job lives in a [city]{.class_3759}; and he is one of the elders who sits in judgment in the "circuit court" (Job 31:21). Abraham's progeny were "sojourners, pilgrims, and strangers," for it is not until Joshua's time that they begin to live in cities and "sit in the gate." (This is another very sound reason for rejecting the "Job" of Gen. 43 as the main character of our book.) ["This man was the greatest of all the men of the east"]{.class_3760} (vs. 3). We are not dealing with some "rookie of the year" in studying Job. When the Bible says that a man or woman is ["great"]{.class_3760} (2 Kings 4:8) they are GREAT, and when a character like Simon the Sorcerer shows up (Acts 8:9 and comments), he is never a third- string cornerback. When God gave Solomon more wisdom than ["all the children of the east country"]{.class_3760} (1 Kings 4:30), He undoubtedly took Solomon to the [Book of Job ]{.class_3759}for his first lesson in ["vanity of vanities\...all is vanity."]{.class_3760}

"[And his sons went and feasted\...everyone his day\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 4). The standard interpretation is that this is a reference to seven "birthday parties" held once a year; Job's daughters are invited to "eat and drink with their brothers" (vs. 4). Job's family is a close knit family, and the invitation of the sisters to the "party" shows that these feasts were no immoral affairs, but times of genuine joy and thanksgiving. However, Job takes nothing for granted; knowing what is in man (see John 2:25), he sanctifies them at the end of their celebrations and then rises up early to offer ["burnt offerings according to the number of them all"]{.class_3762} (vs. 5). The Lord accepts these offerings which is apparent by His instructions to Job's three friends at the close of the book (Job 42:8). If the birthdays were seven separate days, then seven feasts a year were held, and this is more probable than Jamieson's conjecture that there was an annual affair with a seven day celebration with each son "doing his thing" on his day during the week.

In Genesis 22:3, Abraham rises up early in the morning to do sacrifice; sacrifices, of course, belong to times long before Abraham (see Gen. 8).

1:6 Now there was a day when the sons of God came to present themselves before the Lord, and Satan came also among them.

7 And the Lord said unto Satan, Whence comest thou? Then Satan answered the Lord, and said, From going to and fro in the earth, and from walking up and down in it.

8 And the Lord said unto Satan, Hast thou considered my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth, a perfect and an upright man, one that feareth God, and escheweth evil?

9 Then Satan answered the Lord, and said, Doth Job fear God for nought?

10 Hast not thou made an hedge about him, and about his house, and about all that he hath on every side? thou hast blessed the work of his hands, and his substance is increased in the land.

11 But put forth thine hand now, and touch all that he hath, and he will curse thee to thy face.

12 And the Lord said unto Satan, Behold, all that he hath is in thy power; only upon himself put not forth thine hand. So Satan went forth from the presence of the Lord."

At this time there enters upon the stage of Job's life the great ["accuser of our brethren"]{.class_3760} (Rev. 12:10). He is mentioned more than fifty times in the scripture, and, aside from the person and work of the Lord Jesus Christ, he is the chief character in the word of God. There are eighteen types of Antichrist in the scripture (see [Mark of the Beast]{.class_3759}, 1970), and there is more information in the Bible on the personality and work of the ["man of sin"]{.class_3760} (2 Thess. 2:1--3) than there is on Peter, James, and John. Satan, in the Scripture, is second only to the Godhead in power and wisdom (Zech. 3:1--2), and not even an archangel can revile him (Jude 9). He is quite able to deceive ["the whole world"]{.class_3760} (Rev. 12:9) without exerting much of an effort; so, he manages to defeat every man who gets into the ring with him (but ONE) in nearly six thousand years of recorded history.

The Bible presents Satan as a PRINCE (like Christ), a GOD (as Christ), an ANGEL (as Christ: see Gal. 4:14), a MESSIAH (["anointed,"]{.class_3760} Ezek. 28:14), and a KING (Job 41:34). Like the Lord Jesus Christ, he rides a white horse (Rev. 6:1--2), he quotes Scriptures (Luke 4:10), he has a bride (Rev. 17) who is a city; and, like Christ, he has ministers, apostles, and churches (Acts 19:37). His main sphere of activity is RELIGIOUS, and although he often uses science and education to debunk "religion," he only does it temporarily. The far-sighted ["god of this world"]{.class_3760} (2 Cor. 4:4) has future plans to descend from outer space (Rev. 12:3--12) and enthrone himself (2 Thess. 2:1--10) as the ruler and saviour of mankind. He WAS a cherub (Ezek. 28:14), he IS a dragon (Isa. 27:1--2), he appears as ["an angel of light"]{.class_3760} (2 Cor. 11:1--14), and since all angels are male, he will appear to the world as a man (Rev. 13:18, Isa. 14:16) from outer space, who will profess to be able to solve every problem that science, education, and religion have failed to solve in the last 5,800 years. "

"[And Satan came also among them"]{.class_3760} (vs. 6). The ["sons of God"]{.class_3762} (vs. 6) who accompany Satan during this interview are pre-Adamic beings (see Job 38:7 and comments). They are the ["gods"]{.class_3762} of Psalm 82 and Genesis 3:5, and we have spoken of them at length in our comments under Genesis 6 in that Commentary.

The scene, then, is a matchmate to 1 Kings chapter 22 where the lying spirit set up Ahab for the "hit."
