The ominous addition ["and Satan came also among them"]{.class_3762} (vs. 6) appears in the Bible text as a simple narrative of a past event, but in view of the history of man---6,000 years of suffering, dying, devil-worship, perversion, ignorance, lying, stealing, starving, killing, murder, and damnation---the addition is terrifying. If Satan ever gets his eyes on you, as he got them on Simon Peter (Luke 22:31) and David (1 Chron. 21:1), you will need all the grace that God can give you, all the prayers that God's people can pray for you, all the Bible promises you can read, and all the comfort the Holy Spirit can minister, if you are to survive in any kind of condition. ["And Satan came also among them"]{.class_3762} is a forecast of "coming events" that would turn your blood to ice if you knew all the possibilities that could attend such a pronouncement.

"[Whence comest thou?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 7). (Observe the peculiar matchmate to this question in Matt. 26:50.) The question implies that Satan could have been occupied elsewhere than on the earth. (See the discussion under ["Jupiter"]{.class_3762} in the comments on the Acts of the Apostles 14:12 and the comments on ["before our God"]{.class_3762} in Rev. 12:10.) ["To and fro in the earth"]{.class_3762} (vs. 7) also matches the "roaring lion" of 1 Peter 5:8 who ["walketh about."]{.class_3762} You will notice that the expression ["in the earth"]{.class_3762} (vs. 7) lines up with ["in the earth"]{.class_3762} as it is found in verse 8, referring to Job. The term is not used in the sense of inside the globe, although it is possible for Satan to travel there as well.

"[Hast thou considered my servant Job\...?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 8). You bet your life he had ["considered"]{.class_3762} him. When a man like Joshua (Exod. 33:11), Moses (Josh. 1:1), David (Psa. 19:11, 13), James (James 1:1), Jude (Jude 1), John (Rev. 1:9), or Paul (Titus 1:1) serves God, you can be certain that Satan "considers" him. If God honors any man who serves Him (John 12:26), don't you know that a god who desires worship (2 Thess. 2:2--8) will be interested in the competition?

"[Doth Job fear God for nought?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 9). That is, "Do you think that Job's motive for fearing you is just because he is pious?" The accusation quickly follows: ["Hast not thou made an hedge\...blessed the work of his hands\...?"]{.class_3762} Satan's proposition is clear: Job is good because he is rich and secure. Take away his security and riches, and he will "go to pot." ["Touch all that he hath and he will curse thee to thy face,"]{.class_3762} (vs. 11).

This perfectly demonstrates the prosecuting job carried on by the District Attorney of the Universe. Christ is our Mediator and Advocate; that is, He is the Counsel for the Defense---the criminal lawyer. Satan is represented as a prosecuting attorney with full power (Heb. 2:14) to carry out the death penalty or anything connected with it (Job 2:6). From verse 9 to verse 11 we may gather that Satan's theory is this: ghettos produce criminals; the ecology problem is behind criminal activity; the environment is to blame; social conditions are to blame; income is to blame; poverty is to blame; and the "establishment" is to blame. Satan's analysis of Job's position is blunt and to the point, and before you overlook it as Old Testament "redemptive history," you'd better look in a mirror. If God stripped you down tonight like He did Job, would you still be morally and spiritually reliable?

The scene is a real scene. It goes on at regular intervals today. There is nothing "apocalyptic" or imaginary about the description; it is as real and as "contemporary" as sin and death. The fact that no modern scientist has ever seen it or has ever lived to tell about it is as immaterial as immaterial can get. The Bible, here, has pulled the veil aside and given us a look into the supernatural realm which we would know nothing about apart from that Divine Revelation. If you reject it, you reject it at the peril of your life. For as surely as Buddhism, Catholicism, and Communism reject absolute truth and aim at an integrated synthesis---with no known goal---all present systems of religion and philosophy reject the New Testament revelations of salvation, and that salvation was from ["the god of this world"]{.class_3760} (2 Cor. 4:4) as well as from the Lake of Fire. Any theology or body of scientific data (or any body of philosophical speculation), which denies the existence of a real, actual, living, and personal Satanic being is forced by virtue of its unreality to reject:

1\. The Biblical doctrine of the Atonement (see Col. 2:8--16).

2\. The correct spiritual warfare for the believer (Eph. 6:10--15).

3\. The Genesis account of the origin of modern problems.

4\. The Gospel accounts of the preparation of the Messiah for His ministry (see Luke 4:1--9).

5\. The historical truthfulness of the book of Revelation where it deals with coming events on this earth (see comments under Rev. 11--13, 19--20).

That is, where Satan is treated as a "principle of evil," the "mythological adversary," or the "helpful witch," there can be no fidelity to Scripture and no grasp of reality.

"[Put forth thine hand now\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 11). Observe how closely the hand of God and the hand of Satan operate. The Lord immediately says, ["\...only upon himself put not forth thine hand"]{.class_3762} (vs. 12), yet, in the next chapter, He says, ["thou movedst me against him, to destroy him without cause"]{.class_3762} (Job 2:3). Notice again the peculiar interchange that goes on in 2 Samuel 24:1 and 1 Chronicles 21:1. Once more we will find it in the wording of Exodus 12:12--13, and 23 (see remarks in that Commentary). The Lord and Satan work so close together that apart from [divine revelation ]{.class_3761}and the [illumination of the Holy Spirit ]{.class_3761}you could not possibly tell one from the other when disaster, disease, disability, death, debt, divorce, desertion, damnation, or drug abuse shows up. The first and foremost practical problem that every dedicated child of God has is figuring out: Is this obstacle [the Lord ]{.class_3761}warning me to stop or is it [the Devil ]{.class_3761}trying to get me to quit? When a man gets "sacked" like Job did, most people figure it just like his wife did; she figures he was cursed of God and predestined for Hell.

"[Behold, all that he hath is in thy power"]{.class_3760} (vs. 12). The first act, then, of the permissive will of God has been to deliver anything that belonged to Job over to Satan ["for the destruction of the flesh"]{.class_3762} (1 Cor 5:5). We should notice that Satan can do nothing without God's permission, that once permission is obtained violent action follows, and that although God delivered Job's children and property to Satan, he spared Job's health. In the next chapter, God destroys Job's health but spares his ["life"]{.class_3762} (Job 2:6). That is, Satan can only go as far as God allows him to go (Rom. 8:28). To the regenerated child of God this is a great comfort, for it means that no matter how bad it gets (Rom. 8:25--35, for example,) God will not give the Devil permission to carry off the redeemed soul that has been "washed in the blood" (Rev. 1:5)! Alleluia!!

"[So Satan went forth from the presence of the LORD"]{.class_3760} (vs. 12). (Compare the wording with Gen. 4:16, where the context is Cain.)

Before continuing with the drama of Job, let us go out into the lobby for an intermission and see what the "highly qualified authorities" are saying about the opening scenes of the book.

Adam Clarke and Meredith Kline ([Wycliffe Commentary]{.class_3759}) get through with little or no trouble and do not make any major corrections on the text, although Heavenor is careful to tell us that the Septuagint manuscripts (all written more than two hundred years after the New Testament) have deleted 20 percent of the Hebrew text (ibid., p. 421). Peake changes ["substance"]{.class_3760} (vs. 3) to "cattle"; Budde changes ["hearts"]{.class_3760} (vs. 5) to "thoughts," and the word ["cursed"]{.class_3760} (vs. 5) has been changed to "bidden farewell" in the writings of Ewald, Delitzsch, Dillman, Davidson, and Schlottmann. (The word for "cursed," in verse 5, can be translated "blessed" in some instances, and this points out the peculiar authority of the [AV ]{.class_3759}text; to this day, many Americans say, "he blesses him out," meaning "he cursed him.")

Oesterly and Robinson tell us that the date and historical background of Job are of little or no importance to anyone, and The "Living" Bible (Tyndale House Publishers) has erased the Shemitic reference in verse 3 and replaced it with "in that entire area." Strangely (or not so strangely if you study apostasy), Taylor (The Dead Bible) has refused to translate ["the sons of God"]{.class_3760} (vs. 6) as "angels" here. (Review your notes on Conservative scholars being Satanically led when approaching these passages: Acts 14, 16, and 19 in that Commentary.) Before deciding to alter ["cursed"]{.class_3760} (vs. 5) to "turned away from God," Taylor decided to ignore the Masoretic text published in English by the Jewish Publication Society of America, for that publication said "blasphemed." (The [AV ]{.class_3759}text usually runs a good deal closer to the Hebrew Masoretic Old Testament than such feather dusters as [The Living Bible]{.class_3759}.) The [New Scofield Reference Bible ]{.class_3759}has made four substitutions in the text, while still kidding its buyers into thinking that it is the "Authorized Version." You will find the [Authorized Text ]{.class_3759}stuck into the margin of the [New Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_3759}, after reading on the frontispiece that the book is "The Authorized King James Version." The [New Scofield Reference Bible ]{.class_3759}is no more the "Authorized King James Version" than The New English Bible is Puss-in-Boots.

1:13 And there was a day when his sons and his daughters were eating and drinking wine in their eldest brother's house:

14 And there came a messenger unto Job, and said, The oxen were plowing, and the asses feeding beside them:

15 And the Sabeans fell upon them, and took them away; yea, they have slain the servants with the edge of the sword; and I only am escaped alone to tell thee.

16 While he was yet speaking, there came also another, and said, The fire of God is fallen from heaven, and hath burned up the sheep, and the servants, and consumed them; and I only am escaped alone to tell thee.

17 While he was yet speaking, there came also another, and said, The Chaldeans made out three bands, and fell upon the camels, and have carried them away, yea, and slain the servants with the edge of the sword; and I only am escaped alone to tell thee.

18 While he was yet speaking, there came also another, and said, Thy sons and thy daughters were eating and drinking wine in their eldest brother's house:

19 And, behold, there came a great wind from the wilderness, and smote the four corners of the house, and it fell upon the young men, and they are dead; and I only am escaped alone to tell thee.

20 Then Job arose, and rent his mantle, and shaved his head, and fell down upon the ground, and worshipped,

21 And said, Naked came I out of my mother's womb, and naked shall I return thither: the Lord gave, and the Lord hath taken away; blessed be the name of the Lord.

22 In all this Job sinned not nor charged God foolishly.

The years of Job's life that these calamities fall on him are not given; however, Job lives 140 years after they take place, and since Job has had time to father ten children and obtain ["a very great household"]{.class_3760} (vs. 3), it is no strain on our credulity to believe that he was seventy years old at the time of the terrible testings. One might reduce this age to fifty, but when one considers that Isaac did not marry till he was seventy-five and Jacob did not marry until he was past seventy-seven, it is dangerous to make Job a parent of ten children before he is at least seventy. Job's friends tell him that they know people who are ["much elder than thy father"]{.class_3760} (Job 15:10), and since Job lived 140 years after his agony, the elders of that verse could well have been 150--200 years old.

Four messengers (vv. 14, 16--18) bring the terrible tidings to Job's ears: Sabeans, Chaldeans, lightning, and hurricane accomplish Job's bereavement. He is wiped out completely, his assets are "liquidated," his stocks are "depleted," he declares bankruptcy, and, if that weren't enough, he (or someone) has to bury ten corpses!

There are not many things that can happen to a man in this life any more hideous than the things which happened to Job, and it would be a hardhearted reader indeed who would follow the account and then not marvel at Job's response in verse 21. Who of us could "worship in spirit and in truth" after such a blow from the hand of God? Have you ever buried any of your children? Ten in one day? And what man among us would not rather die himself than see his sons die? Did any Jew in the death camps at Treblinka, Buchenwald, and Auschwitz lose ten in one day? And if you lost ten, wouldn't it be more desirable to leave with them than to remain to bury them?

The commentators are far too glib or too bland (and much too brief) in their commenting on Job 1:21--31. Generalizations about "temporal goods and domestic comforts" or "staggering blows of affliction," etc., do not cut to the marrow. The marrow is this:

1\. In the Johnstown flood (1889), one woman committed all of her children to the flood waters on pieces of floating boards and trees, kissing each one good-bye and praying over them. The last child was less than four years old. When the flood was over, all the children's bodies were found. The woman returned home to her own people in Virginia; she returned as a [childless widow]{.class_3759}. As she departed Johnstown she was heard to remark blankly: "I've got to get away from here where I can think things over."

2\. In Canton, Ohio, in the 1960's, a woman and her husband returned home to find that [three ]{.class_3759}of their children had burned to death in a fire. The woman spent several weeks in a mental ward before she was able to carry on a near normal life.

When we read of Job losing ten children, the account means what it says: he lost ten of them. No legend is being "passed on by oral tradition," and no "Oriental tale" is being told by Ali Baba. Job mourned the loss of seven boys and three girls in a period of less than twenty-four hours.

So, before anyone passes by the old saint of the Arabian wastelands and classifies him beneath Napoleon, Michael King Jr., Eisenhower, Einstein, General Patton, Woodrow Wilson, Billy Graham, and Pope Paul, they had better ponder well his circumstances.

"[The LORD gave and the LORD hath taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD"]{.class_3760} (vs. 21). Observe that Satan did the taking (Heb. 2:14), but since it was only by the permissive will of God, Job does not sin nor ["charge God foolishly"]{.class_3762} in saying verse 21.

Verse 13 is self-explanatory.

"[And the Sabeans fell upon them"]{.class_3760} (vs. 15). The Sabeans are descended from Sheba (Gen. 25:3), the grandson of Abraham and Keturah. Observe again how this locates the time of Job at the time of Esau and Jacob or shortly thereafter. The Sabeans were desert marauders. Satan allows one man to escape to bring the news, and ["while he was yet speaking"]{.class_3762} (vs. 16), a lone remaining shepherd reports the next disaster. "When it rains it pours" is the proverbial expression; Job's troubles come in double sets before he has time to get his breath. ["While he was yet speaking"]{.class_3762} (vs. 17), more bad news comes in. ["The Chaldeans made out three bands"]{.class_3762} (vs. 17). \[This is what led Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown to assume that "Uz" was in the Euphrates area in the extreme northeast corner of Syria. They figured the Chaldeans from Babylonia (Chaldea) would have no business clear over in Edom. However, the Chaldeans had been fighting all around Petra (in Edom) one hundred years before Job ever showed up. Notice, in Genesis 14, that the king of Shinar (Gen. 14:1) has troops around Sodom and Gomorrah during the time of Abraham, and Sodom and Gomorrah are not fifty miles from Petra, ["in the land of Uz."]{.class_3762} The Bible is always the best commentary on the "recognized commentators." (Cf. Hab. 1:6--8 and comments in that Commentary.)\]

"[While he was yet speaking"]{.class_3760} (vs. 18), another messenger comes in, and, in every case, Satan has contrived things so that only one man gets out alive; this emphasizes the disaster and leaves Job without any hope of recovering anything.

"[A great wind from the wilderness"]{.class_3760} (vs. 19) would indicate a cyclone or tornado of some kind, since the ["four corners of the house"]{.class_3762} collapsed at the same time. In verse 19, [the reader should notice the peculiar omission of any mention of Job's daughters]{.class_3761}. This is exceptionally interesting since the daughters are the only ones whose names are given at the end of the Book (Job 42:14). Kline, Clarke, Bullinger, Jamieson, Williams, Fausset, Brown, and the ["New" Bible Commentary]{.class_3761} (revised) fail to notice the strange omission; they make no comment. Jamieson (in his own single volume commentary) says ["young men"]{.class_3762} should have been "young people," as in Ruth 2:21. However, the same word (Hebrew: ["naarim"]{.class_3761}) used in Ruth 2:21 is found right in the context of Job 1:19. (To tell the whole truth, it is the word for "servants"---not young people---found in verses 15--17.) The ["young men"]{.class_3762} of verses 15--17 are "servants," for they worked for Job and took care of his flocks (vs. 3). The young men of verse 19 are his [sons]{.class_3761} and, therefore, should be marked off from the young men of verses 15, 16, and 17. The [AV ]{.class_3761}translators have done this, and no translator can honestly "raise a stink" about "inconsistencies," for they themselves have translated the same word as "young people" one time and "young men" another time and even "servants" (Masoretic Bible) on another occasion. It is taken for granted by the speaker (vs. 18) that the daughters are dead because look now at what follows: ["I only am escaped alone to tell thee"]{.class_3762} (vs. 19). Further study should be made about the matter in connection with males only appearing at the three feasts of Israel, which match the three parts of the first resurrection (see 1 Cor. 15:23).

"[Then Job arose and rent his mantle, and shaved his head"]{.class_3760} (vs. 20). (See Esth. 4:1 and Mic. 1:16.) Both acts are symbolic of deep sorrow. The first symoblized death by the [tearing of the flesh ]{.class_3761}(Heb. 10:20); the second symbolized the [putting off of everything superfluous ]{.class_3761}(Exod. 33:5) to match the loss of everything that might cover a man's nakedness (see Acts 22:23 and comments). ["And fell down upon the ground and worshipped"]{.class_3762} (vs. 20). Instead of driving Job "to drink" or to suicide, Satan has driven Job into a prayer meeting.

"[Naked came I out of my mother's womb, and naked shall I return thither: the LORD gave, and the LORD hath taken away; blessed be the name of the LORD"]{.class_3760} (vs. 21). Job's response to the poured-out wrath of God on his family and his possessions will stand for true sainthood as long as men breathe upon this earth. If you can "bless" God (Psa. 34:1; 103:11, 20--21, etc.) in Job's condition---instead of cursing Him (vs. 11)---you won't have much trouble keeping the first commandment.

If [The Book of Job ]{.class_3759}ended here, no one would have ever found the bitter root which lay in Job's bosom---that self righteous "ego" that resides in all of us. You see, the dying thief (Luke 23) "had it made." He never lived to see Christians fight in business meetings, steal money "for the glory of God," lie about attendance records, slander people they couldn't compete with, deny the word of God, and backslide. The dying thief (Luke 23) went from his conversion to his eternal home without having a chance to "get out of fellowship" with God. Job has to go on living after a heroic response to disaster.

The problem is TIME.

Instead of going home to glory after verse 21 (or after 2:10), Job goes on for at least another eight days and then has to deal with three "buddies" who would have cursed God if He had taken their welfare checks. Satan knows human nature; give Job time and he will eventually blow the works (foul up the machinery, jam the air waves, short the circuit, blow the fuse, cook his goose, etc.). The law of entropy works in people exactly like it works anywhere else. John Wesley may have been right in speaking about living a sinless life, but only on the condition that at any split second (60 seconds a minute, 60 minutes a hour) the believer is in prayerful communion with God, and there are no short circuits on the line. Time has a way of blowing fuses and wearing insulation thin.

Compare, for a moment, this holy cry of agonized devotion with the cry of Job 19:22: ["Why do you persecute me as God\...?"]{.class_3760} What Satan couldn't do in twenty-four hours throwing everything at Job (including the "kitchen sink"), Bildad, Zophar, and Eliphaz were able to do with [time]{.class_3759}. It isn't the counterattack that withers the souls of men; it is the artillery bombardment that goes on (like it did at Verdun, 1916) and on till your blood is like water and your heart is pounding in your ears. There is many a man who gave a good testimony while burning in the flames of Smithfield, England (1557--1559) who would never have survived the pressures placed on John Paton (1824--1907) in the New Hebrides or the pressures under which Beauchamp Vick (1902--1975) worked most of his life.

Well, we all come in the same way, and we all check out the same way (vs. 21). We come and go naked. Someone will wash your dead body before they embalm it (or cremate it), and they won't wash you with your clothes on. If you are fortunate enough to get a direct hit from a mortar shell or a bomb you will go naked anyway, because those projectiles will blow your clothes clean off you. You might die in the open field or drown in the ocean with your clothes on, but by the time the ants and flies get through with you (or the crabs and fishes), you will still go "naked." We are even when we start and even when we finish (Ecc. 7:8, 14). Job recognizes that he was in an unconscious state of dreamless sleep before his birth (Job 3:16--17), and if he is saved, he will return to that condition (Job 10:18--21). Of course, this is not the case when we die this side of Calvary. Our future state is a conscious rest in the Lord (1 Thess. 5:8--9; 2 Cor. 5:1--12) until the rapture, and then an eternity of glorious service with our "beloved" (Song of Sol. 2:16).

"[The LORD gave, and the LORD hath taken away\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 21). Well, it was Satan who "took it away" (vv. 12--19), but Job is not rebuked by the Lord for attributing Satan's work to Him (God). Satan has acted as ["the minister of God"]{.class_3762} (Rom. 13:1--4), and although Job has done nothing overtly to merit such treatment, He who ["knoweth the hearts"]{.class_3762} (Job 42:4; Amos 4:13) knew that Job needed some "refining" (Job 23:10---"don't we all"); so, God chooses him in ["the furnace of affliction"]{.class_3762} (Isa. 48:10). And ["in all this Job sinned not, nor charged God foolishly"]{.class_3762} (vs. 22; see Job 2:10). Like the perfect man of James 3:2, Job had the body under subjection; for, he was able to do what not one person in ten thousand is able to do: he was able to control his tongue---for awhile!

The most pitiful perversion of Job's classic answer will be found in the ["Living" Bible ]{.class_3759}(Taylor). Here, the powerful cry of Job has been reduced to a stunted "I shall have nothing when I die"---for ["naked shall I return thither."]{.class_3760} The substitution of "revile" for ["nor charged God foolishly"]{.class_3760} is a further diminishing of truth. The [New English Bible ]{.class_3759}says Job didn't charge God with "unreason," whatever in the ever-lovin', blue- eyed world [that ]{.class_3759}is supposed to mean. Ewald (and others) suppose that the ["womb"]{.class_3760} of verse 21 is the womb of "Mother Earth". The [New Scofield Reference Bible ]{.class_3759}adds the word "thee" to the verse to match a comment made by Adam Clarke in 1830. (["NEW" Scofield]{.class_3759}---did you get that?) Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown pick up Ewald (nineteenth century) and tell us that the womb is "poetically" the earth, "the universal mother." The so-called proof for this is Psalm 139:15. But in Psalm 139, David is speaking literally of the original creation (see Gen. 1:27; 2:7), not his own personal birth. Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown simply spiritualize both passages (as Origen would do it) and then give us their private interpretation. Fortunately, the Jews retain the [AV ]{.class_3759}(1611) reading in the Masoretic Bible of their publishing house.

Umbreit says that Job did not "commit folly against God," while B. Duhm says Job gave God "no occasion of offense." Budde says that Job kissed the ground; Dillmann, Peake, and Bullinger make a number of changes which all come under the same category: hot air.

[]{#IMh58iSMTFKKU0J9qyaeHw278.xhtml}

CHAPTER 2

2:1 Again there was a day when the sons of God came to present themselves before the LORD, and Satan came also among them to present himself before the LORD.

2 And the LORD said unto Satan, From whence comest thou? And Satan answered the LORD, and said, From going to and fro in the earth, and from walking up and down in it.

3 And the LORD said unto Satan, Hast thou considered my servant Job, that there is none like him in the earth, a perfect and an upright man, one that feareth God, and escheweth evil? and still he holdeth fast his integrity, although thou movest me against him, to destroy him without cause.

4 And Satan answered the LORD, and said, Skin for skin, yea, all that a man hath will he give for his life.

5 But put forth thine hand now, and touch his bone and his flesh, and he will curse thee to thy face.

6 And the LORD said unto Satan, Behold, he is in thine hand; but save his life.

Time passes, and although the old saying is "Time heals all wounds," time also will get you in the end; for it is "time" that eventually puts you into a hospital bed or a grave or both (see [The Sure Word of Prophecy]{.class_3759}).

"[Again, there was a day"]{.class_3760} (vs. 1) is not located chronologically in Job's life. One may suppose a lapse of an interval of anywhere from a week to a year. At any rate, the heavenly hosts report in for a review of their work---the scene is literal---and ["Satan came also among them to present himself before the LORD"]{.class_3762} (vs. 1).

The conversation of chapter 1 is repeated with the Lord's additional remark that Job ["still\...holdeth fast his integrity, although thou movedst me against him to destroy him without cause"]{.class_3760} (vs. 3).

The words above have a peculiar resemblance to the words which Christ spoke in John 15:25. And these words seem to have been forgotten by the commentators when they began to expound some of the passages later in Job. You see, Job is correct in his statements about ["the perfect and the wicked"]{.class_3760} in Job 9:22. The Lord admits (in Job 2:3) that He had no ["cause"]{.class_3760} for taking Job's children and property, yet the testing goes on! (Note Israel in the Tribulation.)

"[Skin for skin"]{.class_3760} (vs. 4). The exception proves the rule that most men value their own flesh above everything (Eph. 5:29), and although a man may love his children enough to die for them (2 Sam. 18:33), not many men will commit suicide even if they lose their children. Satan's point is sardonic and sarcastic, but it is terribly true. Even though Job wished for death (Job 3:20), we notice that he did not take his own life (see Elijah, Moses, Jonah, et al.). It is very difficult to pull the trigger on a .45 when you aim it into one of [your own ]{.class_3761}eyes.

In December of 1944, you could have found high school educated American boys choking each other, and then slitting each other's throats to get blood to drink; they were POW's on a Japanese ship sailing from Pier Seven (Manila) to Japan. ["Skin for skin\...all that a man hath will he give for his life."]{.class_3760} A man will give up his character, reputation, and even his humanity to stay alive. European citizens ate corpses in Buchenwald (1942--1944). In the death chambers of Auschwitz, the young men stamped over the bodies of their mothers and fathers to get air, while their mothers and sisters were trampling their little children to death in order to get the same. ["Skin for skin\...."]{.class_3760} You say: "Oh, I wouldn't do that!"

You don't know what [you'd]{.class_3759} do under some circumstances. I have known some Christians who (without a doubt!) would have murdered another Christian for fifty cents if they really thought they would not be held accountable for it. If you could hate a brother in Christ like that while you are well fed and well clothed, I wonder what some of you would do if you were starving for bread?

On April 21st, twelve days after the American capitulation on Bataan (1942), the American prisoners reached Camp O'Donnell (Luzon). During those twelve days more than two hundred of them had fallen down in the march due to exhaustion; whenever a man fainted he was shot to death with a pistol. The Japanese guards who convoyed the march left a trail of dead bodies from Corregidor to Cabanatuan. When a man began to stagger in the march, the guard would pull alongside him and say, "You sleepee? You lie down you get good long sleepee!" Why did they kill them? Simple: the Japanese couldn't waste time to fool with them. Prisoners take time, money, and food, and the Japanese soldiers were short on all three. In such a situation who lives? ["Skin for skin\...."]{.class_3760}

Do you know why U.S., old-line, regular officers were often "fragged" in Vietnam? Simple: if they lived, the troops would be involved in desperate hand-to-hand fighting on the ground in the underbrush. You can't whip an Oriental till you beat him on the ground man-to-man or else annihilate him with an atom bomb. The bomb is cheaper, but everyone is afraid to use it, so? So, the war in Vietnam had to be a stalemate. When a sure enough World War II line officer showed up---[one who believed in the aggressive Infantry attack]{.class_3759}---he was in danger of being "fragged" by his own troops. The troops in Vietnam were not there to WIN; they were there to get home alive after "365 days." So, many times a Captain, Major, or Colonel was found dead in his bunk, killed by his own men: ["Skin for skin\...."]{.class_3760}

"Me first, you next" is the universal law of human nature, and the rare exceptions only prove the rule. And---sad to say---some of the modern Christian "celebrities" who talk about "recognized scholarship," "godly men," and "spiritual works" are nothing but stuffed shirts who have enthroned their own egos at the expense of the Reformation Bible, the liberty of the Holy Spirit, and the next generation of preachers.

A friend of mine from Texas, named Wolfenbarger, was bear hunting in California with a buddy of his. A 550 pound bear slid down into a cave, after being hit thirteen times with a .22 rifle. (Wolfenbarger's buddy was drunk, and had taken a .22 with him instead of a 30.06.) The dogs were attacking in force, and a \$500 hunting dog was getting dangerously near the bear's fangs. The wounded bear began to spit out dogs right and left, and after awhile Wolfenbarger's buddy said, "Say there, Gene, how 'bout goin' in there and gettin' mah dog? He's worth \$500.00!" Wolfenbarger replied: "To you or to me?"

"[Skin for skin, all that a man hath\...."]{.class_3760}

A colored gentleman was telling how he jumped up a tall tree when a bear got after him. He related: "Dat fust limb looked like it wuz a mile ovah mah haid!" "Did you ketch it?" asked an interested friend. "Yasshu! I missed it gwine up, but ah ketched it comin' down."

A man will risk a jail sentence to feed his family. A man will risk the electric chair to get rid of a competitor. A man will pay \$5,000 to stay alive after he knows the rest of his life will be in a wheelchair or in a hospital bed. Exceptions like Stephen---who didn't value his life as much as he valued the Old Testament, and Paul---who didn't value his life as much as he did the gospel, or the Lord Jesus Christ---who laid down His life for sinners---only prove the rule. Wherever International Socialists or Communists get control, their first step is to remove any element that might "revolt against the revolting revolutionaries." It is going to be your skin for theirs, or vice versa, and if you think that 5800 years of Science, Education, and Religion has altered this basic element in human nature, you are a self-deceived idiot. (You're welcome! Anytime we can put it plainer, let us know.)

"[Put forth thine hand now and touch his bone and his flesh" ]{.class_3760}(vs. 5, cf. Eph. 5:29). This ought to work. Where the Gestapo goes to work on a man's insides with a hat pin, some results are usually obtained. The "bone and flesh" is where "it hurts." What is tolerable when we are in good health is a devilish torture when our limbs ache, and our flesh is lacerated or scorched. "A man can stand almost anything better than he can stand [pain]{.class_3761}," is the doctor's dictum; again, the exceptions prove the rule. In Malaya (1943--44), they skinned American flyers alive when they caught them. In Russia (1942--44), they drove shell casings through the knee caps of their prisoners, and the standard way of extracting information from a POW, in any action, is to pull out a long knife and threaten to castrate the prisoner if he doesn't cough up the information. ["Skin for skin\...."]{.class_3762}

"[And he will curse thee to thy face"]{.class_3760} (vs. 5). The reader should avail himself immediately of the work by Richard Wurmbrand, entitled [Stronger than Prison Walls]{.class_3761}, and see the principle in practise. Wurmbrand, like Job, was deprived of his family (temporarily), and then, for a longer period than Job, Wurmbrand was turned over to every diabolical device the human mind could invent to get him to ["curse God and die"]{.class_3762} (Job 2:9). He never did. However, there were times (see sermons in [Stronger than Prison Walls]{.class_3761}) when the "old man" in Wurmbrand could not help but speak like Job does in Job 29--31. Every Christian should spend some time in Wurmbrand's book; it is a mirror of the human heart---every human heart.

In this case, Satan has underestimated the moral strength of his victim. Job's character was as strong as his [reputation]{.class_3759}. Reputations are made in a moment and destroyed in a moment; [character ]{.class_3759}is built through a lifetime. Reputation is what you are supposed to be; [character ]{.class_3759}is what you are. Reputation can make you rich or poor, but [character ]{.class_3759}makes you happy or miserable. A man's reputation can be learned in an hour, but a man's [character ]{.class_3759}only comes to light after thirty to fifty years.

"[Behold he is in thine hand; but save his life"]{.class_3760} (vs. 6). The fearful permission is granted again (see 1:12), and now Job is "in for it" in no uncertain terms. In absolute ignorance of God's designs, and Satan's as well, Job unwillingly becomes a model for patience at the cost of everything a man values on this earth. "To lose your wealth is much, to lose your health is more, to lose your soul is such a loss as no man can restore." Job now loses his health, his friends, his wife's confidence, and, eventually, his own peace of mind and trust in God. As a man replied when another man asked about an estate of a deceased tycoon: "How much did he leave?"---[HE LEFT IT ALL]{.class_3761}. Job now loses everything but his life, and that is not worth living (see Job 3:1--26.)

The corrupt [Septuagint]{.class_3759}, written several hundred years [after ]{.class_3759}Paul's death, has written "bless" for ["curse"]{.class_3760} in verse 5, even though every orthodox Jew from Moses to David Ben Gurion read it as "blaspheme." The ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} has mistranslated ["the sons of God"]{.class_3760} (vs. 1) as "the angels," while refusing to translate the same wording as "angels" in Genesis 6:14. (The ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} is not a Bible at all; it is not even a "paraphrase" as advertised. It is the private interpretation of Mr. Taylor, promoted by Billy Graham and the Southern Baptist Convention. Genesis 9:26--27 in the ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} is not even near a "paraphase" as advertised. It is the private interpretation of 9:6, John 1:13; Luke 23:42; Prov. 13:13; Jer. 23:37; Rev. 13:18; Zech. 9:9, 12:10; Dan. 3:25; or Isa. 6:13).

2:7 So went Satan forth from the presence of the Lord, and smote Job with sore boils from the sole of his foot unto his crown.

8 And he took him a potsherd to scrape himself withal; and he sat down among the ashes.

The "sore boils" have been interpreted as a number of things; among them, smallpox, leprosy, and elephantiasis. The [Septuagint ]{.class_3761}had added "without the city" [(Greek: εξω της πολεως)]{.class_3779} to verse 8 so you will have to accept "leprosy" as the disease. Further proof for this is adduced by the fact that Job's friends stand ["afar off"]{.class_3762} (vs. 12) from him. However, the text did not say they stood afar off; it said ["when they lifted up their eyes afar off and knew him not, they lifted up their voice, and wept"]{.class_3762} (vs. 12).

Perhaps the most interesting diagnosis of Job's medical condition is given by Dr. Quain ([Dictionary of Medicine]{.class_3759}, Vol. 1, p. 431). Dr. Quain says that the disease is ["Elephantiasis Arabun,"]{.class_3759} a "non-contagious disease characterized by recurrence of cebrile paroxysms, attended by inflammation and progressive hypertrophy of the integument and areolar tissue, chiefly of the extremities and genital organs, and occasionally by swelling of the lymphatic glands, enlargment and presence in the blood of certain nematode haemotozoa, together with various symptoms of a morbid or depraved state of nutrition." (Oof!) Professor Macalister calls the infection ["Biskra sores"]{.class_3759} a Mesopotamian disease.

But since ["boils"]{.class_3760} (vs. 7) do not ordinarily attend leprosy or elephantiasis, the question is still open. The best description of Job's disease will be found in the material given by the Lord God for Israel in the Tribulation (see Deut. 28:59--61 and Lev. 16:16). As we stated in our Preface, the [Book of Job ]{.class_3759}is a type of the Tribulation, and if one will read Revelation 16:2, 11, he will see the similitudes immediately. Job is probably smitten with smallpox, leprosy, and elephantiasis. ["And he took him a potsherd\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 8), the word for a "sherd" of pottery or a broken piece of pottery. This is for the purpose of scraping off the pus and bugs, and he could also use it as a "scratcher." ["And he sat down among the ashes"]{.class_3760} (vs. 8, cf. Ezek. 27:30 and Jonah 3:6). Job fell down upon the ground (Job 1:20) the first time, indicating that he was brought as low by his troubles as a man can get. Now he takes his place among the ashes, to symbolize "ashes to ashes, dust to dust." The figure is that of a [dead man ]{.class_3759}who has [decomposed ]{.class_3759}(Matt. 11:21, Gen. 18:27, Mal. 4:3, Dan. 9:3, Jer. 6:26, etc.). Since ashes are found where bodies have been burned (Gehenna, the city dump), Job is obviously outside of his hometown, sitting down with the refuse of "ecological pollution." He has now enrolled in Ashheap University, and there he will learn what no "recognized scholar" could teach him at the largest universities in Europe, Asia, or America.

2:9 Then said his wife unto him, Dost thou still retain thine integrity? curse God, and die.

10 But he said unto her, Thou speakest as one of the foolish women speaketh. What? Shall we receive good at the hand of God, and shall we not receive evil? In all this did not Job sin with his lips.

It is the opinion of Adam Clarke and Gesenius (the great Hebrew lexicographer), that Mrs. Job said, "Bless God and die," but this overlooks the work of the Adversary. The infallible [Authorized Text ]{.class_3759}quickly corrects the blunders of Gesenius and Clarke by chapter 1, verse 11. Job's wife has a "hot line" to the throne room, and one should give her speech a "second take" before altering it. She has "besought Job, in Satan's stead, to be alienated from God." She couldn't have made the suggestion better if she had been in the council room of the third heaven (Job 1:11) when the "original" was "verbally inspired."

Mrs. Job, Mrs. Adam, Mrs. David (2 Sam. 6:20), and Mrs. Abraham (Gen 16:2) are about as helpful to their husbands, sometimes, as Mrs. John Wesley.

"[Dost thou still retain thine integrity?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 9)---after all of [this]{.class_3761}? Dead children, ruined property, destroyed cattle, incurable diseases, and unanswered prayer. "You still think you are a spiritual saint, in the will of God?" How could a [blind man ]{.class_3761}be in the will of God (John 9:2)? How could a [poor man ]{.class_3761}get saved (Luke 18:26)? How could [infirmities ]{.class_3761}be found in a man who could raise the dead (2 Cor. 12:10)?

Job's trial is a sore trial. And perhaps the fiercest things that a man in pain ever had to endure were the added taunts of those who take his misfortune to be a sure token of his damnation (see Phil. 1:28 and comments). Isn't it amazing how close, again, the parallel runs to Matthew 27:39, 41 and the death of Jesus Christ? When you are right and know you are right and are suffering for it (1 Pet. 2:19--21), the greatest tormentors are loved ones and "kin-folk" who think in terms of "success means spiritual power," "attendance records mean God's blessing," "money means fellowship with God," and "monuments to orthodoxy prove fidelity to the word of God," or better still: "book sales mean you are the mightiest pen in the twentieth century!"

Ah, how prone we are to judge by the ["outward appearance"]{.class_3760} (1 Sam. 16:7)!

"[Curse God and die"]{.class_3760} (vs. 9). That is, "Go on, get it over with! God is obviously against you, and you can't deny it. Don't tell me you could be right with God and have all of these things happen to you. Why play the hypocrite, Job? Take your place with the atheists and Satanists and go on home to Hell." How would that sound coming out of the mouth of your "help-meet"? (The culprit told the judge: "Jedge, if you knew dat woman like ah knowd dat woman, you wouldn't call me no [desertah]{.class_3761}! Ah ain't no desertah; ah's a REFUGEE!")

I know of a fine Christian man in Florida who still witnesses for Christ and is faithful in church attendance, family devotions, and giving. In a period of eight years his mother died, his wife's brother died, his boy got killed, his son-in-law's house and his house burned to the ground, he and his wife took three trips to the hospital, and then his daughter's home broke up. I never knew that man when he wasn't praising God and rejoicing in God's mercy. Perhaps the townspeople said, "He must have some terrible, unconfessed secret sin in his life for God to deal with him like that," but that is exactly what Job's three reprobate friends said about him (Job 8:4--6)!

"[But he said unto her\...What? Shall we receive good at the hand of God, and shall we not receive evil?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 10). After telling his "Missus" that she talks like a fool (vs. 10), Job lays the foundations for Romans 8:28. We [do ]{.class_3761}receive evil at the hand of God, and that is exactly where Job got his "evil" from on this tour of duty. Notice how the word is used, especially in Lamentations 3:38; Jeremiah 4:6, 6:19, 18:11, 19:3, 49:37; and Jonah 3:10. (See also Dan. 9:12 and Isa. 45:7.) In every case, the Lord is speaking of something terrible that He will allow to happen to people because of [their ]{.class_3761}wickedness. When the Lord "repents" of [His own ]{.class_3761}evil designs in Exodus 32:12--15 (see remarks in that Commentary), He merely decides not to let the logical effect come to pass that was caused by the actions of evil people, [and people are "evil" according to the Lord Jesus Christ ]{.class_3761}(Luke 11:13).

A building contractor donated two buildings (worth \$12,000) to Jack Wyrtzen's Camp in up state New York; they were to room fifty boys each. But his business fell through later, and the man had to declare bankruptcy. Jack appologized to the contractor about his donation, which had contributed to his loss, but the contractor responded with: "Look here, brother, don't do away with the last bit of money I have left. That \$12,000 was given to the Lord; [I still have it!"]{.class_3759}

There was the Dutch seaman (1830) who fell from a mast and broke an arm so badly that they had to amputate it. Before the operation he grinned and said, "Well, thank God! It was the one that had arthritis in it!"

A preacher, back in the 1940's, saw a man in the revival services who was under deep conviction. On the fourth night, he approached this man and pressed the claims of Christ on him. The story he got back in return was this:

"Preacher, I want to get saved, but I know if I get saved, I'm going to have to start giving God a large share of my wealth, and I don't want to give it away. I own four of the largest wheat farms in western Kansas. If I get saved, I will have to give the Lord over \$10,000 a year. You see, I was not always rich. As a matter of fact, during the depression I was so poor I had to get food out of garbage cans. One night on the shores of Lake Michigan, I saw a bunch of rich folks having a drunken party on board a yacht out in the harbor. They were throwing T-bone steaks overboard without eating them. I could see it from the pier. I cursed them and I vowed, then and there, that some day I would get rich, I would have me a yacht like that, and when I did, I would throw away some T-bone steaks myself!"

"Well," he continued, "I have my yacht. I've eaten steak nearly every day of my life for the last five years, but I'm miserable. I'm lost and I know it, and I want to get saved; but if I get saved\...."

The preacher had prayer with that wheat farmer and left him to God. A week later the man received Christ. He got peace immediately and began to give to God liberally. But a year had not passed when he received a telegram from western Kansas. It said in effect: "Grasshopper plague. Crop destroyed. Lost 8,000 acres. Can't make ends meet." The landowner showed this to the preacher who had led him to Christ. The preacher shook his head in wonder and sympathy and said, "Oh that's too bad! That's terrible! I know the Devil must really be shaking your faith in the Lord after that." The man laughed and said, "Listen preacher. You said the Lord made all the animals, right?" "Yes," said the preacher. "Well when I got saved, I gave all that wheat to the Lord, right?"

"Well, yes, I guess you did," said the preacher.

"Well, Reverend," said the landowner, "the wheat is His and the grasshoppers are His; so, if He wants to feed [His grasshoppers on His own wheat]{.class_3761}, that's His business, and I ain't going to worry about it!"

"[In all this did not Job sin with his lips"]{.class_3760} (vs. 10).

Job is past Gethsemane without knowing it. He is at the place where his own will is inoperative, except in deciding how he will respond in [God's]{.class_3759} will. "No man is happy on the rack," says the ancient proverb, and Job is on the rack: miserable but faithful, pained but thankful, tormented but prayerful, forsaken but trusting, and harassed but truthful. Oh remarkable man!

The obscene [Septuagint ]{.class_3759}manuscripts used for the[ Living Bible]{.class_3759} and the [New American Standard Version]{.class_3759} have added the following Alexandrian playlet to verse 9: "How long wilt thou hold out, saying, Behold, I wait yet a little while expecting the hope of my deliverance? For behold, thy memorial is abolished from the earth, even thy sons and daughters. The pangs and pains of my womb which I bore in vain with sorrows; and thou thyself sittest down to spend the nights in the open air among the blah, blah, blah, etc." (Some scribe at Alexandria must have had some trouble with his blabber-mouthed wife before he added some one hundred words to the true text.)

2:11 Now when Job's three friends heard of all this evil that was come upon him, they came every one from his own place; Eliphaz the Temanite, and Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite: for they had made an appointment together to come to mourn with him and to comfort him.

12 And when they lifted up their eyes afar off, and knew him not, they lifted up their voice, and wept; and they rent every one his mantle, and sprinkled dust upon their heads toward heaven.

13 So they sat down with him upon the ground seven days and seven nights, and none spake a word unto him: for they saw that his grief was very great.

Well, at last Job's "friends" show up. The pitiful number ["three"]{.class_3760} is striking; for, don't you know he had more friends than he could count (see Job 29:1--25) when he was "in the chips." How many "friends" the prodigal had when he arrived in the far country. But when he[ "had spent all"]{.class_3760} and there arose ["a mighty famine in that land,"]{.class_3760} don't you know all he had to eat was "swill"? Job's ["three friends"]{.class_3760} (vs. 11) were three real friends, at least when they approach the city dump where Job is communing with rats, lice, fleas, tin cans, beer bottles, car tires, busted refrigerators, and worn out mattresses.

"[Eliphaz the Temanite"]{.class_3760} (vs. 11) is clearly associated with Genesis 25:5--15 or Genesis 36:10, 11 (or both). ["Bildad the Shuhite"]{.class_3762} (vs. 11) is connected with Genesis 15:2, 3 and Ezekiel 25:13; both he and Eliphaz are from Edom. ["Zophar the Naamathite"]{.class_3762} (vs. 11) is associated with the land area of Joshua 15:21--24 and 41. (Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown's note that this is not the area is to be taken with a tablespoon of salt.)

One should notice the insertion of the word ["comfort"]{.class_3760} in verse 11. They had not only agreed to come and mourn with him, but also to ["comfort him"]{.class_3760} (vs. 11). How this ["comfort"]{.class_3760} turns out is well illustrated in Job 12:1, 2 and 13:4. But who could really ["comfort"]{.class_3760} a man in Job's position? The seven days of silent mourning (vs. 13) are just about as good a ["comfort"]{.class_3760} as a man in Job's position could get. (For the seven days see 1 Sam. 31:13 and Ezek. 3:15.)

As we have remarked before, all attempts to stereotype the "three friends" as having [separate philosophies of life ]{.class_3759}are hopeless. All three of them (plus Elihu) wind up condemning Job in one way or another, and all three (plus Elihu), at some time in their discourses, imply that God will not dispense evil to good people; only bad people. This is a universal trait of human nature (see Acts 28:4 and comments), and it is so strong that 800,000,000 people on this earth---or more---think they will not go to Hell if they live "good lives." Wishful thinking.

The ["patience of Job"]{.class_3760} (James 5:11) is now demonstrated; for, Job does not "open his mouth" (see 3:1) until he has spent around 168 hours in silence. Not one murmur, not one complaint, not one appeal for sympathy (Job 6:23), and not one whimper about God's dealings with him: "Ye have heard of the patience of Job" (James 5:11). If "tribulation worketh patience" (Rom. 5:3), Job had more work done on him in those seven days than most of us have done on us in a lifetime.

"[Wept\...rent everyone his mantle\...sprinkled dust upon their heads" ]{.class_3760}(vs. 12). Again, the Oriental gestures are highly expressive. The tears show the grief, and the rent clothes show the [willingness to die ]{.class_3761}(Heb. 10:20), while the dust on the head (see Acts 22:23 and remarks in that commentary), indicates ["bury me."]{.class_3761}

"[For they saw that his grief was very great"]{.class_3760} (vs. 13, cf. Isa. 57:17, 18 and Num. 12:12, 14). And why shouldn't it be? Could fate deal a man a more terrible hand than dead children, loss of money and property, a reprobate wife, and an incurable disease? How much worse can it get short of going to Hell? (The reader should ponder this thought when studying the Great Tribulation and the passages found in Lam. 3 and Job 10, 16--17.) Job is utterly destitute; he was smitten the way that no man in the Old Testament was ever smitten. Not even David, Moses, Saul, Joshua, Cain, Noah, Jacob, Samson, Eli, Hezekiah, Jehu, Uzziah, Ahab, or Jonah were ever persecuted by Satan the way Job was persecuted.

Paul and John Wesley were the only two men, humanly speaking, who could get into the same ring with Job, and not even Paul knew the loss of wife and children, plus the loss of millions of dollars worth of goods. Paul was never the "greatest man" of anywhere when it came to cattle and goods, and Paul never had a demon-possessed wife to put up with.

(In answering a questionnaire at the HEW department, the conversation went as follows:

"Your height?"

"Five feet eight inches, suh."

"Your age?"

"Ah's forty-two years old, yassuh."

"Married?"

"No suh, dat scar is when ah was kicked by a mule."

Or, as one colored gentleman said when another one confessed that his own wife was an "angel": "Boy, you sho is lucky. Mine's still libin!")

Time now has done its work, and although Job never arrives at the destination predicted for him by Satan (Job 1:11), he does, at least, curse the day of his birth. Before the dialogue is over he comes pretty close to cursing his "fellow companions in tribulation" (Rev. 1:9).

[]{#IMh58iSMTFKKU0J9qyaeHw355.xhtml}

CHAPTER 3

3:1 After this opened Job his mouth, and cursed his day.

2 And Job spake, and said,

3 Let the day perish wherein I was born, and the night in which it was said, There is a man child conceived.

4 Let that day be darkness; let not God regard it from above, neither let the light shine upon it.

5 Let darkness and the shadow of death stain it; let a cloud dwell upon it; let the blackness of the day terrify it.

6 As for that night, let darkness seize upon it; let it not be joined unto the days of the year, let it not come into the number of the months.

7 Lo, let that night be solitary, let no joyful voice come therein.

8 Let them curse it that curse the day, who are ready to raise up their mourning.

9 Let the stars of the twilight thereof be dark; let it look for light, but have none; neither let it see the dawning of the day:

10 Because it shut not up the doors of my mother's womb, nor hid sorrow from mine eyes.

Job's friends have probably been talking to each other through the week (2:13), but they do not speak ["a word unto him"]{.class_3760} (2:13). Now Job himself opens his mouth and curses ["his day"]{.class_3760} (3:1). ["His day"]{.class_3760} is obviously the day of his birth (Ecc. 7:1). The ["man child" ]{.class_3760}of verse 3 clearly puts the passage (prophetically) into the Great Tribulation (see Rev. 12:5 and Isa. 66:8--9). Paul is a type of this ["man]{.class_3760} [child,"]{.class_3760} born out of ["due time"]{.class_3760} (1 Cor. 15:8), and the day that is[ "darkness"]{.class_3760} (Job 3:4) is so plainly the time of Jacob's troubles (see Zeph. 1:15) that only a premillennialist like Dr. Barton Payne or Biederwolf would make the foolish mistake of saying there are only ten prophetic references in Job. [There are better than twenty in the first ten chapters. ]{.class_3759}For the sake of clarity we shall list the elements in Job 3:1--10 that will match the Great Tribulation exactly, and after this we will make our comments.

1\. Job ["cursed his day"]{.class_3760} (vs. 1), and this matches the ["cursed"]{.class_3760} of Jeremiah 20:14. (Jeremiah means "Jehovah will cast out." The term "cast out" occurs thirteen times in that book of fifty-two chapters---four times thirteen---and Jeremiah is so plainly a type of the Tribulation remnant at the time of Rev. 11:1--3, that no comment should be necessary.)

2\. ["There is a man child conceived"]{.class_3760} (vs. 3) matches the ["man child"]{.class_3760} of Revelation 12:5, and there is no way around the passage even if a secondary application is made to the Lord Jesus Christ. The primary application is to an "anointed of the Lord" (Lam. 4:20) who shows up in Daniel's Seventieth Week.

3\. ["Let that day be darkness"]{.class_3760} (vs. 4) as it will be in Revelation 6:12 and 16:10 where the conditions of Exodus are reduplicated.

4\. ["Neither let the light shine upon it"]{.class_3760} (vs. 4) as in Zephaniah 1:15; Acts 2:20; and many other places.

5\. ["Let\...the shadow of death stain it"]{.class_3760} (vs. 5) as the cloud of death will move across this earth in the Tribulation, distributing death-dealing radioactivity to anything underneath it (Isa. 9:2; Psa. 44:19; Jer. 13:16; Amos 5:8, etc.). And don't let any backslidden, Bible-rejecting, conservative scholar seduce you from that truth by pointing out the [partial fulfillment ]{.class_3759}of Isa. 9:2 at the First Advent. (See [The Bible Believer's Commentary on Matthew]{.class_3759}, chaps. 1, 2, and 3).

6\. ["That night"]{.class_3760} (vss. 6--7) is a reference to the end of this age (Matt. 28: 19--20) before the "sunrise" of Matthew 13:43 (see comments in that Commentary).

7\. ["Let it not be joined unto the days of the year"]{.class_3760} (vs. 6), so Daniel's Seventieth Week is a parenthesis, or [interval]{.class_3759}, which stands between the Church Age and the Millennium.

8\. ["Who are ready to raise up their mourning"]{.class_3760} (vs. 8) is the mourning that will go on at the death of the Antichrist (Zech. 11:17) when he receives the ["deadly wound"]{.class_3760} (Rev. 13:3) and is supposed to stay dead.

For naive and gullible expositors like Dr. Barton Payne---see his so-called Encyclopedia of Prophecy---the Hebrew preserves "Leviathan" for "mourning" (see comments under Job 41:1--34) showing a double application; for, Leviathan will be "raised up" as Christ arose from the dead. The failure of Conservative and Fundamental faculties (Pikeville College, Nyack College, McPherson, Malone, Azusa Pacific, Houghton, Howard, Payne, Pfeiffer, Hope, Grove City, Huntington, Judson, North Park, Oakland City, Rockmount, Olivet Nazarene, Messiah, Manchester, etc.) to follow this line of Scriptural revelation is due to their irreverent and stupid treatment of the [Authorized Text ]{.class_3759}(1611). To say that there are only one to ten "prophetic references" in the [Book of Job ]{.class_3759}is the equivalent of saying there are only two references to salvation in the epistle of Paul the Apostle to the Romans.

9\. ["Neither let it see the dawning of the day"]{.class_3760} (vs. 9) indicates that Moses' judgments on Egypt (Exod. 10:21--23 and comments in that commentary) will be repeated when Moses again faces "Pharaoh" in Egypt (Rev. 11:3--9 and comments in that Commentary).

10\. ["The doors of my mother's womb"]{.class_3760} (vs. 10) clearly matches the birth of the ["man child"]{.class_3760} in 1 Samuel 1:11 (see Job 2:9--10). Other verses along this line are Jeremiah 13:21; Micah 4:9--10; and 1 Thessalonians 5:3.

Job's lament opens with ten references to the time of ["Jacob's trouble"]{.class_3760} (Jer. 30:7), and woe to the monument-building, face-saving apostate who treats such discourse as a "hobbyhorse" because he has rejected everything in the word of God that is not politically or economically expedient to his own work. Those of us who are instructed to labor ["in the word and in doctrine"]{.class_3760} (1 Tim. 5:17) are not to be hoodwinked by these professional religious "Oscar Award" winners who won their medals laboring to "get visible results" at the expense of the [Holy Bible.]{.class_3759}

There is nothing difficult about the passage from a [practical ]{.class_3759}standpoint. Job is cursing his birth because he feels that it would be better for him if he had never been born (Matt. 26:24). He finds himself where Jeremiah found himself in Jeremiah 20:15 and where Ezekiel found himself in Ezekiel 5:12--17.

Now, setting aside our Holy Bibles for a few seconds, let us see what the "highly qualified authorities" have to say about Job's soliloquy.

A. The [Pulpit Commentary ]{.class_3759}says that "days cannot perish" because the year "will always have its full complement of 365 days till time shall be no more" (p. 49). Evidently Rev. Rawlinson (The Canon of Canterbury) didn't spend much time reading his Bible. God did not count several thousand days as parts of any year in the time that Israel was out of fellowship with Him (see the charts by Clarence Larkin in [Dispensational Truth]{.class_3759}).

B. Verse 8 is "an impossible rendering" according to the [Pulpit Commentary]{.class_3759}. For a "possible reading" we are given this mish-mash: "Those who are rash enough to stir up the fury of the [crocodile]{.class_3759}\...." (p. 49).

C. This fable about Leviathan being a "crocodile" (see Job 41:1 and comments) is found preserved in all of its lying stupidity in the [New English Bible ]{.class_3759}(Cambridge, 1971). E. S. English ([NSRB]{.class_3759}) blows C. H. Dodd a kiss by saying that "perhaps" Leviathan is a crocodile ([NSRB]{.class_3759}, 1967, p. 599).

Well, he could be a crocodile as quickly as the [New Scofield Reference Bible ]{.class_3759}could be "the [Authorized Version]{.class_3759}." That is---never.

D. Adam Clarke led the way for English and his buddies (Culbertson, Feinberg, MacRae, Weniger, McClain, Wilbur Smith, and Walvoord) when he told Rev. Rawlinson that "those who are ready to raise up their mourning" meant "those who are ready to provoke the crocodile to tear them to pieces." (Clarke's Commentary, p. 32). Schuttens says ["mourning"]{.class_3760} should be the "hosts of evil."

Now that the apostates of the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries have showed us how to sit in judgment on the word of God and to reject it as being authoritative, all of the men who taught the faculty members of Evangel College, George Fox College, Bethany, Eastern Mennonite, Geneva, Eastern, Dordt, Calvin, Bryan, Concordia, Cedarville, Chapman, Anderson, and Augustana are now aboard the sinking ship.

"Leviathan" is a "[mythical ]{.class_3761}sky monster" (R. A. Torrey, F. C. Eiselen, Andrew Zenos, John R. Sampey, Milton Terry, A. T. Robertson, Charles Scoville, D. R. Dungan, J. W. Monser, Ira Price, and the [ASV ]{.class_3761}committee of 1901).

You'll find how ["mythical"]{.class_3759} he is when he lands (Rev. 12:12).

Imagine such "good and godly men" as A. T. Robertson and R. A. Torrey falling for that liberal, allegorical, Alexandrian BUNKO. Then imagine John R. Rice and Robert Sumner whining about the "godly lives" of these men to get you to take a clear cut stand against the word of God.

Paul, ["born out of due time,"]{.class_3760} is also a matchmate to the lament of Job in verse 10, and therefore, Paul (as Jonah and Jeremiah) becomes a type of a Jew who will witness to Gentiles in the Tribulation (see Rev. 7 and comments in that Commentary).

E. The [Bible Commentary ]{.class_3759}tells us that the ["mourning" ]{.class_3760}of verse 8 is the Leviathan of Job 41:1, and this Leviathan is a ["crocodile."]{.class_3759} (See notes under Job 41:1, and then tell God Almighty how anyone could reject that much of the word of God, at sight, and then be passed off as "loyal to the word!")

3:11 Why died I not from the womb? Why did I not give up the ghost when I came out of the belly?

12 Why did the knees prevent me? or why the breasts that I should suck?

13 For now should I have lain still and been quiet, I should have slept: then had I been at rest,

14 With kings and counsellors of the earth, which built desolate places for themselves;

15 Or with princes that had gold, who filled their houses with silver:

16 Or as an hidden untimely birth I had not been; as infants which never saw light.

17 There the wicked cease from troubling; and there the weary be at rest.

18 There the prisoners rest together; they hear not the voice of the opressor.

19 The small and great are there; and the servant is free from his master.

As we have said in our Preface, there is nothing complicated about Job's speeches if they are only dealt with in their historical, practical setting, divorced from the books of Revelation, Daniel, and Matthew. Plainly, Job is wishing he were dead or had never been born in the first place, and in his position, who could really blame him? George Williams of Panama City, Florida---lifetime wheelchair case---told me that he had tried often to roll off his bed at night and bang his head on an iron radiator so that he could put himself out of his misery; he never could get enough force and momentum in the roll to produce a concussion. After he was converted, George lived in a life of joy and victory in Christ, and he preached the gospel all over the Southland. Even then, who (saved or lost) would enjoy a lifetime of sitting where he constantly feels he is a burden to his loved ones?

"[Why died I not from the womb?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 11 cf. Psa. 59:10). Why not a stillbirth with no chance to breathe ["the breath of life"]{.class_3762} (Gen. 30:3, 50:23, etc.) on someone's lap? Why were breasts ready to feed me (Jer. 20:18)? What was the point in all of it if my life has come to [this]{.class_3761}?

And hasn't that thought occurred to all of us at one time or another?

And if it hasn't ever occured to you, what of real life have you ever seen?

When Margaret Holden (Panama City, Florida) was about forty years old, she suffered the amputation of first one leg and then the other. When I went to her hospital room and drew her a picture of the Second Advent, she was waiting to have an arm amputated. She wept when I spoke of the Millennium. I asked her about spiritual things, and she confessed that she had never been saved; whereupon she trusted Christ, right then and there, as her Saviour. When she expressed sorrow over a wasted life and wept over how much suffering she had gone through for her own problems, I spoke to her of her Millennial inheritance. Through tears she said, "Well I can suffer for Him now! I can suffer for Him now!" Yes, and she is.

A transient (twentieth-century Ugartic for "BUM") was brought to my house one time by a friend. I dealt with that poor bum for an hour and could not get him to receive Christ. Finally, I quit talking and just stared at him. His gums were yellow brown with a snaggle-tooth sticking out here and there. His breath smelled like rancid petunias; he had been drinking Bay Rum. The toes of his tennis shoes were out; the soles were held on by rubber bands. His hair was matted, he was unshaven, and he still had three scabs on his face where someone had "peeled" him the night before. Finally I said, "Where is your [wife?]{.class_3759}" He said, "I ain't got one." I pressed further: "Where are your [children]{.class_3759}?"

"Gone."

"Where is your job?"

"Don't have one."

"Where is your money?"

"It's all gone." And then he began to weep.

"Where is your health?"

"lt's gone! It's all gone, preacher, all gone!"

"All right," I said, "Look at you! You've served the Devil for forty years, and he's taken everything from you you ever had. In the name of Heaven, what makes you love Satan so much that you can't turn your back on him and receive God's Son as your Saviour?"

Down he went. He knelt on the floor by my couch as we prayed, and when he got up he had a Master who could put clothes on his back, food in his stomach, money in his pockets, and children in his living room. But not so with Job.

Job had served the [right ]{.class_3759}Master (Job 1:5), but he had received the pay which comes from serving the wrong one.

Verses 13--19 are self-explanatory. They describe the unknown, unrevealed state of the dead or the never-alive (Ecc. 6:3). Of course, Job speaks from the standpoint of Solomon---before the death, burial, and resurection of the Lord Jesus Christ. ["Still\...quiet\...slept\...at rest"]{.class_3760} (vs. 13). The so-called ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} was written by a man who could not figure out the expression ["which built desolate places for themselves"]{.class_3760} in view of the fact that they also ["filled their houses with silver"]{.class_3760} (vs. 15); so Kenneth Taylor ([The Dead Comic Book]{.class_3759}) simply erased half of verse 14 and then reduced the verse markings to a size where you would not take time to check the verse: Very typical of "living" Bibles in the "language of the people," etc. However, this private interpretation (not "paraphrase") is not original with Taylor. C. H. Dodd, the Bible-denying infidel ([NEB]{.class_3759}), did the same thing in his publication ten years (1961) before Taylor followed his steps. "All roads lead to apostasy"---at least since 1880. You see, the reading was not even original with Dodd. The [Septuagint ]{.class_3759}of 330--500 A.D. (not B.C.) left out the reading also, although it is in the Masoretic Hebrew text of the Hebrew Old Testament. (Well, well, well. Back at Alexandria again, after so short a vacation.)

Now the irrational prejudice which lurked behind the [Septuagint ]{.class_3759}manuscripts (and found its way through fifteen centuries of "living Bibles") was the conceit that ["kings and counsellors of the earth"]{.class_3760} (vs. 14) could [not ]{.class_3759}possibly build ["desolate places for themselves."]{.class_3760} But what man acquainted with any literature like ["Ozymandias of Egypt"]{.class_3759} or the ["Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam"]{.class_3759} could possibly fail to see the appropriateness of the verse? And how could the faculty members at Alexandria (or Chicago Bible College, Circleville Bible College, Columbia, Dallas, Trinity, Detroit, Beulah Heights, Sterling, Eastern, Whitworth, Westmont, Piedmont, Tabor, Simpson, Southwestern, Spring Arbor, or Seattle Pacific, etc.) miss the import of the statement with Isaiah 27:10; Ezekiel 26:19; Jeremiah 7:34; Zechariah 7:14; Ezekiel 16:20; Malachi 1:4; and Proverbs 3:25 sitting on their desks?

The kings and counsellors did not intend to build desolate places (Psa. 19:10--11), but that is how it turned out.

In the place of departed spirits are dead "kings and counsellors" (vs. 14), ["princes that had gold"]{.class_3762} (vs. 15), and ["infants which never saw light."]{.class_3762} There class, racial, and social distinctions cease (vss. 17--19), and the things for which modern politicians and religious leaders beat the drum are accomplished. Death is a great leveler. In the Old Testament the weary rest (1 Sam. 18:15), the wicked in Hell can no longer bother anybody (vs. 17), the prisoners (Luke 18) sleep (John 1:11), and the oppressor can no longer harangue them. ["The small and great are there: and the servant is free from his master"]{.class_3762} (vs. 19), and not before then, according to the apostle Paul (1 Tim. 6:1--4).

3:20 Wherefore is light given to him that is in misery, and life unto the bitter in soul;

21 Which long for death, but it cometh not; and dig for it more than for hid treasures;

22 Which rejoice exceedingly and are glad, when they can find the grave?

23 Why is light given to a man whose way is hid, and whom God hath hedged in?

24 For my sighing cometh before I eat, and my roarings are poured out like the waters.

25 For the thing which I greatly feared is come upon me, and that which I was afraid of is come unto me.

26 I was not in safety, neither had I rest, neither was I quiet; yet trouble came.

Verse 20 is one of the great hallmarks of the [Book of Job]{.class_3759}, and for the benefit of those who really think about life---not just "philosophy"---we shall list the rest of these "hallmarks" that appear as questions:

1\. ["Why is light given to a man whose way is hid\...?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 23).

2\. ["Shall mortal man be more just than God?"]{.class_3760} (4:17).

3\. ["Shall a man be more pure than his maker?"]{.class_3760} (4:17).

4\. ["What is man that thou shouldest magnify him?"]{.class_3760} (7: 17).

5\. ["If I be wicked, why then labour I in vain?"]{.class_3760} (9:29).

6\. ["Canst thou by searching find out God?" ]{.class_3760}(11:7).

7\. ["Canst thou find out the Almighty unto perfection?"]{.class_3760} (11:7).

8\. ["Man dieth, and wasteth away\...and where is he?"]{.class_3760} (14:10).

9\. ["What is man that he should be clean?"]{.class_3760} (15:14).

10\. ["And he which is born of woman, that he should be righteous?"]{.class_3760} (15:14).

11\. ["Wherefore do the wicked live\...yea, are mighty in power?"]{.class_3760} (21:7).

12\. ["Can a man be profitable unto God\...?"]{.class_3760} (22:2).

13\. ["How then can man be justified with God?"]{.class_3760} (25:4).

14\. ["Whence then cometh wisdom? Where is\...understand ing?"]{.class_3760} (28:20).

15\. ["What profit shall I have if I be cleansed from my sin?"]{.class_3760} (35:3).

16\. ["Where is God my maker\...?" ]{.class_3760}(35:10).

17\. ["Wilt thou condemn me, that thou mayest be righteous?"]{.class_3760} (40:8).

18\. ["Who is he that hideth counsel without knowledge?"]{.class_3760} (42:3).

Now these are the [real ]{.class_3759}questions. These are the weighty questions which Socrates, Aristotle, and Plato rarely discussed. In their hypocritical pretense of "searching for the supreme good," they were careful to take the detour around such questions as Numbers 2--3, 8--9, 13, and 16. Most philosophers (see Col. 2:8 and comments) also skirt Numbers 14--15, and 18. As we remarked in our Preface, the [Book of Job ]{.class_3759}deals with the basic, fundamental, root problem of Life: [Why do the righteous suffer? ]{.class_3759}The questions above are all related to that problem, and until these questions have been answered, or at least given consideration (prayerfully!), there is no understanding of God or man that is worth the trouble to obtain. And you will notice that the answer (or answers) to these questions never takes the form of the modern Religio-Philosopher (Bonhoeffer, Niebhur, Pike, Tillich, Updike, Weigle, Herber, C. S. Lewis, Karl Adam, Donald Baillie, lan Barbour, Peter Berger, Bruckeger, Eiseley, et al.).

That is, it never takes a form that even resembles the following:

1\. "We suggest that a person find his identity only in relation to [others]{.class_3759}."

2\. "Distributive justice involving coercion is the necessary instrument of love in the impersonal relationships of society."

3\. "Cumpulsory segregation is humiliating to a minority group, therefore the [majority ]{.class_3759}should be [forced ]{.class_3759}into compulsory integration."

4\. "The real starting point in maturity is overcoming prejudice."

5\. "We must be sensitive to the need for change even when faced with ambiguous alternatives."

6\. "The idea of God expresses man's ultimate concern, transferred to a horizontal plane."

7\. "In the depths of the dimension and symbols, the total thrust of coordinate truths and relative meanings tends to a dynamic whole."

8\. "The culture of modernity is the answer to religious literalism."

9\. "Vital religion is richer than the conceptual system in which the framework of the areas are coordinated."

10\. "Ultimate concern and total involvement is meaningfully [relevant ]{.class_3759}to [validated ]{.class_3759}humanism and the ethnic social [environment]{.class_3759}."

Now you can get just as much hot air from a jet engine, and it won't smell any worse. Erich Frank, Erich Fromm, Kim Malthe-Bruun, Rollo May, David Read, David Roberts, Martin Buber, and E. Cherbonnier make their living with this kind of ding-a-ling theology, and where a man's living is bound up with his beliefs, he will die and go to Hell with his "beliefs" before he will alter them: ["skin for skin\..."]{.class_3760} etc.

"[Why is light given to him that is in misery, and life unto the bitter in soul?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 20). What about the thousands who starve to death every year in Asia and Africa? What woman likes to see her baby's stomach swell out with hunger as the child's limbs become sticks poking through skin stretched as tight as a drumhead? How do you think some German private in the Wermacht felt when he finally died in Siberia in 1970, after twenty-six years in a prison camp? Do you realize that more than fifty thousand German POW's were never released at the end of World War II? More than half of them died in Siberia after a "lasting peace" (ho-ho!) was brought in. We didn't get all of our POW's back from Korea. Don't you know that some of them are in ["misery, and\...bitter in soul?"]{.class_3762}

"[Which longeth for death, but it cometh not\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 21). Note that, again, this is prophecy as well as history. In Revelation 9:6, during the Great Tribulation, men seek for death and cannot die. ["And dig for it more than for hid treasures"]{.class_3762} (vs. 21). Well, Job's "digging" did not amount to driving the potsherd through his throat or heart, nor did it come to slashing his wrists. Job's desire is purely that God will finish quickly what He evidently had begun---Job's complete destruction.

However, there have been people who have fired a 16-gauge shotgun into their mouths and lived ten years afterward with nothing to show for their "suicide" but \$30,000 worth of hospital bills. Karl Menninger's works are filled with cases where people hug hot stoves, jump down chimneys to suffocate themselves, strangle themselves with barbed wire, choke to death on swallowed underwear, leap into boiling vats of oil or vats of animals blood, etc. Some make it, and some don't. The classic case is where the "suicide" ties a rope to his neck and, after soaking himself with gasoline, he mounts a tree over a river. He ties the rope to the limb, draws a pistol, and lights his cigarette lighter as he jumps off the limb! (The cunning scheme in all of this is to insure death; if he misses with the pistol, he will burn up, but should the gasoline fail to ignite, he will still hang to death.) Unfortunately (if you look at it from Job's ash heap), he shoots the rope in two with the pistol. Then he falls (blazing!) into the river, and the water puts the fire out. Climbing up the bank, the "suicide" is heard to mutter: "Man, if I hadn't learned how to swim, I would like to have [drowned]{.class_3759}."

Of a cantankerous wife who was always threatening suicide, a spouse said, "Promises! Promises! All I get is promises!"

"[Glad, when they can find the grave"]{.class_3760} (vs. 22).
