To really get a grasp of the verse, one should put all of the Greek and Hebrew lexicons on the shelf (after carefully closing the [New Bible Commentary]{.class_3761}, [Wycliffe Commentary]{.class_3761}, and the works by Williams, Dummelow, Adam Clarke, and John Peter Lange), and read the last three chapters of [All Quiet on the Western Front]{.class_3761}, by Erich Maria Remarque. When Remarque says that the expression on Paul Baumer's dead face indicated that "he was glad the end had come," you can believe it. The only way out of combat infantry is a grave or a stretcher (see [The Sure Word of Prophecy]{.class_3761}, Chap. 1--3).

"[Whom God hath hedged in?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 23). (See Jer. 20:8.) This time the ["hedge"]{.class_3762} is not a hedge of prosperity as in Job 1:10; this time the hedge is a ["hedge of thorns"]{.class_3762} (Prov. 15:19, 22:5; Isa. 5:6, 7:23, 27:4; and Jer. 12:13), and these thorns are associated with men burning in Hell (Heb. 6:8; 2 Sam. 23:56). Job sighs before he eats, instead of asking a blessing on the food (vs. 24), and then, instead of eating, he moans (Psa. 42:3). The ["roaring"]{.class_3762} of verse 24 is\
to be taken literally in spite of the mass of conservative scholarship that objects to it. The Lion's roar of Psalm 22:1 and 32:3 was the Lion of the Tribe of Judah in distress. And lest any twentieth-century allegorizer, like Origen (note his work on verse 24 in Clarke's Commentary, p. 34), tends to reduce the word ["roaring"]{.class_3762} to "groans" or "groaning," let him spend some time with Eugene Kogon's scholarly work ([The Theory and Practice of Hell]{.class_3761}) and not waste time with the corrupt [ASV ]{.class_3761}of 1901, the ["Living" Bible]{.class_3761} of 1971, the Lockman Foundation, or the "Tyndale" House Publishers. When prisioners who escaped from Buchenwald were recaptured, they were put into a 4 x 4 foot box that had chickenwire on three sides of it. The floor and roof of the box were studded with ten penny nails---points up. After two days in their boxes, the cries of the hapless victims became animal sounds. And don't deceive yourself or anyone else about the wording of the [King James ]{.class_3761}text: above all, don't let some Girl Scout revision committee substitute their watered down interpretations for the Infallible Truth of God Almighty.

"[For the thing which I greatly feared is come upon me" ]{.class_3760}(vs. 25), shows that Job had had a premonition all along about the tragic possibilities of life. Not once in the heights of Job's prosperity did he ever forget that God was the supreme Judge, and that God [could ]{.class_3761}dole out a harsh sentence (see 31:1--10 and comments). Job had a negative view of life as well as a positive view, and he was not looking at the "silver lining" only when he faced tomorrow. ["I was not in safety, neither had I rest, neither was I quiet; yet trouble came"]{.class_3762} (vs. 26). The meaning is all too clear. One might expect trouble to come to a man settled down "at ease in Zion" (Dan. 4:29--32), for this would be a sort of system of "balances" (Ecc. 7:14) to even things up. But Job never ceased to be on the alert (see 1:5 and comments) even in his ["nest"]{.class_3762} (29:2--3), and he never was presumptuous enough to state that he had "arrived" or was beyond the chastening hand of God. Trouble is usually preceded by a little backsliding indulgence, but in Job's case, it came upon him in the midst of alert watchfulness---for his own family as well as his own moral condition. And further, Job was not at rest nor quiet when he was smitten with his diseases, since he had already lost his family and property. Mr. Good and Adam Clarke cannot understand the first thing about either verse (vss. 25--26), so Clarke invents his own Bible and suggests that three question marks are to be placed in verse 26. The corrupt [ASV ]{.class_3761}of 1901 (recommended by the college faculty of Bethel, St. Paul, Heston, Grace Bible Institute, Bryan College, San Jose, Toccoa Falls, Vancouver, Mid-South, Faith Bible College, Lancaster, Eugene, Fort Wayne, Graham, Indiana, and Taylor University) has changed the tense (["was\...had"]{.class_3762}) to "am," since none of the men on that committee---including Robertson and Torrey---could grasp the verse.

Taylor (the ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759}), anxious to follow the crowd but fearing he will not be "paraphrasing" if he does, rewrites the verse as "I was not fat and lazy\...."(!) The [New English Bible ]{.class_3759}alters the tense, while Peake, Duhm, and Budde suggest the usual alterations which are not worth wasting time with. The fable that the verse referred only to the "beginning of his troubles" (Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown) is one more case of wretched bushwhacking, trying to cover up the fact that the majority of Conservative "brush-beaters" forgot to compare Scripture with Scripture before they undertook the correcting of God Almighty.

In closing our chapter, we may take note of the varied ways in which the Septuagint manuscripts (used in the ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759}) have mangled the truth.

In verse 8, somebody is attacking "the great whale." (A unique reading, if you ever got it.) A "star" has been inserted at the end of verse 9. "Who gloried in their swords" has been added to verse 14. "Burnt out the fury of rage" has been added to verse 17. "Men of old time" has been substituted for ["prisoners"]{.class_3760} in verse 18. "Death is rest to such a man" has been added to verse 23, and the entire thought of the verse has been obliterated. There are five to ten minor changes besides these, but these are enough to give you an idea of the source of the so-called "reliable" translations recommended by Weniger, Schuyler, Bob Jones III, and the faculty members at Hyles-Anderson, Lynchburg, Miami, Grace, Hope, Gloom, Faith, Charity, Wrath, Joy, Peace, and Apostasy. You see, these "reliable translations" are all based on Sinaiticus and Vaticanus; Sinaiticus and Vaticanus are the manuscripts which are quoted by the Scholars Union, from Origen's Fifth Column Old Testament.

[]{#IMh58iSMTFKKU0J9qyaeHw477.xhtml}

CHAPTER FOUR

4:1 Then Eliphaz the Temanite answered and said,

2 If we assay to commune with thee, wilt thou be grieved? but who can withhold himself from speaking?

3 Behold, thou hast instructed many, and thou hast strengthened the weak hands.

4 Thy words have upholden him that was falling, and thou hast strengthened the feeble knees.

5 But now it is come upon thee, and thou faintest; it toucheth thee, and thou art troubled.

The man who attempts to "comfort" Job (see 2:11) is a Temanite, a native of Edom. He is supposed to be the mildest of Job's accusers, and in his first speech he claims to have had a revelation from God. This revelation matches Elihu's conclusions (Job 35--37) and also God's own words to Job in Job 40--42. However, Eliphaz could hardly be called a "comforter," in any sense, in view of verses 7, 8, and Job 5:1, 4, and 5. He may have been mild, but in Job's position the slightest mention of blame or wrong-doing will unleash a thousand volts of resentment and bitterness.

The gist of the first five verses is: You are probably going to be grieved if we attempt to communicate with you (vs. 2), but we can't sit here for another week with our mouths shut; we have to speak (vs. 2). You have helped people out and instructed them when they were in trouble (vs. 3)--- "physician, heal thyself"---and you've comforted people with words (vs. 4), but now that trouble has hit you---"physician, heal thyself"---how come you're fainting? Why are you troubled?

Now there is much truth in what Eliphaz says; the trouble is, this is the wrong time to say it (see 1:21) even if you think it. Notice how closely it resembles: ["He saved others; Himself he cannot save"]{.class_3760} (Matt. 27:42).

The contradiction is a universal truth. Advice is always easier to give than to take. It is one thing to claim Romans 8:28 on a clear day in June when you and all your family are in good health and you are out fishing in a new boat; it is another thing to claim it over the grave of a seven-year-old son or daughter. We preachers who major in "talk" (1 Cor. 1:21) had better learn how to tread lightly on a broken heart, and if our foot has to fall like a jackboot on anyone, let it fall on the proud, self-righteous, Bible-perverting "celebrities" of our day who go through life thinking they are doing us a favor by being here.

I discussed the matter of pain and sleepless nights with a good friend of mine, John Hall, who was, and is, a wheelchair case. We talked about drugs and pills, and the orders given under 1 Corinthians 6:12. But it is one thing for a [healthy man ]{.class_3759}(who can still play tennis, water polo, soccer, football, volleyball, and baseball) to talk about "habit forming drugs," and it is quite another thing for a man to talk about them who is paralyzed from the waist down and can't sleep five hours a night because of the pain. Sure, you have the answer preacher, sure! Sure, you have the Book with all the answers, preacher, but be careful how you dish it out to Job's "kinfolk." What could be more ridiculous than these great tax-exempt, corporative, multi- million dollar, religious enterprises talking about [ministering ]{.class_3759}to people when many of the people they minister to live in trailers and sweat out gas, light, and phone bills every month? What would you think of me if I undertook to tell Jack Hyles how to run a Sunday School or Billy Graham how to conduct a union campaign? Wouldn't that be a riot? Wouldn't it be a boffer if I tried to tell John R. Rice how to print a newspaper? Or how about me telling Bob Jones Jr. how to operate a Christian university? Wouldn't that be Endsville? Then what do you suppose we think of the Greek and Hebrew scholars who try to correct our Bible or our teaching with their ridiculous textbooks by Nutz, Davis, Bell, Panosian, Custer (Bob Jones University), Gesenius, A. T. Robertson, Keil, Adam Clarke, Zacharopoulos, Albright, Heelios, Sideris, and J. G. Machen? Do you think that those of us who believe the Book, and spend 90 percent of our time teaching it and preaching it, need some bland promotional executive or some watered-down backslider correcting our work? [Get back in the shallow water, sonny, the crabs will get you.]{.class_3759}

4:6 Is not this thy fear, thy confidence, thy hope, and the uprightness of thy ways?

7 Remember, I pray thee, who ever perished, being innocent? or where were the righteous cut off?

8 Even as I have seen, they that plow iniquity, and sow wickedness, reap the same.

9 By the blast of God they perish, and by the breath of his nostrils are they consumed.

10 The roaring of the lion, and the voice of the fierce lion, and the teeth of the young lions, are broken.

11 The old lion perisheth for lack of prey, and the stout lion's whelps are scattered abroad.

Eliphaz continues, but before he runs another verse Jamieson, Fausset and Brown, Clarke, the [Living Bible ]{.class_3759}(with the [ASV, NASV, RSV, NRSV]{.class_3759}), and the Dead Corpse, tear up verse 6. As the passage stood, Job's "fear" is stated in verse 8: ["Even as I have seen, that they that sow iniquity, and sow wickedness, reap the same."]{.class_3760} Job's hope and confidence and "uprightness" (vs. 6) clearly lay in verse 7: ["Remember I pray thee, who ever perished, being innocent or where were the righteous cut off?"]{.class_3760} Unable to sort out this simple construction, Adam Clarke has torn up the passage and made it read: "Where is your fear of God, etc." And the [Living Bible ]{.class_3759}(with the [ASV]{.class_3759}) has inserted this interpretation with no manuscript authority whatsoever. The [New English Bible ]{.class_3759}goes one better and says: "Is your [religion ]{.class_3759}no comfort to you?" But the[ "this" ]{.class_3760}of verse 6 (which is also interpolated) is a reference to verse 7 and 8 in the context.

Now, this peculiar "jamming" of the airwaves comes from the liberal tendencies of modern, Conservative scholars to make "the fear of God" refer to a "reverential trust" (see any [Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_3759}). Since we have dealt with this matter at length under Proverbs 1:7 in our Commentary, we will not review the ground again. Job was afraid of God and sin (Job 23:15) because he knew about the wages of sin (4:8 in the context). His hope and confidence was in reaping good by sowing good (31:1--20 and comments), and Eliphaz knew this; hence, ["Is not this thy fear, thy confidence, thy hope\...etc."]{.class_3760} And then Eliphaz proceeds to name it. Observe exactly the same thing in Acts 2:16, where Peter says ["This is that\..."]{.class_3760} and then names what Joel said.

The drift of Eliphaz's comfort is: "Weren't you afraid that if you did wrong you'd get it in the neck? Weren't you trusting your good works to bring you blessings and keep you alive? Well ["Even as I have seen" ]{.class_3760}(vs. 8) that those two beliefs of yours are true, and you've just temporarily forgotten them. After all, if you were innocent (vs. 7), God would bring you out fine" (Job 5:17--27). That is exactly how it comes out of Eliphaz's mouth, and the ridiculous meddling with the text done by the "authorities" is about as Biblical as Zodhiates' exposition of the book of James ([The Behavior of Belief]{.class_3759}, pp 305, 233, 9, 18, 37, 61, 67, 241, 258, etc.).

"[By the blast of God they perish"]{.class_3760} (vs. 9) lands us right into 2 Thessalonians 2:8 and Isaiah 11:4---back in the Tribulation. This makes twelve references to Bible prophecy in four chapters; so, when one reads Dr. Payne's [Encyclopedia of "Prophecy," ]{.class_3761}one must never forget that nearly eighty percent of the "Encyclopedia" on Job is missing.

"[The roaring of the lion\...fierce lion\...young lions\...old lions\...stout lion's whelp"]{.class_3760} (vss. 10--11). Each lion is a different Hebrew word, and a literal rendering would give: "to tear off, to be black or dark, a young lion, to knead or kneel upon, and to suckle with milk." In view of the fact that Satan and the Lord Jesus Christ are both spoken of as "lions" and in view of the fact that the world empire preceding the BEAR and LEOPARD empires (Dan. 7:4) is a lion, the words should be taken to heart. The ["young lions"]{.class_3762} of Ezekiel 38:12--13 have been identified by Pember, Larkin, and Scofield (for the benefit of modern Premillennialists) as the [English-speaking countries]{.class_3761}. This is all the more significant when we remember Scofield's error (made with the consent of Gabelein, Robertson, English, Torrey, Pentecost, Newell, Darby, Baxter, Lehman, Feinberg, et al.) about the lion of Daniel chapter 7. The winged lion of Daniel 7:4 is [not ]{.class_3761}Bablylon, for all of the beasts of Daniel 7 come up [after ]{.class_3761}the last king of Babylon is dead (Dan. 7:1). Somewhere in the Bible, the old lion (England or Germany) and her off-spring (the U.S.A., Canada, Australia, Scotland, Ireland, etc.) are lurking, and you can be certain that when they are identified, they will not match the pussycats that G. T. Armstrong has been playing with the last thirty years.

Eliphaz's sudden slip into talk about lions hunting for food is not too much in keeping with the context, although one may say that these lions represent the wicked of verse 8 whom God will "consume." But there are too many lions in the context to ignore; Taylor's alterations ([TLB]{.class_3759}) of verse 9, to get rid of the Second Coming references (2 Thess. 2:8), doesn't help things a bit. (Nice try.) The [Pulpit Commentary ]{.class_3759}also erases this reference by altering ["breath"]{.class_3760} to "blast." (Another nice try.) Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown don't like the construction of verse 10, so they add "silenced" to the verse to shut up the voice of the ["roaring."]{.class_3760} But the voices are broken, and the whole ungodly mess is broken (Dan. 2:35). They [are ]{.class_3759}["scattered abroad"]{.class_3760} (vs. 11) in Daniel 2:35, and they [do ]{.class_3759}["perish" ]{.class_3760}(vs. 11), according to Malachi 4:1--4. (Never abandon the [King James ]{.class_3759}text simply because one hundred percent of the "recognized scholars" object to it. After all, it is quite able to object to them in the strongest terms possible: Hebrews 4:12--13.)

4:12 Now a thing was secretly brought to me, and mine ear received a little thereof.

13 In thoughts from the visions of the night, when deep sleep falleth on men,

14 Fear came upon me, and trembling, which made all my bones to shake.

15 Then a spirit passed before my face; the hair of my flesh stood up:

16 It stood still, but I could not discern the form thereof: an image was before mine eyes, there was silence, and I heard a voice, saying,

17 Shall mortal man be more just than God? shall a man be more pure than his maker?

18 Behold, he put no trust in his servants; and his angels be charged with folly:

19 How much less in them that dwell in houses of clay, whose foundation is in the dust, which are crushed before the moth?

20 They are destroyed from morning to evening: they perish for ever without any regarding it.

21 Doth not their excellency which is in them go away? they die, even without wisdom.

Now everything that Eliphaz says is true, and we may not doubt that the Lord did speak to him in a dream (see Num. 12:6). God often revealed himself to men in this manner (Gen. 20:6) before His Bible was written. Furthermore, the speech delivered by the ["spirit"]{.class_3760} (vs. 15) is in perfect agreement with the body of revelation in either Testament. If Eliphaz had stopped at verse 21 and hugged Job---or at least patted his head---many a vilification which followed would never have been uttered. But Eliphaz goes on with 5:1--4, plainly classifying his victim (Job) as a foolish, silly, and cursed sinner. Some comfort! Verse 13 will match Genesis 15:12, and again, we are dealing with Tribulation material. ["Then a spirit passed before my face"]{.class_3760} (vs. 15). The angels are ["ministering spirits"]{.class_3760} (Heb. 1:14); so, this is one of them, for he has a ["form"]{.class_3760} and an ["image"]{.class_3760} (vs. 16). His short sermonette is aimed at those made ["a little lower than the angels"]{.class_3760}---those who ["dwell in houses of clay"]{.class_3760} (vs. 19).

They are ["crushed before the moth"]{.class_3760} (vs. 19). (See Job 27:18 and Psalm 39:11.) That is, they rot away like a piece of old cloth that a moth chews (Isa. 51:6). They are ["destroyed from morning to evening"]{.class_3760} (vs. 20); that is, they die like flies, and hundreds of them die every day, at all hours of the day or night. Their ["excellency" ]{.class_3760}goes away (vs. 21) according to Psalm 49:12, 16, 17, and 20, and they ["die, even without wisdom"]{.class_3760} (vs. 21), exactly like David said in the same Psalm (Psa. 49:20); they are ["like the beasts that perish"]{.class_3760} (Psa. 49:20; Eccl. 3:18). Since their ["foundation is in the dust"]{.class_3760} (vs. 19), they must return to it (Eccl. 3:20); for, from ["dust thou art, and unto dust thou shalt return"]{.class_3760} (Gen. 3:19).

Can such a feeble, fickle, frail, fragile, fluttering, fallible spark in the wind ["be more pure than his maker?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 17). Not on your life.

"[Behold, he put no trust in his servants; and his angels he charged with folly" ]{.class_3760}(vs. 18). Notice that the reference is the first real reference which indicates the time of Job's troubles. Other references which mark out this pre-deluge period (["in the days of Noah"]{.class_3762}) are 9:8, 15:19, 21:12, 22:15--16, and several others. The themes that keep reoccurring in the conversations between Job and his three friends are not the themes of Pharaoh and the Exodus (see Clarke's theories) or the Tower of Babel; [they are the flood and the original creation.]{.class_3761}

God would not trust Moses, David, Jude, James, John, or Paul (His "servants") completely. The one warning which God placed "smack-dab" in the middle of His Book---the exact middle (Psa. 118:8)---was a warning not to put confidence in man. ["Mortal man"]{.class_3760} (vs. 17) can never be as just as God, let alone ["more just than God." ]{.class_3760}God [did ]{.class_3759}charge His angels with folly (vs. 18); these matters, again, deal with the Second Advent, where one third of the angels in Heaven will defect to Satan (Rev. 12:4, 10). To fool gullible people like Dr. Barton Payne, John Milton constructed a fable where Revelation 12:9 took place before Genesis 2. (Some more "nice work.") The angels are not only charged with folly, they [commit ]{.class_3759}folly (Jude 7; 2 Pet. 2:4); so ["how much less"]{.class_3760} will God trust those who ["dwell in houses of clay?"]{.class_3760}

The passage is as clear as a June sky in perfect flying weather. Nothing in it is obscure or difficult, and it is only the destructive imaginations of Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown, Lange, Clarke, Peace, Dillmann, Schlotmann, Gesenius, Taylor, Kline, and Heavenor that gums up the works. The Hebrew text reads as the [AV ]{.class_3759}of 1611.

1\. The so-called ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} has not even attempted to paraphrase verse 21. Instead, Kenneth Taylor has invented a reading about a "candle."

2\. The [Septuagint ]{.class_3759}"blows upon them" in verse 21, and the [New English Bible ]{.class_3759}invents a phrase found in no Greek or Hebrew manuscript.

3\. Maurer thinks the man is crushed like a moth, although the text does not say this.

4\. The [ASV ]{.class_3759}of 1901 says: "Is not their tentcord plucked up within them?" Heavenor ([The New Bible Commentary]{.class_3759}) uses the same reading which is the same old rehash of the [ASV]{.class_3759}.

5\. Michaelis thinks a cord of a tabernacle has become "unstrung" in verse 21, but Umbreit thinks a man has a "slack bowstring."

6\. There was no ["form"]{.class_3760} in verse 16, according to Meyer and Bickell, but, of course, they were reading from the godless Septuagint put out by Origen and Eusebius when they decided to make a liar out of God. A lot of Christians do that these days.

7\. The [Living Bible ]{.class_3759}has made the ["servants"]{.class_3760} and ["angels"]{.class_3760} of verse 18 identical, although the words are two completely different words in Hebrew ("ebed" and "malach"). But this is the trouble that the modern "Christian" gets into for taking these "scholars" at face value (reputation-wise) when they talk about "paraphrases." Job 4:18 in the ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} is NOT a paraphrase; it is the private conjecture of Mr. Taylor.

"[They die, even without wisdom"]{.class_3760} (vs. 21), and the statement is true. If it is true that ["The fear of the Lord\...that is wisdom"]{.class_3762} (Job 28:28), the majority of the human race has never attained wisdom, especially the highly [educated ]{.class_3761}portion of the human race. If there is one thing that higher education does not teach, it is ["the fear of the Lord\...that is wisdom."]{.class_3762} And even if this is not the exact meaning of the text, there is one thing certain: No man can get enough wisdom to keep from dying. They all die.

Furthermore, most of us die without learning the lessons that our life trials were supposed to teach us. When one considers that men have been dying for nearly six thousand years of recorded history and killing each other at a rate of better than five hundred a day (exclusive of thirty-seven major wars!), it would appear that ["they die, even without wisdom."]{.class_3760} There is no record of any generation being able to pass on to any other generation any plan that would stop poverty, persecution, religious differences, war, sin, disease, death, and starvation. The superman who will profess to have this plan (Rev. 13:1--8) will be followed by war, famine, death, and Hell (Rev. 6:1--8).

[]{#IMh58iSMTFKKU0J9qyaeHw530.xhtml}

CHAPTER 5

5:1 Call now, if there be any that will answer thee; and to which of the saints wilt thou turn?

2 For wrath killeth the foolish man, and envy slayeth the silly one.

3 I have seen the foolish taking root: but suddenly I cursed his habitation.

4 His children are far from safety, and they are crushed in the gate, neither is there any to deliver them.

5 Whose harvest the hungry eateth up, and taketh it even out of the thorns, and the robber swalloweth up their substance.

And now Eliphaz mixes the salt in with the vinegar and applies it deftly to Job's open sores. First, he challenges Job to get out his rosary and say a few "Hail Marys" (vs. 1) and see if "blessed Joseph" or "blessed John the Baptist" will get him out of his pickle. (Observe the strange similitude between Eliphaz's mockery and that of the chief priests in Mark 15:35.) ["And to which of the saints will thou turn?"]{.class_3760} Abraham, Noah, Enoch? ["Wrath killeth\...and envy slayeth"]{.class_3760} (vs. 2), so i[f Job is wishing for death, it is evidence that he is ]{.class_3760}"foolish" (vs. 2) and ["silly"]{.class_3760} (vs. 2). (How "comforting" can one get!)

The ["foolish taking root"]{.class_3760} (vs. 3) is so common a thing that everyone has seen it. But the root of the foolish (Mal. 4:1) will be [plucked up ]{.class_3759}(Prov. 2:22), and he will lose his habitation (18:14--19); the reference is not only aimed at Job but at the Antichrist himself (see Psa. 52).

The remark about the foolish man's children (vs. 4) is just about as hard a blow as anyone ever hit Job with since his wife said,[ "curse God and die."]{.class_3760} Job's children, by virtue of what took place, were ["far from safety"]{.class_3760} and were ["crushed"]{.class_3760} (1:19); neither could anyone ["deliver them"]{.class_3760} (vs. 4). Whether he intended it or not, Eliphaz has just called Job's habitation "cursed" and has called Job a silly, foolish man. Verse 5 only adds insult to injury, for it was "robbers" (vs. 5) who got Job's substance (1:15)! Some comforter.

So Eliphaz has done little more than heap coals of fire on Job's discomfort; his advice is about as soothing as sulphuric acid poured into an open wound. Here, as elsewhere, the destructive critics of the [Authorized Text ]{.class_3759}cannot believe or receive the words of truth.

The corrupt [New Scofield Reference Bible ]{.class_3759}has thrown the Authorized reading into the margin by verse 1 (["saints"]{.class_3760}) and has substituted "holy ones." After doing this, the committee turned right around and translated the same word (Hebrew: ["qadosh"]{.class_3759}) as "saints" in Hosea 11:12. Not to be outdone in inconsistency---while complaining about the "inconsistent translations" of the [AV]{.class_3759}---The ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} has translated the word as "gods" here and then as "the Holy One" in Hosea 11:12 and then as "angels" in Psalm 89:7. (And you understand, this is all done with the most "godly" piety and "dedicated devotion," while talking about the [King James Bible ]{.class_3759}not translating "Spirit" the same way every time or "Hades" the same way every time. My, aren't we sanctified!)

The [New English Bible ]{.class_3759}has the children "brow beaten in court" for ["crushed in the gate"]{.class_3760} (vs. 4), but evidently Dodd forgot that you can be literally crushed in a literal gate (2 Kings 7:17), if you don't take such expressions as ["the windows of heaven"]{.class_3760} (Gen. 7:11) literally---see 2 Kings 7:2 and 17--20).

"[The hungry eateth up, and taketh it even out of the thorns\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 5) shows a gleaning has taken place which leaves absolutely nothing (Judg. 6:4, 6). The hungry robber even goes into the "sticker bushes" to pick up pieces of grain or fruit that have been dropped there.

The [Septuagint ]{.class_3759}has mistranslated all of the verbs in verse 4. The saints should be "angels" according to Heavenor, and a strong man is seizing something from the "panniers" ([NEB]{.class_3759}) in verse 5. (That is one of the "new translations" in the "living language" of the etc.) Hitzig, Peake, Duhm, Budde, and Olshausen suggest about ten changes for the first five verses, but I am sure the Devil could suggest a greater number than that.

5:6 Although affliction cometh not forth of the dust, neither doth trouble spring out of the ground;

7 Yet man is born unto trouble, as the sparks fly upward.

Verse 7 is one of the great verses in the book of Job, and later (7:7) we will put these kind of verses into a bundle so that a man can see what "life" really is, according to the [Author of Life]{.class_3759}. There are twelve definitions of "life" in the [Book of Job]{.class_3759}, and anyone of them is as authoritative as the combined works of all of the ancient and modern philosophers.

"[Affliction"]{.class_3760} does not come from the dust (vs. 6), nor does ["trouble spring out of the ground"]{.class_3762} (vs. 6); for if all you had to contend with was the sun, the moon, the rivers, the mountains, the soil, the trees, the animals, and the verdure, you could live without sickness (as Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob did.) till you gradually "wore out." As Dave Gardner has said: "The only thing wrong with the country is the city." Dust and the ground may be responsible for your eventual death since they are cursed (Gen. 3:17), but they are not the source of affliction and trouble; these two adversities have their roots in human nature (James 4:1--4; 1 Tim. 6:10) and the spirit world (Eph. 6:10--16).

Man is ["born unto trouble"]{.class_3760} (vs. 7) because he is born into a fallen creation (Gen. 3) that is moving away from its Creator as fast as it can move (2 Tim. 3:1--5). The creature in it is ["subject to vanity"]{.class_3760} (Rom. 8:20). The whole creation groans and travails in pain (Rom. 8:22), and when a man comes into this system, he comes into a system that is under the control of the ["god of this world"]{.class_3760} (2 Cor. 4:4)---into whose hands its kingdoms have been delivered (Luke 4:6). With your nature against you, the world system against you, and Satan against you (1 John 5:19), you are ["born unto trouble as the sparks fly upward"]{.class_3760} (vs. 7). (As they used to say at Buchenwald and Treblinka: "The only way out is UP THE STACK.")

Man is of ["few days, and full of trouble"]{.class_3760} (14:1), and some of those troubles will cross your life before you are two years old, even if you are a middle-class American. You will have affliction and trouble through spankings and cuts, spilled milk, bruised knees, bumped heads, pinched fingers, and spanked "bottoms" before you are [three]{.class_3759}. You will suffer ridicule, ostracization, bad grades, to "bed without meals," fist fights, and no lunch money before you are [ten]{.class_3759}. You will be troubled with girl problems, date problems, teacher problems, unpopularity, homework, lack of school supplies, dirty and torn clothes, and inflation before you are [twenty]{.class_3759}. You will put up with hospital bills, car wrecks, parking tickets, busted water mains, stopped-up commodes, unburned trash, unemployment, phone and light bills, and domestic problems before you are [thirty]{.class_3759}; and many of you will suffer gun wounds, knife wounds, doctor bills, lawyer bills, undertaker expenses, and judgment before you are [forty]{.class_3759}.

You will learn about life by living it, and where you don't really live it (see 2 Cor. 1:4), you will be unable to minister to anyone who does. The great "isolated, multimillion dollar, dedicated, separated, tax-exempt, air-conditioned SPREAD" that accompanies some "ministries" is a foolproof sign that someone can no longer minister to anyone. I have never known a great preacher in America---Beauchamp Vick, J. Harold Smith, John Rawlings, Maze Jackson, Dallas Billington, B. R. Lakin, Frank Norris, Harold Sitler, Bob Jones Sr., or Mordecai Ham, for example---who did not have to go through "hell on wheels" before God made him a "great" preacher. And what many a religious politician and professional promoter inherits these days, he got by avoiding Biblical issues. ["Man is born unto trouble as the sparks fly upward,"]{.class_3760} and if you don't get your share of it, it is because you are living in an ivory palace and pretending you are something when you are nothing (1 Cor. 8:2).

1\. [Problems ]{.class_3759}are sent our way to make us think.

2\. [Criticism ]{.class_3759}comes our way to get us to check on ourselves.

3\. [Pain ]{.class_3759}comes our way to teach us patience.

4\. [Sorrow ]{.class_3759}pays us a visit to make us sympathetic.

5\. [Disappointments ]{.class_3759}come our way to keep us humble.

6\. Fear overwhelms us to engender righteousness.

7\. And [difficulties ]{.class_3759}come one after another to teach us to depend on God and ["have no confidence in the flesh"]{.class_3760} (Phil. 3:3).

"[Man is born unto trouble."]{.class_3760}

A boy, trying out a new pair of ice skates, fell down on the ice eight times in a row. An amused bystander advised him to quit trying and go home. The boy got manfully to his feet and said with quivering lips: "I didn't buy these new skates to [quit]{.class_3759}, but to learn to skate!" And he went on learning.

I don't know how many brush strokes I make when I paint a portrait or a landscape; I am sure it is well over 2,000. Did you ever think how many times a sculptor has to trouble the marble with his chisel before he gets the image he wants? We can't choose our troubles or they would not be real "troubles." A snappy husband, a griping wife, no children in the marriage, an arthritic limb, etc., are troubles which are sent to us by the One who knows what will trouble us and what will not.

The Lord Jesus Christ knows the sweat, pain, and tears of sorrow (Isa. 53:3--9), and He "feels them" (Heb. 4:15). He knows the sweat of the miner, the burden of the longshoreman, the boredom of shift work, the panic of big business, the strain of the truck driver, and the humdrum routine of housecleaning. Our disappointments are His "appointments," and we cannot escape tribulation (John 16:33) even though we are saved. Sometimes it will take prayer, grace, a sense of humor, and the support of friends to get us through our troubles, but we will save ourselves a lot of "sweat" if we avoid singing the old refrain "Land me safe on Canaan's shore" as "Land MY SAFE on Canaan's shore." We may lose plenty on the way home, and we are not home yet. Until we get there, our lot is the lot of every unsaved man on the face of this earth---at least where "trouble" is concerned. Paul had more "trouble" in ten years of ministry (Acts 9--20) than the average man has in a lifetime; if you don't believe it read 2 Corinthians 11.

When the spark flies off the ember or log, it goes up if there is enough of a blaze to picture life at all. Even though troubles do not sprout out of the soil (like flowers, grass, and weeds), man sprouts out into a blazing fire (1 Pet. 4:12) and finds himself carried away with smoke, flame, and wind before he can get his senses about him. (As a mother chicken said to the baby chick when it cracked open its shell: "Now look what you've done!")

The ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} makes no attempt to paraphrase verse 6 at all, but invents a reading that is completely divorced from the sense of the passage. There are no Hebrew manuscripts in any collection of manuscripts that even faintly resemble the reading of the so-called ["Living" Bible. ]{.class_3759}Umbreit substitutes "birds of prey" for ["sparks"]{.class_3760}; Gesenius and Maurer substitute "sons of lightning," and the preposterous Septuagint joins the ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} in "way out" improvisations by adding: "even so the vulture's young seek high places." Bullinger would use "sons of flame" for ["sparks,"]{.class_3760} and the Roman Catholic [Vulgate ]{.class_3759}has made verse 1 into a command (!) so you will have to turn to the "saints."

Haven't you thought it rather odd, at times, that there are five classes of sinners who cannot stand to accept the [AV ]{.class_3759}1611 as written? Look at them:

1\. Roman Catholics (see [Bible Believer's Commentaries on Matthew, Acts,]{.class_3759} and [Genesis]{.class_3759}).

2\. [Atheists ]{.class_3759}(M. M. O'Hare, Bertrand Russell, et al.).

3\. [Satan and demons]{.class_3759} (see Gen. 3:1 and John 8:44--47).

4\. [Dead Orthodox Conservatives ]{.class_3759}(Peake, Rendall, Robertson, Machen, et al.).

5\. [Fundamentalists ]{.class_3759}(English, Zodhiates, Feinberg, Sumner, Rice, et al.).

My, my, what a "communion between light and darkness."

5:8 I would seek unto God, and unto God would I commit my cause:

9 Which doeth great things and unsearchable; marvellous things without number:

10 Who giveth rain upon the earth, and sendeth waters upon the fields:

11 To set up on high those that be low; that those which mourn may be exalted to safety.

12 He disappointeth the devices of the crafty, so that their hands cannot perform their enterprise.

13 He taketh the wise in their own craftiness: and the counsel of the froward is carried headlong.

14 They meet with darkness in the daytime, and grope in the noonday as in the night.

15 But he saveth the poor from the sword, from their mouth, and from the hand of the mighty.

16 So the poor hath hope, and iniquity stoppeth her mouth.

Job has evidently just turned his head away from Eliphaz, or at least dropped his eyes to the ground. Perhaps he has picked up his homemade backscratcher and gone back to tending his sores; at any rate, Eliphaz suddenly says: ["I would seek unto God, and unto God would I commit my cause"]{.class_3760} (vs. 8). Job is no longer listening. (And besides this, Job had already committed his own cause to God before Eliphaz showed up: Job 10:2 and 13:2).

What follows is intended to glorify God, and it does; but still couched in the middle of it (vss. 12--14) is the insinuation that Job is a crafty schemer whose sins have at last "caught up" with him.

Verse 9 is self-explanatory. Then there follows some "great things" which the Lord does:

1\. He ["giveth rain upon the earth"]{.class_3760} (vs. 10), in spite of the contemporary bunko about condensation and "evaporation" being responsible for it.

2\. He ["sendeth water upon the fields"]{.class_3760} (vs. 10) both by rains and floods.

3\. He sets up on high ["those that be low"]{.class_3760} (vs. 11) like He did Joseph, David, Paul, and Christ (see Phil. 2:9--10 and comments).

4\. He takes ["those which mourn"]{.class_3760} and exalts them to safety (vs. 11) as in the case of all three raptures (Old Testament saints, Church Age saints, and Tribulation saints).

5\. He disappoints ["the devices of the crafty"]{.class_3760} (vs. 12) as He did to Tobiah, Geshem, Absalom, Ahithophel, and BarJesus.

6\. He takes the wise ["in their own craftiness"]{.class_3760} (vs. 13) like He took Socrates, Aristotle, Sartre, G. B. Shaw, Erich Fromm, Heidegger, Tillich, Freud, Marx, Engels, Lenin, et al.

7\. He carries the counsel of the ["froward"]{.class_3760} headlong (vs. 13) like He did to Adolph Hitler, Mussolini, Dean Rusk, Michael Luther King Jr., John Dulles, Alger Hiss, the Kennedy family, the Fabian Society, and the United Nations.

8\. He ["saveth the poor"]{.class_3760} from: a) The [sword ]{.class_3759}of the froward. b) The ["hand of the mighty."]{.class_3760} c) The [results ]{.class_3759}of their own talking (vs. 15).

"[So the poor hath hope, and iniquity stoppeth her mouth"]{.class_3760} (vs. 16).

Well, iniquity is yet to open her mouth (Rev. 13, 17); so once again, we are on prophetic ground. The "meek" do not inherit the earth until the Advent, and any reader of Matthew chapter 24 and James chapter 5 can see immediately the great emphasis God places on the "poor" during the Tribulation; for, during that time you cannot get rich, or even comfortable, unless you "take the mark" (Rev. 13:15--18). Rich Christianity will end with Daniel's Seventieth Week (Psa. 41:1, 72:13, 22:16, 22, 28:5, 19:13, 10:2, 9, 12:5, 14:6, 35:10, 37:14, 70:5, 72:2, 86:1, etc.).

It is the apostle Paul who quotes verse 13 in his Epistle to the Corinthians (1 Cor. 3:19), and Paul was writing to a town less than seventy miles from the educational and cultural center of Europe and America---Athens. (See the extended comments made under Acts 17:16--31 in that Commentary.)

There are five prophetic references in the passage that have an application to the Second Advent. The ["rain"]{.class_3760} of verse 10 will match the rain that precedes the Advent in Psalm 68:9; Hosea 6:3; and 2 Samuel 23:4. And even though Biederwolf consulted more than four hundred scholars (Conservative, Liberal, Catholic, and Fundamental) before he wrote his [Second Coming Bible ]{.class_3759}(Baker Book House, 1972---formerly [The Millennium Bible]{.class_3759}), he could no more find the references than he could find a shark in Montana. (The [Second Coming Bible ]{.class_3759}says that it is "the complete text of [every ]{.class_3759}scripture passage concerned with the Second Coming of Christ\..." You couldn't find more fradulent advertising on commercial television.)

Those who are ["set up on high"]{.class_3760} are hauled up by the nape of the neck and the seat of the britches (Psa. 50:2--5; Isa. 26:19; Job 37:1--4). The words are intended to be taken doctrinally as well as spiritually.

When Biederwolf (with the aid of Alford, Aquila, Areteas, Augustine, Bengel, Driver, Dwight, Caspari, Cheyne, Bahr, Barnes, Davidson, Delitzsch, Didymus, Doddridge, Calmet, Calvin, Ellicott, Bez, Bossuet, Brightman, Baumgarten, and the other four hundred) tells you that Job 19:25 is the only verse in Job on the Second Coming, he is telling you exactly what any Atheist or Satanist would tell you if he said that the [Sutras ]{.class_3759}are the only books in print on brick masonry.

"[The crafty"]{.class_3760} and ["the wise"]{.class_3762} of verses 12 and 13 refer to Revelation 12--13 and 18:22, and the ["darkness in the daytime"]{.class_3762} is given in Acts 2 and Revelation 16:10. The ["sword"]{.class_3762} is mentioned so many times (Ezek. 21:3--28) in connection with the Second Advent (Rev. 19:15) that all we can say about Biederwolf (with Umbreit, Zwingli, Zoeckler, Zahn, Kuenen, Kurtz, Lange, Lachmann, Lactantius, Maurer, Maier, Mede, Mosheim, Neander, and the other four hundred) is that if he had believed the word of God instead of wasting his time with Olshausen, Oecumenius, Oosterzee, Origen, Osiander, Paulus, Pierce, Pelt, Rueckert, Steir, Salmond, Sayce, Photus, Pfeiderer, Pettingill, Porphyry, Riddle, Reinke, Tischendorf, and the other four hundred, he would have listed forty references on the Second Advent in the Book of Job before he got to Job 19:25. No, we are not bragging; no, we are not downgrading anybody; we are not "knocking" everyone but ourselves. No, we are not condemning the good, godly, "intelligent men" (to quote Dr. Rice as he blatantly lied about "questionable words" in the [Authorized Version]{.class_3761}: see [Sword of the Lord]{.class_3761}, October, 1975). We are simply saying that you and your friends and your supporters and your teachers and your school and your church are spiritually sick if you think that four hundred scholars (of any profession), with a combined total of eight thousand years of education, can teach you anything about a Biblical text where they have followed science, tradition, or philosophy. You're welcome. If you don't like it, [lump it]{.class_3761}.

The [ASV ]{.class_3759}(1901) has obliterated the Second Advent reference in verse 15 by making the ["sword"]{.class_3760} the mouth of the poor. The [Dead Bible ]{.class_3759}has changed the first person of verse 8 to the third person and destroyed the entire meaning of the verse; the [New English Bible ]{.class_3759}has destroyed the cross reference to 1 Corinthians 3:19 by changing verse 13, and there is no sword of any kind in the grossly corrupt [Septuagint]{.class_3759}, promoted by you-know-who.

5:17 Behold, happy is the man whom God correcteth: therefore despise not thou the chastening of the Almighty:

18 For he maketh sore, and bindeth up: he woundeth, and his hands make whole.

19 He shall deliver thee in six troubles: yea, in seven there shall no evil touch thee.

20 In famine he shall redeem thee from death: and in war from the power of the sword.

21 Thou shalt be hid from the scourge of the tongue: neither shalt thou be afraid of destruction when it cometh.

22 At destruction and famine thou shalt laugh: neither shalt thou be afraid of the beasts of the earth.

23 For thou shalt be in league with the stones of the field: and the the beasts of the field shall be at peace with thee.

24 And thou shalt know that thy tabernacle shall be in peace; and thou shalt visit thy habitation, and shalt not sin.

25 Thou shalt know also that thy seed shall be great, and thine offspring as the grass of the earth.

26 Thou shalt come to thy grave in a full age, like as a shock of corn cometh in in his season.

27 Lo this, we have searched it, so it is; hear it, and know thou it for thy good.

The opening words are quoted by the writer of the [Book of Hebrews]{.class_3759}, and they are also found in Proverbs 3:11. The references to the Tribulation and the Millennium in the section are so numerous we will have to condense our comments. Verse 18 will match Deuteronomy 32:39, where it deals with Israel; verse 20 will take place literally in Revelation 6:3--7. The "seven" of verse 19 is plainly Daniel's Seventieth Week of seven years (Dan. 9:17), and the seven things listed will be on earth in the Tribulation (vss. 20--23). Verses 24--26 will take place literally for Israel in the Millennium. (And you still think the [Second Coming Bible ]{.class_3759}is the "complete text of every scripture passage concerned with the Second Coming\..." do you? You do? Do you ever read the Bible? [Which Bible?]{.class_3759}")

"[Happy is the man whom God correcteth\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 17). Well it is true, and yet ["no chastening for the present seemeth to be joyous, but grieveous"]{.class_3762} (Heb. 12:11). No one is happy on the rack. As it turns out, Job is being chastened and disciplined---not [punished ]{.class_3761}and [condemned]{.class_3761}; however, you couldn't have convinced him, and don't ever try to convince anyone in his position. In Job's shoes, an ounce of sympathy and comfort is worth two thousand tons of "correct doctrine."

Well, God chastens us with the word, and that is the easiest "whipping" to take (2 Tim. 3:16). However, the last generation of Christians (2 Tim. 4:1--6) will not be able even to take that kind of a "tongue lashing." So, God may resort to the THORN (2 Cor. 12:7) to humble the great twentieth century, two-versions-a-year-Child-evangelism experience-sharing- money-grabber so that he will not think he is smart enough to correct the Holy Bible. If this doesn't work, God sometimes gets out His WHIP (Heb. 12:6) and "scourges" the believer (1 Cor. 11:32), and where all else fails the pruning KNIFE (John 15:2) will cut back the branches so that the fruit of the Spirit can be borne once more. We are not to faint under God's chastening---that is, get depressed and quit (Heb. 12)---and we are not to despise it---underestimate it and continue on in sin. (See the extended comments in the [Bible Believer's Commentary on Proverbs]{.class_3759}; Prov. 19:18, 13:24, 3:11, etc.)

"[For he maketh sore, and bindeth up: he woundeth, and his hands make whole"]{.class_3760} (vs. 18). (See Deut. 32:39.) All of this is true, and it all comes to pass as prophesied, although neither Job nor his tormentors (excuse me! "his comforters!") think that the words will apply to Job. The ["six"]{.class_3762} and ["seven"]{.class_3762} of verse 19 is the familiar thing we find in Proverbs 6:16 (see comments). This use is dismissed by Adam Clarke with a flippant "the numbers six and seven are put here for many," which is about as profound as saying: "the numbers 100, 200, 300, and 400 indicate several." Why any man would dismiss the six-and-seven system in the Bible, after it proves to be the scriptural measurement for the Second Advent, is beyond me. There are six thousand years between Genesis 2 and Revelation 20. The seven thousandth year must be a "sabbath of rest," or the dating system of Exodus 25; Leviticus 23, 25; Daniel 9; Genesis 1; John 1--2, 11; Hosea 6; Matthew 17; and Luke 9 is "out of whack." And before any of God's mathematical time tables go "out of whack," the sun, moon, and stars will alter their courses to keep up with the chronology (Josh. 10:13). (See the complete treatment in [Seven Sevens]{.class_3761}.) The seven "troubles" (not "many") of verse 19 are all listed:

1\. Famine.

2\. Death.

3\. War.

4\. The sword.

5\. The scourge of the tongue (Rev. 13:5).

6\. Destruction.

7\. The beasts of the earth (Rev. 9, 16).

Since [destruction ]{.class_3759}and [famine ]{.class_3759}have been combined in verse 22, we can have six or seven, depending upon the division. Notice exactly the same thing in Proverbs 6:16; seven items have been listed but two of them match---a ["lying tongue"]{.class_3760} (6:17) and a ["false witness that speaketh lies"]{.class_3760} (6:19).

It would pay Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown---who say the six and seven system is a "Hebrew idiom" to "call your attention to the importance of a thing"---and Adam Clarke to learn how to count on their fingers first before they begin to talk seriously about something they know nothing about. The Bible does not make statements on the Tribulation or the Advent that are couched in the "generalities" of the "Hebrew idiom." Those expressions are used by men who rejected the revelations on the Tribulation and the Advent. (If you can't expound the Bible, then just "idiom" your way out of it, you old "idiom.")

Verses 24--27 appear as a long green stretch of beautiful pasture in the Shenandoah Valley when compared with most of the discourse around it. The beautiful prospects of peace, life, health, prosperity, and restoration sound almost too good to be true---to Job at least---and yet it is for his ["good"]{.class_3760} (vs. 27). Isn't it a pity that such a glorious rainbow of hope could not be spread over the life of every man---from the cradle to the grave? And yet a full study of God's revelation shows that these utopian promises are only possible for three classes of people on this earth. First of all, the saved dead from Adam to Acts chapter 2 who are resurrected before Revelation 20:1--3. Secondly, the saved sinner in this age who eventually gets home to New Jerusalem where there is ["no more death, neither sorrow, nor crying, neither shall there be any more pain"]{.class_3760} (Rev. 21:4) and ["shalt not sin"]{.class_3760} (Job 5:24). Thirdly, the restored remnant of Israel (Rom. 9:27) who will be reigning on earth with the Lord Jesus Christ in the Millennium. (The Christian will share in this inheritance "if he suffers with Him" \[Rom. 8:17\].) The blind promises of the "Golden Age" of the Greek philosophers, the deluded guesses of the Hindu and Buddhist "gurus," the gossamer hope of "Liberty, Equality, and Fraternity" held forth by Frenchmen, the ridiculous evolutionary theories of Lorenz, Einstein, and Huxley, the violent "thousand year Reich" of Hitler, the blasphemous "Holy Roman Empire" of the popes, the Soviet "People's Republics" (and similar hopeless mirages) set up by Fascist asses, all bear the same stamp: unregenerate man's incurable faith in himself (instead of God) to bring about "peace on earth" without obeying or believing God. God has already foreordained that no lasting peace will show up apart from the Person and Presence of His own sinless Jewish Messiah.

"[And thou\...shalt not sin"]{.class_3760} (vs. 24). Oh, that is the real heaven. There isn't a Bible believer reading this page who hasn't cried out from the bottom of his heart, at times, ["Oh wretched man that I am! Who shall deliver me from the body of this death?"]{.class_3762} (Rom. 7:24). If you can think of any greater blessing, or any more glorious experience, than living in a place where you can think or do whatever you want to---twenty-four hours a day---without having to worry about it pleasing or not pleasing God, and at the same time knowing that God approves heartily of everything you are doing or thinking---well, "ole son," tell us about it will you!? Quickly. We've heard of everything else and have tried most of it. If there is anything better than that, let's hear it---quickly. (And don't give us any static about "loving each other and serving your fellow human" or "making this earth a better place to live on," you old religious fakir, you. Unsaved conmen, politicians, religious hucksters, dictators, and philosophers have been giving us that pitch for sixty centuries, and it has never been known to settle the Sin problem, the Death problem, the War problem, or the Truth problem more than a matter of minutes in isolated localities, under controlled conditions.)

"[Thy seed shall be great, and thine offspring as the grass of the earth"]{.class_3760} (vs. 25); so it comes to pass for Job (42:10--13), and so it will for Israel when their "captivity is turned" (see Job 42:1--6 and comments). Job does come to "his grave" (vs. 26) at a full age---well over 180.

The [Dead Bible ]{.class_3759}(Kenneth Taylor) makes such a slaughter of the passage that it is scarcely intelligible when you get through it. The first person plural (vs. 27) has been changed to the singular (for no good reason), and "nothing stolen from your barns" (vs. 24) has been substituted for ["thou shalt visit thy habitation and shalt not sin."]{.class_3760} That is about as "paraphrased" as saying that ["thou shalt be in league with the stones of the field"]{.class_3760} means "first base in Yankee Stadium is a granite slab." The absurd mistranslation of verse 19 to "he shall deliver you again and again" came from taking men like Clarke, Fausset, and Jamieson seriously: Don't ever consider any man to be a "serious" student of the Bible where he sits in judgment against it.

"[Lo this we have searched"]{.class_3760} (vs. 27)---Eliphaz and his friends. ["Hear it, and know thou it for thy good"]{.class_3762} (vs. 27). But how do you get a man to listen to such advice when he has buried ten children, had his wife turn against him, and then contracted enough infection and ulcers to kill three men?

[]{#IMh58iSMTFKKU0J9qyaeHw633.xhtml}

CHAPTER 6

6:1 But Job answered and said,

2 Oh that my grief were throughly weighed, and my calamity laid in the balances together!

3 For now it would be heavier than the sand of the sea: therefore my words are swallowed up.

4 For the arrows of the Almighty are within me, the poison whereof drinketh up my spirit: the terrors of God do set themselves in array against me.

What follows is as real as an on-the-scene, stereophonic, tape recording. Job simply ignores the entire discourse of Eliphaz. Instead of beginning with, "What do you mean it's for my good?" or "When is all of this going to take place?" or even "Who doesn't know that?", Job says: ["Oh that my grief were throughly weighed\..."]{.class_3760}!

You see, Job got off the track back in Job 5:8 about the time that Eliphaz began to talk about "trouble." After all, if you were Job, would you take lessons from a greenhorn after going through what he had just been through?

And these disjointed monologues are quite common to men even when they have not tasted Job's bitter wine. Did you ever listen carefully to three or four women talking while they were cleaning house, playing bridge, "visiting," or getting ready to shop? Most of it is little more than: "Well, I did this and this" or "Well, she said this and this" or "Well, I think that\...." And the gaps in the communication sound like a tape that has been spliced sixteen times and "scotched back" together haphazardly. If the little boys and girls in "Peanuts" and "Tiger" talked like little boys and girls really talk, it would sound more like this:

"Hey, look at me! I've got a new kite!"

"Yea, well my daddy drives a fire truck!"

"Look at the squirrel, Jimmy, look at the squirrel!"

"I hurt. Something is sticking me."

"You can't eat my biscuit."

"Jimmie has a girl friend! da-da-da-da-da-daaa!"

"So what, I've got a new bike."

"I've got to go to the bathroom."

Job relapses into self-pity by the time Eliphaz is halfway through with his "ministry" (and you couldn't get me to cast the first stone at him for doing so, even if you quoted eighty verses from the Pauline epistles!). ["For the arrows of the Almighty are within me\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 4). No, you are not going to keep a man's attention long who has visible proof that God has "sacked him" with arrows, poison, and ["terrors"]{.class_3760} (vs. 4). And who could refute him? Imagine some naive dude like Sigmund Freud (or Adler, Jung, Rank, Fromm, Horney, Reik, Klein, or Schilder) trying to get Job to "integrate his personality" into a "meaningful whole" so that the "Gestalt of identity" could validate the "inate capacities and latent potentialities" of his "Libido and Ego impulses!" They would sound like a three-year-old yelling into the Grand Canyon.

Job complains: "Oh, that you really knew what I'm putting up with. Boys, if you weighed it out on a scale, it would weigh 2,000,000,000 tons. I can't even open my mouth, and if I do, it won't sound right to you because you won't even listen. God has set me up like a target for target practice, and He has shot poisoned arrows at me; God has encamped around me like the army of an enemy; He is out to destroy me."

Job is gulping between sobs and is hardly able to talk (vs. 3). The ridiculous reading of The ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} at this point is as far from the truth as you can get unless you adopt the [New English Bible ]{.class_3759}reading: "what wonder if my words are wild?" (Kenneth Taylor got his reading from the antiquated [Pulpit Commentary]{.class_3759}, p. 103, and this is why the [RV ]{.class_3759}of 1885 read the same way, nearly ninety years before someone decided to call the Dead Bible the ["Living"]{.class_3759} [Bible.]{.class_3759}) The muddled [Septuagint ]{.class_3759}says the words "are in vain." But none of these superficial readings match the context. You see, not only are Job's words ["swallowed up"]{.class_3760} (vs. 3), but he is "drinking up" poison (vs. 4). The arrows are so similar to the ["fiery darts of the wicked"]{.class_3760} (Eph. 6:16) that, again, you could not tell them apart (see Psa. 38:2, 45:5, 120:4, 144:6; Zech. 9:14; and Hab. 3:11).

6:5 Doth the wild ass bray when he hath grass? or loweth the ox over his fodder?

6 Can that which is unsavoury be eaten without salt? or is there any taste in the white of an egg?

7 The things that my soul refused to touch are as my sorrowful meat.

Job now attempts to confront Eliphaz; he asks him four questions, and all four have a negative answer. Does a wild ass bray when he has food to eat? No. Does an ox low when his muzzle is in the trough? No. Is there any taste in the white of an egg? Not unless your taste buds are abnormal. But, of course, you can always eat "the slime of the PURSLANE"! (You read it right. That's the [RSV ]{.class_3759}reading for 1952, in a "bible" that was supposedly written to ["clear]{.class_3759} [up"]{.class_3759} the archaic words in a [King James Bible]{.class_3759}.) Then if you are really abnormal you can eat a "MALLOW." (You read it right again. That's the [New English Bible's reading, to help you "clear up the archaic words" in a King James Bible]{.class_3759}.) Of course, if you can't stomach eggs, "purslane," or "mallows," you can just make the egg be "empty words" ([Septuagint]{.class_3759}). But why not go "all out" and just say: "Is there any fish oil in a baked watermelon?" I mean, if you think you are God, why not just write your own Bible and quit complaining about people like me being "egotistical"?

Now where do you suppose all these "modern, updated" versions (in the "living language of the people") got their "purslanes" and "mallows" from? Well, we'll have to be a little "reactionary," at least nonprogressive enough to back up one hundred years to the writings of the [Pulpit Commentary ]{.class_3759}(Rev. Rawlinson); and there we find this brainstorm: "We have certainly [no other evidence ]{.class_3759}that eggs were eaten in primitive times" (p. 103). So? So, if God Almighty presents ANY evidence before the Scholars Union begins to write (or "dig up" evidence), then God's legal exhibits are to be thrown out of court? If Ramsay, Wooley, Garstang, Free, Conyebeare, or Nelson Glueck can't find "evidence" to match the Bible, then Ramsay, Wooley, etc., are [right ]{.class_3759}and God is [wrong]{.class_3759}? This is the thinking in the Scholars Union when it comes to "the search for truth." For example, didn't it ever seem rather strange to you that there is no "evidence" that the people of Job's time ate liver, salads, pies, bacon, chili, or chickens? Hasn't it struck you as rather odd that the word "cat" never appears in the Bible one time, and yet the cat was one of the most sacred animals in Egypt? If Moses lived in Egypt forty years (see Exod. 1--2 and comments) and wrote the parts of Genesis that deal with Egypt (Gen. 40--50), why didn't he mention the "cats"? Because there were none?

Now this is the kind of "scientific research" that passes off as rational "logic" when we deal with such deluded critics as Nowack, Plumptre, Plummer, Riddle, Schleiermacher, Schottgen, Schott, Schroeder, Steiger, Tregelles, Tischendorf, Tholuck, Westcott, Hort, and the other you-know-who's.

"Eggs" figure in primitive fertility rites before 2000 B.C., and the constant reoccurrence of colored eggs at the feast of Ishtar (Acts 12:4 and comments) shows that eggs have been around quite awhile; the ancients usually ATE what they sacrificed to their gods (1 Cor. 10:28)---including people! Don't hand us any jazz about "no eggs being eaten in primitive times." Feed that to some chump who doesn't know a flat-joint from a flop-house.

"[The things that my soul refused to touch"]{.class_3760} (vs. 7). Notice how the word "soul" is used, as it is used in Genesis 19:20; Leviticus 22; and Ezekiel 18---identical with physical life. (See comments under Genesis 2:7 in that Commentary.) ["Are as my sorrowful meat"]{.class_3762} (vs. 7). This has all been "paraphrased" (if you will pardon the out-and-out lying) to read: "I gag at the thought of eating IT"---the "it" being an uncooked egg. The writer responsible for this "paraphrase" (if you will overlook the ribaldry) is Kenneth Taylor (The Dead Bible), but at least this is better than "my bowels rumble with a rumbling sound" ([NEB]{.class_3761}). Umbreit says: "My sickness is like such nauseous food." Anything but believe the text. Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown try "such is the sickness of my food." But after all, when could the majority of Conservative egg-heads (or "mallow heads") do anything with 400 years of education that John Wesley,\
D. L. Moody, George Whitefield, Jonathan Edwards, David Brainerd, Billy Sunday, Peter Cartwright, or Charles Finney couldn't do better with half a year? The meaning is perfectly obvious to anyone but a scholar: "When I was well, I chose my own menu, and now that I am sick, I have to eat stuff that would have made me vomit." And beyond this lies a much deeper thought: "my soul refused to have concourse with sorrow, suffering, pain, insomnia, and loneliness, but now I have all five for a steady diet!"

6:8 Oh that I might have my request; and that God would grant me the thing that I long for!

9 Even that it would please God to destroy me; that he would let loose his hand, and cut me off!

10 Then should I yet have comfort; yea, I would harden myself in sorrow: let him not spare; for I have not concealed the words of the Holy One.

Following Job's questions to his friends (which were never answered), the poor man lifts his tearstained face to heaven and cries: ["Oh\...that God would grant me the thing that I long for"]{.class_3760} (vs. 8). The words are wrung from the heart like vinegar is squeezed from a sponge. This is the heart-cry of the centuries, and God alone knows how many times it has sounded forth from battlefields, mine shafts, prison cells, pits, hospitals, graveyards, mental wards, churches, life rafts, and even homes. The unfulfilled "longing" which God has placed in man (Rom. 8:20) takes on a thousand forms, and everyone of them is intended to drive one great truth home to mankind, yet mankind has resisted that truth from the beginning of time. Unfulfilled longing (Psa. 41:1, 43:5) is God's own witness to man that [this ]{.class_3759}earth and this life---the material things which can be apprehended by the senses---is NOT the final goal of life nor the solution to man's problems. In the final analysis, the only real "reality" is Heaven itself, in the presence of God, and all of this "pie on the earth, bye-and-bye" peddled by deluded sinners like Mao Tse-tung, Castro, Rockefeller, the Kennedys, the NAACP, the RCC, the SDA, the CFR, the HEW, the United Nations, the League of Nations, the Communist Commissars, and the National Educational Association is nothing but the pitch of a pipe-man who wants to get credit for benefiting "the people" (see [The Sure Word of]{.class_3759} [Prophecy]{.class_3759}). These little demon-possessed do-gooders are all trying to salve their guilty consciences by "helping" their fellow man---at your expense. The answer of this type of degenerate is: "Well, all right then, we'll [kill ]{.class_3759}you and you can go to [your ]{.class_3759}heaven, but we are here to make [this world ]{.class_3759}a better place to live in." And that about wraps it up. They've been killing each other for six millennia, and they will continue to annihilate God's testimony against them (the Bible) and go on searching for a pot of gold that was taken away before Cain killed Abel.

"[Oh that I might have my request\...."]{.class_3760} Oh, that there might be peace on earth! Oh, that my little boy might live! Oh, that I might be able to make the payments next month! Oh, that Ishmael might live before thee! Oh, that I had married that one instead of this one! Oh, that there might be a revival in our church! Oh, that there might be peace in our home ! Oh, that my mother might get saved! Oh, that I might get out of this bombardment alive! Oh, oh, oh, oh!

Well, God [can ]{.class_3759}help you, and He will in [due time]{.class_3759}; but if He [doesn't]{.class_3759}, you're a fool to think that you, or any man, can solve your problems [without ]{.class_3759}God's help. Asa ["sought unto the physicians"]{.class_3760} and NOT ["unto the LORD"]{.class_3760} (2 Chron. 16:12), so he died. Luke was a beloved physician (Col. 4:14), and God could use him; but if you think that any physician can help you without God's help, you haven't talked to very many physicians.

"[Even that it would please God to destroy me"]{.class_3760} (vs. 9). Plato viewed suicide as rebellion against God, while Aristotle thought it was "cowardly." (Socrates was either of a different opinion or else he was under more pressure than Aristotle and Plato were.)

"[That he would loose his hand, and cut me off"]{.class_3760} (vs. 9). Have you ever noticed how many men in the Bible desire to polish themselves off, but the Lord completely ignores them? Consider Moses in Exodus 32:32; consider Jonah in Jonah 4:9; consider Peter in Luke 22:33. The Lord doesn't seem to be too sympathetic with a suicide's request, although He did give Samson an answer to prayer (Judg. 16:30). Karl Menninger would probably refer to Job's desire as a "self-destructive tendency from the Thanatos drive, guided by the Nirvana principle." (Which is a long way of saying: "I can make seventy dollars an hour any time with the tradesman's terminology."

So far, God's hand has been restrained from total destruction (see 1:12 and 2:6). Job is requesting God to untie His hands (["loose his hand"]{.class_3760}) and smite the final blow that will bring the tragedy to an end. And again, a man called to minister to human beings had better be careful how he treats such outbursts. It wouldn't hurt any minister to study the works of Willi Heinrich, especially the little paperback called [The Crack of Doom]{.class_3759}. When Captain Schmitt finally dies---naked, hanging by wires upside down from a roof beam, in weather of 10 degrees Fahrenheit--- he is within his rights in crying, "Let me die, Oh Lord !" In such a position, a shot through the lower intestine doesn't improve matters, either.

The contemporary row over "mercy killings" comes into the spotlight here, but none of Job's friends make a move to kill him. Recently (1973), in America, a young man was acquitted for killing his sick brother, who had begged for release from his sickness. Cases in combat are so common as to be routine. In World War I, when they saw a man shot through the groin or the testicles and he was screaming at the top of his lungs, they often put him out of his misery. You see, there was no "dust off" by the "Jolly Green Giant" (medical helicopters) in those days, and often the wounded lay in "No-man's land" three days before expiring. Remarque is telling the truth when he says that he has seen men still living with half of their faces gone, or their backside bare to the lungs and kidneys, or has seen them running on stumps after their feet have been blown off. This world has never been a "Child's Garden of Verses," and Fehrenbach has well stated ([That Kind of a War]{.class_3759}) that the men who trained the generation who fought in Korea forgot to tell them that there were TIGERS in this world. (For example, a generation that is raised to believe that a [race war ]{.class_3759}is not going on in the United States is a deceived bunch of neurotics.)

In 1918, when the troops came home to the U.S.A., one woman was in distraction because the troop train had unloaded in her hometown, and her husband was nowhere to be found in the group that got off. She knew that he had been wounded but was on his way home on this particular train. After asking all the porters and conductors about her husband, she was finally told: "He's back in the baggage car." She found him. He was a double amputee---a basket case---and all that was left of him was his head, neck and torso; that woman lost her mind.

In 1945, near the end of the war, a mother and father in a northeastern state got a phone call from San Francisco. Their boy was coming home, and he was bringing a "buddy" home with him. The mother and father told him to bring his pal on with him for a short visit, but the son insisted that his buddy was "hurt badly" and should live permanently with the family. The boy's father tried to soft-pedal this by saying, "Well, of course, yes, well\...well, he can stay for a while, if you like." The son pressed his point: "But Dad, my buddy lost both of his legs; he's got to be somewhere where people can love him and take care of him. He has no folks of his own." "Well," said the father, "we will do what we can, but of course you understand, if he is helpless, he will have to go somewhere after a while. We can't afford to put up with every\...." And the telephone connection was cut. Two days later, that mother and father were notified by the police department of San Francisco that their son had committed suicide by hurling himself out of a twelfth story hotel window. They got to San Francisco and had the funeral for him. They looked into the casket before it was put out on display and saw their son for the first time since he returned stateside: [both his legs had been amputated at the hip. ]{.class_3759}["Even that it would please God to destroy me!"]{.class_3760}

I believe the most terrible sight that I ever saw was something that crossed my field of vision for only a few seconds and to which, at that time, I did not really give much consideration. I had been in the Veteran's Hospital at Tuscaloosa, Alabama, for about six days with a bad "strepthroat" and a light case of flu. In going down one of the corridors on my way out of the hospital, I passed a\
room where I saw three or four nurses gathered around a patient in bed. However, the bed looked more like a canvas hammock than a real "bed. " I stopped for just a moment and went on, and then, as a nurse came out of the room, I asked rather impolitely: "What's wrong with that man in there?"

She looked me straight in the eye and said, "He was hit by shrapnel in World War I. The wounds never healed. We keep him in two inches of vaseline, and we have to turn him from side to side to keep the bed sores from getting infected." With that, she went on down the hall. I don't believe it was until twenty-four hours later that I completely comprehended what that nurse had said. But when I finally digested it, I felt a wild compulsion to drop ROTC and forget a commission in the Infantry. You see, that wounded veteran had been bedridden for twenty-five years. [Twenty-five years in a hammock with people turning him!]{.class_3759} ["Oh\...that God\...would let loose his hand, and cut me off!"]{.class_3760} Don't get too critical of Job till you have been ["in the body"]{.class_3760} with him (Heb. 13:3) on the ash heap.

"[Then should I have comfort; yea, I would harden myself in sorrow"]{.class_3760} (vs. 10). "If I knew He was going to do it, I would quit worrying and get some sleep. I would too. I would just take old Zeno and his stoicism and 'tough it out' till the axe fell." (Taylor's work on the verse is a desperate attempt to reinstate Adam Clarke \[eighteenth century\] back into the twentieth century. Kennicott and De Rossi were the destructive critics who altered the Masoretic text to read as the ["Living" Bible.]{.class_3761})

"[I have not concealed the words of the Holy One"]{.class_3760} (vs. 10). At the appearance of this terrifying expression--- ["WORDS"]{.class_3762} (see Prov. 30:6; Eph. 6:17, and comments in those commentaries), C. H. Dodd ([NEB]{.class_3761}) nearly has a heart attack, and before anyone can shoot the glycerine to him, he throws the sentence clean out of the Bible. The Hebrew text is thrown down into a footnote, and instead, we are told that The [New English Bible ]{.class_3761}reading is "the probable reading." That is, C. H. Dodd and his playmates think that the Hebrew text is probably wrong, and [theirs ]{.class_3761}is probably right. But since all relativists who followed Lorenz and Einstein believe that "probable" only means "probably [probable]{.class_3761}," it is also probably probable that Dodd is only probably probable. And since we have an absolute, infallible authority, there is not much sense in following Dodd or Taylor anywhere. Nor can we follow Adam Clarke any further; that worthy gentleman will not even print the text in his commentary (since it says ["the words of the Holy One"]{.class_3762}). Instead, Clarke bows out with "I have not resisted his decree." More applesauce. But Heavenor was raised on applesauce; he omits the words also and tells us that Job did not "deny" any of God's [commands]{.class_3761}, and so say Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown. Observe how the "WORDS of God" are always perverted into generalizations by these great recognized, conservative, dedicated, godly, separated [liars]{.class_3761}.

"[The words of the Holy One"]{.class_3760} are what these men hate. They will maintain the [decrees, commandments, teachings, principles]{.class_3761} (see any Liberal work), [statutes, fundamentals]{.class_3761}, or [doctrines ]{.class_3761}(see Council of Nicaea), but they will not tolerate THE WORDS of God (John 5:47, 8:47). Like John R. Rice, they will talk about the verbal inspiration of every word and say, in the same breath, that [no Bible ]{.class_3761}is trustworthy when it comes to every [word]{.class_3761}. When John R. Rice wrote his normal propaganda about "the Bible his mother and father used" (and believed) and tells that THAT Bible is the word of God, including every word God spoke, he was lying as flat as a Persian rug, and what is worse, he knew he was. When Rice waved his Bible in the air (like 30,000 of his playmates) and hollered "this Book" and "this Bible," [he was making no reference to the Bible he had in his hand]{.class_3761} (according to his own confession of faith) than Wilbur Smith or Spiros Zodiates ([The Behavior of Belief]{.class_3761}, see pp. 134, 181, 245, etc.).

Now Job had not "concealed the words of the Holy One" (vs. 10), for he knew the words of the Holy One (Job 23:12), and so did his friends. It is only the boundless conceit of the faculty members of Bethel St. Paul, Heston College, Taccoa Falls, Vancouver Bible Institute, Faith Bible College, Pensacola Christian College, Eugene Bible College, Northwestern, Bob Jones, Midwestern, and Liberty Bible College, that makes them think that Clarke, Fausset, Brown, Taylor, Gesenius, Keil, Delitzsch, Duhm, Budde, Kline, Bullinger, or Godde had enough sense to comment on the passage. Discarding these men (and their work and their writings and their ministries and their opinions), let us turn to the infallible words of the Holy Bible and see what God really said (and preserved), in spite of 6,000 stuffed shirts who mistook "Education" for intelligence.

1\. Elihu (the author) says that ["the inspiration of the Almighty"]{.class_3760} (32:8) gives men understanding.

2\. God did speak to men in Job's time (33:14), and ["God speaketh once, yea twice, yet man perceiveth it not"]{.class_3760} (22:22).

3\. There [were ]{.class_3759}messengers sent to men to show them their uprightness (33:23), and Eliphaz received one of these messengers (4:16--17) in the very chapter which preceded Job's lament.

4\. God appeared constantly to people in the Old Testament [before the time of Moses ]{.class_3759}(Num. 12:6), and when He did (Acts 7:2), He spoke words to them (Gen.20:3, 6--7).

5\. Elihu said that his own words were not false (36:4), because ["He that is perfect"]{.class_3760} (36:4) is bearing witness to them.

6\. Furthermore, Job quotes God verbatim in Job 28:28 after saying, ["The spirit of God is in my nostrils"]{.class_3760} (27:3)

7\. And if that weren't enough, Job taught what God showed him (27:11), and he did not ["conceal"]{.class_3760} it (27:11) exactly as he states it TWICE, for he esteemed it (23:12) more than his necessary food.

Therefore! Therefore, you may reject the [ASV ]{.class_3759}(any edition), the [RSV ]{.class_3759}(any edition), the [NEB]{.class_3759}, the ["Living" Bible,]{.class_3759} plus the next thirty translations, along with everyone who promotes, supports, sells, uses, or recommends them, on the grounds of:

a\. Their deliberate refusal to believe the [words ]{.class_3759}of God.

b\. Their vicious, prejudicial hatred for the [AV ]{.class_3759}of 1611.

c\. Their boundless ignorance of the very Book they profess to believe in.

d\. Their reactionary bias for the Dark Ages Roman "bibles."

e\. And their inability to compare Scripture with Scripture in any language, in any version, including [their own versions]{.class_3759}.

This is not one of those cases where it is "When in doubt, etc." This is one of those cases where there is no doubt whatsoever about the correct text.
