As surely as the [New Scofield Reference Bible ]{.class_3759}is not the [Authorized Version]{.class_3759}; the new "bibles" are not the truth in any sense of the meaning of that word.

When Job ministered as a circuit court judge (29:7--9, 22--23), he passed on what God gave him by prayer and revelation (23:12); and he had not ["concealed the words of the Holy One"]{.class_3760} (vs. 10), even when Bob Jones University, the Training Union Quarterlies, the [Sword of the Lord]{.class_3759}, the [Christian Herald]{.class_3759}, the [Biblical Monthly Review]{.class_3759}, the National Council of Churches, and [Christianity Today ]{.class_3759}(look at Job 31:34. Go on---LOOK AT IT!) were stamping all over the Bible like it was a dirty rag. Religious and political majorities of any persuasion never bothered Job anymore than they bothered Noah, Amos, Jeremiah, Micaiah, or Stephen. He went by the old saying of Dr. Bob Jones Sr. (deceased): "One man and God is a majority." Job, who had the ["words of the Holy One,"]{.class_3760} winds up getting his "friends" out of a real jam (42:7), for "the wrath of God" (42:7) was never kindled against Job; it was kindled against those ["who hold the truth in unrighteousness"]{.class_3760} (Rom. 1:18). And even though it appears for a while that Job is "in dutch" with the Lord, at the end of the game Job has all the blue chips, and his friends are looking for a handout.

6:11 What is my strength, that I should hope? and what is mine end, that I should prolong my life?

12 Is my strength the strength of stones? or is my flesh of brass?

13 Is not my help in me? and is wisdom driven quite from me?

The lament is vivid in the [AV ]{.class_3759}text, and it needs no "updating" by anyone. "What can I hope for, my strength is gone, isn't it? Why should I prolong my life when the end of it can't come out right? Do you think I'm made of brass and stone? I'm flesh, I tell you! The only help I can get is inside myself because God won't help me. And do you think I don't know that: Do you think that wisdom is completely driven from me?"

Now that is the "lay of the land," and the attempts by the commentators, revisers, and "word detectives" to dress it up so that Job says what they think he ought to mean is just so much fried purslane and poached mallows. Job did not say: "how shall I find help\...?" ([NEB]{.class_3759}) He did not say: "\...that I continue" ([Septuagint]{.class_3759}). He did not say: "There is no help in me" (Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown). He did not say: "Is it not that I have no help" ([ASV]{.class_3759}, 1901), nor did he say any fool thing that any fool [said ]{.class_3759}he said that he did not say. He said: ["is not my help in me?"]{.class_3760} And if you cannot understand that after believing, prayerful, serious Bible study, you have no business preaching the Book of Job or commenting on it or teaching it, let alone revising it.

6:14 To him that is afflicted pity should be shewed from his friend; but he forsaketh the fear of the Almighty.

15 My brethren have dealt deceitfully as a brook, and as the stream of brooks they pass away;

16 Which are blackish by reason of the ice, and wherein the snow is hid:

17 What time they wax warm, they vanish: when it is hot, they are consumed out of their place.

18 The paths of their way are turned aside; they go to nothing, and perish.

19 The troops of Tema looked, the companies of Sheba waited for them.

20 They were confounded because they had hoped; they came thither, and were ashamed.

21 For now ye are nothing; ye see my casting down, and are afraid.

Again, we are placed into the Tribulation. As the commentators ride roughshod over verses 16--20 and confine them to an incident that happened [before ]{.class_3759}the time of Moses, the Holy Spirit points the way [forward ]{.class_3759}to Obadiah 9; Habakkuk 3:3; Amos 1:12; Jeremiah 49:7, 20; and Psalm 72:10, 15. The reader should notice that the expression Job uses (["the Almighty"]{.class_3760}) was a famous expression in the days of Abraham (Gen. 17:1, 28:3, 35:11, 43:14). There are only three uses of this word in Moses' day, and two of these are in connection with a Gentile priest who was not under the Mosaic law (Num. 24:4,16). We are obviously in a pre-Mosaic "economy" (dead orthodox cliche used by those who like "redemptive history").

Job is right. ["Pity should be shewed"]{.class_3760} to him that is afflicted (vs. 14), and when a man refuses to show pity to his friend, he does forsake ["the fear of the Almighty"]{.class_3760} (not "reverential" trust, as in the [New Scofield ]{.class_3759}notes).

Job's brethren are likened to a stream or a brook that dries up about the time you come to it to get refreshing (vs. 17). The streams in Palestine in the wintertime are frozen and later turn into the "blackish" muck (vs. 16) so characteristic of streams. This ice melts (vs. 17), and then, it "goes to nothing" (vs. 18). Like a gulley in West Texas, the brook is full of water one day while a week later it is empty. Job's friends are these icy waters, for they not only comfort no one, they eventually "turn aside" (vs. 18). Furthermore, in the next illustration, Job likens himself to the melted ice (which could have at least parched a thirsty throat) and his friends like unto a troop-escorted caravan on its way to Sheba. They come to a water hole and don't find any water, so they are disappointed. And the reception committee at the other end of the line (Sheba, in South Arabia) is disappointed, for the Temanites never arrive. ["They were confounded because they had hoped"]{.class_3760} (vs. 20) that the caravan from Tema would arrive. ["They came thither, and were ashamed"]{.class_3760} (vs. 20) would refer to the Temanites who came to the brook (vs. 15) and found no refreshing. So the similitudes have double application. Job's buddies are like a stream that was supposed to refresh him and they didn't; Job's friends are like travelers who came to him for comfort and were ashamed and confounded because of the condition they found him in (see 2:12). And verse 21 will confirm this interpretation absolutely: ["For now ye are nothing; ye see my casting down, and are afraid"]{.class_3760} (vs. 21).

There is nothing to stir up your conscience, awaken your "guilt complexes," upset your day, and move you to criticism like seeing what "evil" God has done to someone who was as good as [you ]{.class_3761}or BETTER---is there? Did you ever wonder why the preaching of "Christ crucified" upsets so many religious people? Really there are only about three ways you can get around Calvary and get home to Hell. One way is to say that God wasn't in it and that Christ merely suffered a needless martyrdom for being foolhardy about his beliefs (see "[Jesus Christ Superstar]{.class_3761}," presented by your local Fruit stand); another way is to say that "accidents can happen to anyone," "we must take the bad with the good," or "well, those things happen"; a third way is to say that Christ was paying for His [own sins ]{.class_3761}to earn His own salvation, so we should work to earn [ours]{.class_3761}. Aside from those three stratagems there is not much you can do, for as surely as Jesus Christ was a better man dead than you are living, God "spared Him not" but delivered Him over to one of the cruelest deaths that man ever devised. If that happened to Him, I wonder what can happen to YOU or to ME? (See Rev. 20:11--15 and comments.)

Verses 18 and 20, in the [Septuagint]{.class_3759}, are so mangled you couldn't even associate them with the Biblical text. Ewald, Schlotmann, Davidson, Delitzsch, Siegfield, Dillmann, and Ley have about thirty corrections they would like to make on the first twenty verses in the chapter; but Voltaire, Rousseau, Celsus, Tom Paine, Bob Ingersoll, and Timothy O'Leary could "raise the ante" by ten and never bat an eye. Why take the Conservatives when the infidels can go them ten better?

6:22 Did I say, Bring unto me? or, Give a reward for me of your substance?

23 Or, Deliver me from the enemy's hand? or, Redeem me from the hand of the mighty?

24 Teach me, and I will hold my tongue: and cause me to understand wherein I have erred.

25 How forcible are right words! but what doth your arguing reprove?

26 Do ye imagine to reprove words, and the speeches of one that is desperate, which are as wind?

27 Yea, ye overwhelm the fatherless, and ye dig a pit for your friend.

28 Now therefore be content, look upon me; for it is evident unto you if I lie.

29 Return, I pray you, let it not be iniquity; yea, return again, my righteousness is in it.

30 Is there iniquity in my tongue? cannot my taste discern perverse things?

Job is now aiming his remarks directly at his friends; he spits out the questions without waiting for an answer: "Did I ask you to bring me anything? Did I ask for financial help (vs. 22)? Did I ask any of you to deliver me from God or the Devil (vs. 23)? Are you trying to straighten me out with words (vs. 26)? You think I'm lying? Don't you know my taste is just as sensitive as yours when it comes to good and evil (vs. 30)?" Blam, blam, blam!! ["Teach me, and I will hold my tongue: and cause me to understand wherein I have erred"]{.class_3760} (vs. 24). Job actually wants to know. He is not a dishonest skeptic, and he is not dismissing anyone's advice if it can get to the root of the problem. But Job's problem was [self-righteousness ]{.class_3759}(27:5 and 40:8), and Job's "physicians" (13:4) have already diagnosed his disease as "committed sins."

"[How forcible are right words! But what doth your arguing reprove?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 25). Implying that their arguments were not "right." ["Do you imagine to reprove words, and the speeches of one that is desperate, which are as wind?"]{.class_3762} It is like: "Are you trying to entangle me in my words because they are coming out of me like wind out of a bellows?" When a man is desperate (see text), he is liable to say many things he does not mean. And, as far as that goes, many people say things they do not mean when they "fly off the handle," even when they are in good health. If Job's complaint of Chapter 3 was composed of "words and speeches" like wind, what of it? You'd get kind of windy too, wouldn't you, if you lost home, family, house, cattle, goods, land, health, and friends without any apparent reason for it? Yes, you would!

"[Look upon me; for it is evident unto you if I lie"]{.class_3760} (vs. 28). Job says: "I'm not falsely accusing you; [you ]{.class_3761}are falsely accusing me. [I'm]{.class_3761} not the one who is overwhelming the fatherless (see 31:2): you are." Job tells his friend to "rethink it" (twentieth-century cliché for getting rid of Bible doctrines). ["Return, I pray you, let it not be iniquity; yea, return again, my righteousness is in it"]{.class_3762} (vs. 29). And Job's righteousness was in his argument, but the trouble was that Job is trusting his righteousness (see Rom. 10:1--4) exactly as Israel trusted theirs. Furthermore, Job is now pleading with his friends not to sin: ["Let it not be iniquity"]{.class_3762} (vs. 29). He doesn't want the discussion to degenerate into a name-calling fest where his friends will sin against God by violating the ninth commandment. And though the "Ten Commandments" were not yet on the "tablets of stone," they were written in the "hearts" of Job's audience according to Romans 2:15. Job is challenging Eliphaz and his buddies to by-pass a wordy argument about words and speeches and lay their fingers on the root of the problem, but the wordy conversation gets more "wordy" by the minute. (The paraphrase of the [Dead Bible ]{.class_3761}is a private interpretation of Taylor's, and from verse 22 to verse 30 there is little in it that resembles the text of the Bible.)

[]{#IMh58iSMTFKKU0J9qyaeHw728.xhtml}

CHAPTER 7

7:1 Is there not an appointed time to man upon earth? are not his days also like the days of an hireling?

2 As a servant earnestly desireth the shadow, and as an hireling looketh for the reward of his work:

3 So am I made to possess months of vanity, and wearisome nights are appointed to me.

4 When I lie down, I say, When shall I arise, and the night be gone? and I am full of tossings to and fro unto the dawning of the day.

5 My flesh is clothed with worms and clods of dust; my skin is broken, and become loathsome.

6 My days are swifter than a weaver's shuttle, and are spent without hope.

Job continues to complain. He complains that his life is not worth living, that he can't sleep, that he is like a slave looking for the day to end so he can rest, that his skin is loathsome and revolting, and that he doesn't have anything to look forward to; and, from his standpoint, all of this is absolutely true.

Adam Clarke calls verse 1 a "bad translation"---exactly as John R. Rice treated Revelation 22:14 and Wuest treated Romans 8:22 and Thieme treated 2 Thessalonians 2 and Zodhiates treated James 5:20 and English treated Daniel 3:25 and---but why go on? Why not just say that Clarke wrote a bum commentary here and that his "godly"life had no more to do with his trashy piece of God-dishonoring commenting than Vanderbilt's home (North Carolina) had to do with Hitler's resting place in Hell. (Cornelius Vanderbilt professed the new birth to no one a single day that he lived on this earth.)

There is ["an appointed time to man upon earth"]{.class_3760} (vs. 1), according to Acts 17:26 in the New Testament Psalm 79:11 in the Old Testament, and the appointments are so clear in 1 Peter 2:8; Luke 12:46; and Acts 17:31 that only a Bible-rejecting Fundamentalist (who believed in the "verbally inspired originals") could compete with Mr. Clarke. The days of man are "like the days of an hireling" (vs. 1), for the wages that he earns are paid to him (Rom. 6:23). "Man" is given orders (Exod. 20) as a servant. He is punished for breaking laws (moral, natural, social, Divine, or economic). He is paid off for his works (1 Cor. 3; 2 Cor. 5; Rom. 2:1--5), saved or lost. He looks for rewards (Luke 12:43--44; 1 Cor. 3:1--14) whether he gets them or not, and for most men, "wearisome nights" and "months of vanity" (vs. 3) are quite common experiences. Job has more than his share, but everything in the first two verses is doctrinally, historically, and scripturally correct. (Mr. Clarke's note is a bad mistake.)

We run into Bible prophecy again in verses 4 and 5, for these were the curses promised to Israel if she rejected God's commandments (Deut. 28; Lev. 26). The worm-clothed flesh and the loathsome skin (vs. 5) is nothing new under the sun. When Dr. Stanley Pavillard ministered to British and American prisoners in Malaya, who were building the infamous "bridge over the river Kwai," he used maggots as an antiseptic to clear infections from open wounds; and he often saw his "comrades" dissolving into watery masses beneath "a swarm of flies and maggots." Job's days are ["swifter than a weaver's shuttle"]{.class_3760} (vs. 6) as she weaves the thread through the ["shuttle";]{.class_3760} but Adam Clarke said: "It has been doubted whether any such instrument was in use in the days of Job" (p. 48). To which may be replied: "Ah, your father's moustache!" (But since the Septuagint also erased the ["shuttle,"]{.class_3760} Taylor didn't have much choice: After all, who could offend all of these "godly, dedicated, etc.," by not giving "due consideration" and "reverential respect for their considered opinions"? Several of us can.)

7:7 O remember that my life is wind: mine eye shall no more see good.

8 The eye of him that hath seen me shall see me no more: thine eyes are upon me, and I am not.

9 As the cloud is consumed and vanisheth away: so he that goeth down to the grave shall come up no more.

10 He shall return no more to his house, neither shall his place know him any more.

11 Therefore I will not refrain my mouth; I will speak in the anguish of my spirit; I will complain in the bitterness of my soul.

And now we find life defined as ["wind"]{.class_3760} (vs. 7); so here will be a good place to list the twelve definitions of life as found in the oldest known real "book" in the world.

1\. Life is like [WIND ]{.class_3759}(7:7).

2\. Life is like a [weaver's shuttle]{.class_3760} (7:6).

3\. Life is like a [servitude ]{.class_3760}(7:1).

4\. Life is like a [trial ]{.class_3760}(7:2, 18).

5\. Life is like a [shadow ]{.class_3760}(8:9).

6\. Life is like a [race horse ]{.class_3759}(9:25).

7\. Life is like an [eagle's flight ]{.class_3759}(9:26).

8\. Life is like a [swift boat ]{.class_3759}(9:26).

9\. Life is like a [leaf ]{.class_3759}blown in the wind (13:25).

10\. Life is like a [rotten tree ]{.class_3759}(14:10).

11\. Life is like a [flower ]{.class_3759}(14:10).

12\. Life is like a [cloud ]{.class_3759}(30:15).

Now I don't care how you "cut the cake" or "how the cookie crumbles," those are the descriptions of life found in the Wisdom Books, given by the Author of Life and connected with a man who went through more in a year than most of us will go through in a lifetime. You may become more pessimistic if you like and try Solomon's ["vanity of vanities, all is vanity,"]{.class_3760} or you may prefer to pick up the refrain of James: "Your life is a vapor." But the only real life worth living is the one Paul spoke of when he said, ["for to me to live is Christ"]{.class_3760} (Phil. 1:21). Since the Lord Jesus Christ is ["the life" ]{.class_3760}(John 14:6), the rest of the "lives" can only be classified in one of Job's twelve brackets. (See comments on "life" under Proverbs 15:24; 18:21; 8:35; 22:4; 4:13, and 10:17 in that Commentary.)

A. Life is like WIND: That is, it is a spiritual thing (John 3:5--8), and any fool like Heidegger, Sartre, or Kierkegaard, who makes the mistake of thinking that you can put spiritual things secondary (Ezek. 37:9, 10) and still find "fulfillment" and "meaning" to your life, is to be classified with Darwin, Haeckel, Huxley, and the other monkey-men.

B. Life is like a WEAVER'S SHUTTLE: Back and forth, back and forth it goes, and then the first thing you know---SNIP! There went the thread.

C. Life is a SERVITUDE (see comments under 7:16).

D. Life is like a TRIAL: The Greek word for "sheep" (see John 10) is the word for probationer---someone on "probation." If a boxer or a track man is not "tried" when he trains, what do you call it? Well, Paul said that the Christian life was like boxing and running (1 Cor. 9:26). What do you think a "trial- run" is? Job says: ["What is man that thou shouldest\...try him every moment"]{.class_3760} (vss. 17--18)? The Lord Jesus Christ was tried in the wilderness and then tested all His life till He stood "trial" before Herod and Pilate.

E. Life is like a SHADOW: It runs twelve hours (or sixteen at the most), and that's the end of it. The sun casts shadows from daybreak to dark, but after that the shadows are not real; they are created by artificial light.

F. Life is like a RACE HORSE: "They're at the post! They're OFF!" And it is mane to mane, bridle to bridle, stirrup to stirrup, hoofs thundering, nostrils snorting, and muscles straining until you gallop around the far turn and into the home stretch; then the race is over. Life is competitive. Life is a race track (Heb. 12:1--3). You can bet on it. The "postillion" is given orders and told to run with a message. Do you have yours? Who told you to run (see Jer. 23:21!)?

G. Life is like an EAGLE'S FLIGHT: You move through it as fast as an eagle heads for the prey, and you drop in flight like a plummet falls to the earth---"one fell swoop."

H. Life is like a SWIFT BOAT: Now you see it; now you don't. Before you eat a meal, it is vanishing over the horizon. Someone else pilots it by his own compass and chart, and the course of a sailboat is largely determined by the way the winds blow (see [The Bible Believer's Commentary ]{.class_3759}on Acts 27).

I. Life is like a LEAF blown in the wind: It is helpless, at the mercy of the elements, soon to rot on a pile of fallen leaves, and so fragile that pieces of it fall off as it flies before the storm.

J. Life is like a ROTTEN TREE: Sins eat us out from inside, we get old and decayed, and if the "feller" doesn't cut us down, we fall over after a while and are good for nothing but kindling (Ezek. 15:2--5).

K. Life is like a FLOWER: Flowers grow in families; they neither toil nor do they spin (Matt. 6:28), yet God provides for them. Some are beautiful; some are not. Some are poisonous; some are harmless. Some are nourishing; some are only "wall flowers." God picks them from His garden (Song of Sol. 4:16; 6:2) as He sees fit.

L. Life is like a CLOUD: It is a ["vapour,"]{.class_3760} that ["appeareth for a little time and then vanisheth away"]{.class_3760} (James 4:14). It has no real solid substance; again, it is at the mercy of the winds, and it is hard to "lay ahold of" as steam coming out of a kettle.

Now, in all of these similitudes there is one particular truth that is emphasized over and over again, and regardless of the variety of characteristics, there is one theme that stands out clearly: THE BREVITY OF LIFE. Everything given was moving or growing rapidly and passing or disappearing rapidly. A watch doesn't really go "tick, tick, tick." It goes "quick, quick, quick!" "You can't go till your number is up," but your number may come up before Washington raises your taxes again.

7:12 Am I a sea, or a whale, that thou settest a watch over me?

13 When I say, My bed shall comfort me, my couch shall ease my complaint;

14 Then thou scarest me with dreams, and terrifiest me through visions:

15 So that my soul chooseth strangling, and death rather than my life.

16 I loathe it; I would not live alway: let me alone; for my days are vanity.

17 What is man, that thou shouldest magnify him? and that thou shouldest set thine heart upon him?

18 And that thou shouldest visit him every morning, and try him every moment?

19 How long wilt thou not depart from me, nor let me alone till I swallow down my spittle?

20 I have sinned; what shall I do unto thee, O thou preserver of men? why hast thou set me as a mark against thee, so that I am a burden to myself?

21 And why dost thou not pardon my transgression, and take away mine iniquity? for now shall I sleep in the dust; and thou shalt seek me in the morning, but I shall not be."

Several things are outstanding about this second speech of Job. The first thing is the honest confession of sin, in verse 20, which the [ASV ]{.class_3759}(1901) and the ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} have perverted into a question instead of a confession. Both of these apostate corruptions (the former recommended by John R. Rice) got the reading from Origen's [Septuagint]{.class_3759}. There is no "lF" in any Hebrew text known to man. The Greek word (not Hebrew) "ei" was inserted at Alexandria by Origen and others because they could not imagine Job suddenly confessing directly to God in the middle of the discourse, but as we have said (under 5:8 and 6:2), Job's conversations are not to be patterned after the Athenian "Stoa" where all is icely nice and cooly refined. Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown are afraid to follow the [ASV ]{.class_3759}here, but in their commenting, they still make the whole verse a question by conjoining "sinned" to the second clause, so it reads: "What sin shall I do\...?"

Very typical. Very typical of all "godly, intelligent" Fundamentalists who are so worried about being "nuts" that they thumb their nose at God Almighty every time they feel like it. Typical. ["A double-minded man is unstable in all his ways."]{.class_3760}

Next, we should notice Job's own double-mindedness at this time. In the midst of his grief and agony, he cries that he will not come up out of the grave again (vss. 8--9); however, in 19:26 Job (in a more inspired confession) cries out that he will see God "in the flesh" after worms destroy his body. However, even in verses 8 and 9 Job is careful to say that a man will not come up out of the grave to ["return\...to his house"]{.class_3760} and ["his place"]{.class_3760} (vs. 10). Job knows that the resurrection of the body lies in the long distant future and that at that time his "house and place" will no longer be there.

"[I will complain in the bitterness of my soul\...my soul chooseth strangling\...let me alone\...depart from me\...let me alone till I swallow down my spittle"]{.class_3760} (vss. 11, 15--16, and 19). If the unpardonable sin is telling God to get away from you and leave you alone, Job "crossed the deadline" about five times in a minute. (It is a good thing that God did not take Job as seriously as a Roman Catholic takes the "sacraments.")

But Job is merely speaking what millions have thought and still think. For example, in verse 11 Job is speaking for the majority of bitter, educated Agnostics and Communists of today (1800--1980), the majority of bitter, poverty-stricken slaves of the Roman Empire (100 B. C.--A.D. 200), and millions of brilliant skeptics in every age who despised authority (Jude 8). In his terrible state, he is simply speaking his mind and "telling it like it is." Dr. Wilson says that in elephantiasis there are "deep seated burning pains, sometimes of the bone or joint\...these pains are greatest at night; they prevent sleep" (Quain, p. 817). Of the worms that cover Job, Dr. Quain has said that they were called "filaria sanquinis hominis." They were long and thread-like, colored white with no markings. Covered with worms and in burning pain, Job lodges seven complaints against God between verse 12 and verse 21, and some of them are so horribly logical that it would almost make an Atheist out of a Catholic if he read them!

"[Why dost thou not pardon my transgression, and take away mine iniquity?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 21). Good question. Job has admitted that he is not perfect (9:21), that he cannot justify himself (9:20), and that he is stupid (9:2).

Once more we should take stock and notice how faithfully the [Book of Job ]{.class_3759}has been recorded. With the same breath that Job spoke of these things, he said 27:5 and 31. You see, it is exactly as we stated the matter in our Preface: Real suffering, pain, and bereavement do not choose logical or legal ways to express themselves. When Henley writes Invictus, he is not talking like a man in his right mind. When Wurmbrand preaches to his one-man audience (God) in the dungeons of Romania, he is not always coherent, but then again, he is not supposed to be. Let's see you stay "coherent" when someone has strapped you (blindfolded) over a rail and is beating the soles of your bare feet off with a steel rod! One night Wurmbrand heard a maniacal laughter in his cell. He recognized the sound and knew what to expect, because several of his friends who had gone crazy in the darkness of solitary confinement had been straightjacketed ahead of him. It was now his own voice he was listening to. And you'd better beware of any fair-weather friend or parade-ground soldier who tries to alter the Book of Job so that Job will always "make sense." Job isn't supposed to make sense in his condition.

Why doesn't God pardon a man and take away his iniquity?

I mean, the Lord [finally ]{.class_3761}did it (see Heb. 8:12 and Rom. 3:24--26), but what good was that NOW while Job is sitting on an ash heap? We cannot take away our sins---not even the blood of bulls and goats can do that (Heb. 10:4). Then if God doesn't do it, who can? And if God doesn't, how is it to be done? Electrotherapy? Hydrotherapy? Lobotomies? "Christian" Science? Group Therapy? Sensitivity training? LSD? "Values clarification?" The confessional? Well, if any of those means worked, why is it that everyone who uses them (or counts on them for results) cannot tell you anything certain about life after death with the God who created life? If these methods worked, would the ["worshippers"]{.class_3762} have ["any more conscience of sins"]{.class_3762} (Heb. 10:2)? You cannot find one unregenerate psychiatrist, priest, dope addict, or professor in America who can claim 1 John 5:10--13 with a clear conscience. God didn't do their "pardoning"---they [pretended ]{.class_3761}they had it when they [didn't.]{.class_3761}

"[What is man, that thou shouldest magnify him?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 17). David asks the same question in Psalm 8:4 and 144:3. But when Job and David ask it, they have something entirely different in mind than when Niebuhr, Tillich, Updike, Fromm, and Dostoyevsky ask it. The modern Religio-Psychologico-International-Humanist is always on the "hoorah for Man" kick, and whether he goes as far as "Man is the measure of all things" (Protagoras) or not, at least he will leave out the Bible everytime as the measuring stick for man. Some of them---the more socialistic---like to pull out a verse here and there to prove something or other, but if you asked any of them for a verse-by-verse exposition of Romans, they would have to have a miracle take place in their lives before they could attempt it. David and Job are both approaching "Man" from the negative standpoint. ["What is man"]{.class_3762} since he is so small, so filthy (25:4), so insignificant, so abominable (15:16), and so helpless (7:20)? Made ["a little lower than the angels"]{.class_3762} (Psa. 8:5, and getting lower ever since), "man" is on the verge of destroying his own planet before he has been here long enough to learn how to live. The "scientists" (if you will pardon the misnomer) have stuck 1,000,000 to 13,000,000 years onto his sojourn so it will appear that "man" has "come a long way," but we know a bummer when we see it. Made ["a little lower than the angels"]{.class_3762} (Psa. 8:5, which shows the angels are in pretty rough shape), man---in the aggregate---cannot stay alive, he cannot keep his environment clean, he cannot maintain peace without continual killing, he cannot stay sober, he cannot understand the word of God, he cannot stop sinning, he cannot save his soul, he cannot find out where he is going at death, he cannot obey God's orders when he gets them, he cannot be satisfied with food and clothing, and he can no more buy happiness or good health or forgiveness of sins than he can play basketball on the top of Mt. Everest.

"[Am I a sea, or a whale\...?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 12). Adam Clarke's reference to the drowning of Pharaoh here is about as appropriate as his comment about Moses and the Israelites in 15:19. Neither comment is intelligible. Adam Clarke began his commentary by insisting that the [Book of Job ]{.class_3761}had to be written AFTER the Exodus; so this compels him to make constant reference to the Israelites coming out of Egypt. Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown, at least, are not deceived by the passage in 15:19, for they refer it to Genesis 11. But one can see at a glance that the ["fathers"]{.class_3762} of Job 15:18 are SHEM, HAM, and JAPHETH.

However, if the reader has had any background in Greek or Hebrew, he can understand how men like Clarke get into these aborted expositions; the process by which this is done is known as "higgledy-piggledy derangement" (which we will refer to in the future as HPD). ["Am I a sea, or a whale\...?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 12). Clarke saw the word ["tanim"]{.class_3759} (Hebrew for ["whale"]{.class_3760}) in Ezekiel 32:2, and upon noting that the reference was to Pharaoh, he quickly ran it to the ["tanim"]{.class_3759} of Job 7:12 and concluded that the reference was to Pharaoh getting drowned. There are fifteen things wrong with this HPD, but we will only list five.

1\. Pharaoh was only ["AS"]{.class_3760} a whale (Ezek. 32:2); He was not a [whale]{.class_3759}.

2\. The Pharaoh of Ezekiel 32 is NOT the Pharaoh of the Exodus.

3\. The Pharaoh of Ezekiel 32 was [alive]{.class_3759}---not drowned---when God likened him to a whale.

4\. Job wouldn't have known what God said to Ezekiel about a Pharaoh living after the death of Solomon, unless the Lord was doing a little HPD Himself.

5\. There is no reference to anyone "drowning" within twenty verses of Job 7:12 in either direction.

This is a good sample of "higgledy-piggledy derangement" (HPD), and there are many more in the writings of the "godly, dedicated Conservatives" of 1880 to 1980 who devoted their lives to overthrowing the Holy Bible. Typical of this HPD is the work by John R. Rice in his "commentary" on Matthew, where we are told that Christ was praying that the Devil wouldn't kill Him before He got out of Gethsemane and that this death was the "cup" about which He was praying. Another fine example is Kenneth Wuest's work on 2 Thessalonians 2, and the booklets by Spiros Zodhiates are so full of HPD that you couldn't run three pages without some "Greek nugget" overthrowing the words of truth: See John 1:13 or Hebrews 6:1--6 for example, in these works.

Kenneth Taylor makes no attempt at all to "paraphrase" ["sea"]{.class_3760} in the text but simply omits it altogether (Rev. 22:19), and the ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} is filled with "paraphrases" that are nothing but phrases cut out of the Bible by Jehudi's knife (Jer. 36:23).

God does "keep a watch" on whales pretty closely, for the whale of Matthew 12:40 swallowed one of God's prophets and spit him back up after a prayer meeting (see Jonah 2:1--8 and comments). (And don't let some silly Conservative tell you that it wasn't a whale just because "ketos" \[Greek\] "couldn't mean" that. Robertson, Machen, Davis, and Gregory didn't know what they were talking about.) Furthermore, the Lord keeps a watch on rivers (Rev. 9:14) as well as seas (36:30, 38:8, 16, and 41:31), and if you want a "sea monster" in a "sea," check the last reference. Sometimes He tells the seas when to get "stormy" (Psa. 89:9) and sometimes when to quiet down (Matt. 8:24, 26--27).

Job has lost his appetite, he suffers from insomnia, and when he finally drops off into sleep through sheer exhaustion (vs. 13), he has nightmares (vs. 14) in technicolor. Confession of sin does not solve it, so Job comes to the only sensible conclusion that a man in such circumstances can come to: He has been abandoned of God, and the quicker he drops dead the better off he will be. (Notice how acceptable this conclusion would have been to his wife if she had been there at the time.)

We had a preacher study at our Institute who tried (as a young man) to find Christ three times, and every time the person who could have led him to the Lord balled-up the works. The first one had him come forward and kneel, and didn't tell him how to get saved, the second one tried to convince him he was already saved and only needed "rededicating," and the third one told him he was predestinated one way or another; so he arrived at the rational conclusion that he was predestinated to be damned to Hell. Having abandoned ship and "having given up all hope of ever being saved," the young man climbed one of the tallest hills in the town where he lived. He then went up into the highest building on that hill and spent a good twenty minutes shaking his fist at Heaven and cursing God and telling Him what he thought of Him. In less than six weeks that young man was born again, and now he is in the ministry. You see, sometimes the "only rational conclusion" is an [irrational blunder]{.class_3759}. (See almost anything Marx wrote or Lenin said.)

[]{#IMh58iSMTFKKU0J9qyaeHw810.xhtml}

CHAPTER 8

8:1 Then answered Bildad the Shuhite, and said,

2 How long wilt thou speak these things? and how long shall the words of thy mouth be like a strong wind?

3 Doth God pervert judgment? or doth the Almighty pervert justice?

4 If thy children have sinned against him, and he have cast them away for their transgression;

5 If thou shouldest seek unto God betimes, and make thy supplication to the Almighty;

6 If thou wert pure and upright; surely now he would awake for thee, and make the habitation of thy righteousness prosperous.

7 Though thy beginning was small, yet thy latter end should greatly increase.

Now Bildad the Shuhite (vs. 1) appears as the speaker: He is supposed to represent the "traditionalist," according to some commentators. The Scofield note in the ["New"]{.class_3759} [Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_3759} (p. 577) tries to justify this by talking about Bildad's "pious platitudes" and "proverbs." However, there are no more proverbs and platitudes in Bildad's speech than there are in Job's or Elihu's. In an attempt to give the book the old Alexandrian heave-ho and computerize it into a systematic scheme, the dead orthodox Conservatives have largely invented their format.

Bildad's approach is very clear: "Stop spouting off about your troubles; God doesn't make any mistakes. If your children sinned, they deserved to die, and if you would get right and seek God and clean up your life, He would prosper your latter end and increase your prosperity." (And so the Lord does!) What Bildad says is true and his remarks are not in the least to be classified as "pious platitudes." For example: God does NOT pervert judgment (vs. 3) or justice (vs. 3). Job's words [are ]{.class_3759}like ["strong wind"]{.class_3760} (vs. 2) by Job's own admission (see 6:26), and Job's ["latter end"]{.class_3760} (vs. 7) [was ]{.class_3760}greatly increased (42:6--14). The only real mistake that Bildad makes is the "TIME mistake" (see 1:21 and 2:13) which many of us make. Bildad says ["if thou wert pure and upright surely now he would awake for thee\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 6). No, you don't hustle God into anything, and God is not a glorified bellboy just waiting to bring your breakfast up to your bedroom on a silver platter. And this concept of "Prayer---Asking and Receiving" must be guarded against, for some of its sincerest advocates, who write with joyful, glorious, "victorious" pens in their books, write with the hypocritical, vitriolic, twisted pen of carnality when they write private correspondence attacking a brother in Christ or trying to defend their own perversions of the word of God. Don't you think for a minute that all of the bounding, joyous, victorious, "happy writings" on "answered prayers" came from men who counted on God or their PRAYERS to get them through. Some of these books were penned by authors who refused to publicize the sermons of men they hated (and some of these men were greater soul winners than the author himself). Some cut corners with Liberals by promoting men connected with them, set themselves up as more authoritative than the Bible, refused to sell advertisements which exalted the [Authorized Version]{.class_3759}, and, when faced with Bible issues, began to whine about "soul winning" being the real issue---after tearing Billy Graham limb from limb for his emphasis on "soul winning" at the expense of Bible issues. Don't take any book on prayer by such a man too seriously. You may not be willing to cut the corners he cut to "get your prayers answered," and you may not be willing to be that partial and that prejudiced in your dealings with the brethren; so you may not get all the prayers "answered" that he got "answered."

"[If thou wert pure"]{.class_3760} (vs. 6). There is the old Pharisee in all of his glory. ["This man, if he were a prophet would have known\..."]{.class_3762} (Luke 7:39)---LIKE ME!---who is pure and who is not. Oh yes, that's the old bit. These sweet smelling (outside), whitewashed (outside) Pharisees who wouldn't get their little pinkies dirty (Matt. 23:1--3) to lift a sack of potatoes or a sack of cement would stay out of a Gentile building (John 18:28) to be "pure." Yes, and they murdered God's Son (Acts 7;52) and would have murdered His friends (Acts 12:1--6, Acts 23:12) if they could have done it.

Now, all of the Pharisees were "Fundamentalists" as far as the [orthodox fundamentals ]{.class_3759}of their [own religious beliefs ]{.class_3759}were concerned. Like the Roman Catholic priests and popes, they professed to believe what an orthodox "believer" is supposed to believe. However, one cannot fail to notice the great GAP that opens between the Pharisees and the [followers of Christ]{.class_3759}. It is marked, among other ways, by three distinctions which exist today exactly as they existed then:

1\. Christ's disciples were, for the most part (84% or 92%), common, ordinary, [uneducated ]{.class_3759}(Acts 4:13), [working men and women ]{.class_3759}(Luke 4:18).

2\. Christ's disciples followed a MAN, not an [institution ]{.class_3759}or an [organization ]{.class_3759}(Matt. 5:19; 1 Cor. 11:1).

3\. Christ's disciples were taught to minister the word of God and the Holy Spirit (Gal. 3:5) to sinners (Matt. 10:1--20). Not one of them was told to administer "sacraments" to "Roman Catholics."

What the Pharisees couldn't stand about Peter, James, and John was their street preaching. The Pharisees wanted God shut up in a building (Acts 7:48) [which they owned and controlled]{.class_3759}. They wanted the "heavenly merits" to be deposited "in a treasury" where THEY held the purse strings (John 12:6). (Are any of you feeling uncomfortable along in here? Well, ["If thou doest well, shalt thou not be accepted?"]{.class_3760} \[Gen. 4:7\].)

I had a fine meeting in a church in Texas one time, but before going there, one of the editors of the [Sword of the Lord ]{.class_3759}wrote "a brother" a nice sweet "Christian" letter, warning him about having "Brother Ruckman" in---this man calls himself a "Doctor," although he never earned a graduate degree in his life---because "Brother Ruckman" was "unclean." (He did not write this in his first letter but in a later letter to a good friend of mine here in town.) Now, this "godly, devoted, dedicated" nincompoop thought that if the church in Texas had had a clean man come in for a meeting---like HIM---it would have been a much better meeting. This particular editor writes a column of illustrations for preachers: 65 percent of them deal with sex. (See Matt. 12:34 and comments.) What this preacher meant was: "If you live like I do, you're [clean]{.class_3759}, and if you violate my standards, you are [unclean]{.class_3759}." He thought he was God. And the pity of it all was that this poor sinner's theory on Romans 7:1--4 was about as HPD as an exposition can get; he treated the passage exactly as it is handled by the Roman Catholic Church---"orthodoxy" if you ever heard it. ["If thou wert pure"]{.class_3760} (vs. 6). Well, Job was a good bit cleaner than his buddies, if we are able to believe Job 42:8, and where Job had skin trouble they had HEART and MOUTH disease.

And there is more than one modern Christian Celebrity who lives in mortal terror that his bond program will not get paid off, that his attendance will drop, that he'll "hurt his testimony," that his offerings will diminish, that he'll lose radio stations, or that he will lose his teaching staff; and often, he makes his decisions with [those things ]{.class_3759}in mind---not the Lord or the Bible. Heart trouble. Clean hands---dirty heart. Job had [dirty hands ]{.class_3759}and a [clean heart.]{.class_3759}

8:8 For enquire, I pray thee, of the former age, and prepare thyself to the search of their fathers:

9 (For we are but of yesterday, and know nothing, because our days upon earth are a shadow:)

10 Shall not they teach thee, and tell thee, and utter words out of their heart?

11 Can the rush grow up without mire? can the flag grow without water?

12 Whilst it is yet in his greenness, and not cut down, it withereth before any other herb.

13 So are the paths of all that forget God; and the hypocrite's hope shall perish:

14 Whose hope shall be cut off, and whose trust shall be a spider's web.

15 He shall lean upon his house, but it shall not stand: he shall hold it fast, but it shall not endure.

16 He is green before the sun, and his branch shooteth forth in his garden.

17 His roots are wrapped about the heap, and seeth the place of stones.

18 If he destroy him from his place, then it shall deny him, saying, I have not seen thee.

19 Behold, this is the joy of his way, and out of the earth shall others grow.

The first verse in the section pretty well fixes the scholars who are trying to jack the [Book of Job ]{.class_3759}up into the fourteenth or fifteenth century B.C. "The former age" is certainly no reference to the Exodus or the Tower of Babel. ["The former age"]{.class_3760} is defined in Job 11:16, 12:15, 9:5--6, 21:14, 22:15--16, 31:33, and 4:18. It includes angels ["charged with folly"]{.class_3760} (see Gen. 6:1--4), a ["foundation overflown with a flood"]{.class_3760} (Gen. 8, 9), the sin of Adam (Gen. 3), and the ["drying up of the flood waters"]{.class_3760} preceding the advent of Shem, Ham, and Japheth. These things are fresh in the minds of Bildad, Eliphaz, Elihu, and Job, and nothing could be more ridiculous or pitiful than the twisted way in which the commentators try to make the[ "former age"]{.class_3760} refer to the Exodus or the Book of Joshua.

The ["fathers"]{.class_3760} (vs. 8) of the ["former age"]{.class_3760} were Shem, Ham, and Japheth; and if there is one lesson that Shem, Ham, and Japheth learned while they were "helping daddy" build the ark (Gen. 6--7 and comments), it was the lesson of Job 8:13--22, which now follows. Observe further, that Moses has written nothing for anyone to learn. To the contrary, the teaching medium (vs. 10) is ["they"]{.class_3760} (i.e., ["the fathers"]{.class_3760} of vs. 8) who uttered the words ["out of their heart"]{.class_3760} (vs. 10). What follows states that the hypocrite's ["hope shall perish"]{.class_3760} (vs. 13). It will perish as a rush with no mud to grow in, as a reed trying to grow on a desert, as a herb that withers (vs. 12), or as a spider's web is wiped out with a stick or a broom (vs. 14). Verse 14 is aimed right at Job and is designed to contradict his speech of Job 7:6. Verse 18 is another potshot taken at the suffering saint, and this one is designed to deny what Job said in Job 7:10. Bildad is on the firing range. He is taking "pot-shots" at what he considers to be a "varmint." (Verses 13--18 are to be applied directly to the Antichrist and the "place of stones," as given in Ezek. 28:14, in spite of the efforts of the commentators to obliterate the cross-references.)

Observe how the present generation (Job's generation) confesses that their lives are ["but of yesterday" ]{.class_3760}(vs. 9), and that they know ["nothing"]{.class_3760} (vs. 9). Why is this confession made right after ["the search of their fathers"]{.class_3760} (vs. 8), unless it is due to the fact that the "fathers of the former age" lived to be well over 900 years old, while the "fathers" of Job's age were lucky to make 250? The scriptural logic is inescapable. The memory of the terrible events of Genesis chapters 6--10 is still fresh in the minds of the Temanites, whereas the times of Moses and Pharaoh are not even under consideration.

The hypocrite will lean on his house (vs. 15), and it will turn out to support him about as well as a spider web will hold him up (vs. 14). He may hold his house fast---in the sense of taking care of his household (see Acts 16:31 and Heb. 11:7)---but his progeny will be destroyed (vs. 4) even down to his nephews (Job 18:19). Again, the Holy Spirit Himself will show us the way to Isaiah 14:22, where the context is "Satan manifest in the flesh" (Isa. 14:9--15); this clearly puts the doctrinal content of the passage forward into the Great Tribulation, and it reminds us again how comical the works of Payne and Biederwolf are when they undertake to discuss prophecy in "the Scriptures of truth." Running back and forth to Hebrew and Greek words or running back and forth to "traditional interpretations" or running around famous Christian "celebrities," before turning to the Book itself and its Author, will terminate in a theological disaster every time. (For example, see any of the apostate nonsense published by [The Plain Truth]{.class_3759}, out of Australia; especially the ridiculous nonsense about sinners receiving the Holy Spirit when they are baptized in WATER. There isn't a case in the New Testament after Acts 2:38 where any sinner receives the Holy Spirit by being baptized in water, and none of the Apostles in Acts chapter 2 were baptized in water, according to the Jewish formula given there---Acts 2:38.) And woe be to the executive promoter or institutional "motivator" who recommends two ["reliable translations"]{.class_3759} so that his institution can be the final arbiter between them. Woe unto you Christian "wheels" who think that you know more than God Almighty simply because you desire the preeminent place over the English Text of the Protestant Reformation!

"[He is green before the sun"]{.class_3760} (vs. 16). (See Psa. 37:2 and 37:5.) ["His roots are wrapped about the heap"]{.class_3762} (vs. 17). [Practically]{.class_3761}, the figure is that of a growing plant (vs. 16) whose roots go down into the heap of decayed debris (Cf. Jer. 9:11, 51:37; Isa. 17:1; Deut. 13:16, etc.) and past buried stones (Cf. Josh. 7:26, 8:29). If God destroys this man from ["his place"]{.class_3762} (vs. 18)---notice the Antichrist's place in Acts 1:25 and Revelation 9:11---it will be like a plant that is ["rooted up"]{.class_3762} (Matt. 3:10), and the ["place"]{.class_3762} will say: ["I have not seen thee"]{.class_3762} (vs. 18). The ["joy"]{.class_3762} of the Antichrist's way (or any "wicked man" in the context) is a lie; it is an illusion, both temporary and deceitful. ["Out of the earth shall others grow"]{.class_3762} (vs. 19) is clearly a reference to people springing up like plants (Matt. 13:38) to replace the Antichrist's crowd (Cf. Isa. 17:11; Psa. 72:16, and Prov. 11:28).

The ["dream is certain, and the interpretation thereof sure"]{.class_3760} (Dan. 2:45), and if Payne and Biederwolf (with the other four hundred) cannot find those prophetic references, it is because they (with the other four hundred) decided they were fit to sit in judgment on the [Authorized Text ]{.class_3759}before they helped their "brethren" destroy it.

Looking back over the passage, the majority of Christian educators and scholars have decided that God could improve His Book in the following ways:

1\. [Taylor ]{.class_3759}([The Dead Bible]{.class_3759}) has substituted "history books" (written books) for the ["fathers"]{.class_3760} (vs. 8) and then has carefully removed any reference to the ["former age"]{.class_3760} from the text. After altering the proper noun ["they"]{.class_3760} (vs. 10) to a neuter ("wisdom"), Taylor dresses up the hypocrite of verse 13 so he is no longer a hypocrite. If this weren't bad enough, Taylor has also destroyed Bildad's reference to the death of Job's children by altering the words ["cast them away"]{.class_3760} (vs. 4) to "punished them." (And you understand you are supposed to believe in this "bible" because 8,000,000 suckers bought a copy, and it is almost as easy to read as Lil' Abner---although not quite!)

2\. The [New Scofield Reference Bible ]{.class_3759}subtly replaces the [Authorized Text ]{.class_3759}with the grossly corrupt [ASV ]{.class_3759}of 1901 (recommended by Oakland City College, Rockmount, North Park, Pikeville, Nyack, Messiah, Manchester, Mississippi, Judson, Stetson, Hardin-Simmons, Pensacola Christian, Liberty University, Baylor, Mercer, Piedmont, Furman, Tennessee Temple, Cedarville, Fort Worth, Dallas, Moody, Wheaton, Whitworth Bible College, Detroit, Circleville, and the next ten thousand) and translates ["judgment"]{.class_3760} (vs. 3) as "justice," and then alters ["justice"]{.class_3760} (same verse) to "righteousness." And of course this is done after telling the poor boob who bought the book that it was the "[Authorized Version]{.class_3759}." Determined to reinstate the pitiful flop of 1901 ([ASV]{.class_3759}), the [New Scofield ]{.class_3759}editors have also replaced the [Authorized Text ]{.class_3759}with the [ASV ]{.class_3759}text in verses 14 and 18. You see, the faculty members of the Conservative schools must beat the drum for some version other than the [AV ]{.class_3759}(1611), otherwise that Book (the [AV ]{.class_3759}of 1611) would be the [final authority ]{.class_3759}in all matters of faith and practice. To prevent this from taking place, the faculties have agreed to continually recommend the [ASV ]{.class_3759}(in some form) to the body of Christ; it is one of the most God-forsaken pieces of ecclesiastical trash that ever filled a garbage can. And the radical fanaticism of the extremists who recommend it is so close to the lunatic fringe that they actually would slip the [ASV ]{.class_3759}readings into a [King James Bible]{.class_3759}---see the [New Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_3759}---and then lie forthright and deliberately about the text. (And if you don't believe THAT, ask God to forgive you for being a two-faced hypocrite yourself.)

3\. The [Pulpit Commentary ]{.class_3759}is just as anxious to get rid of the date of the authorship of Job as Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown, Duhm, Peake, Budde, or Heavenor. It says that verse 8 really means "from generation to generation since the remotest times." Notice how neatly this plural ("times") destroys any former ["age"]{.class_3760} (singular). Nice work; why don't you run a roulette wheel in Las Vegas? They are always looking for ambitious young crooks.

4\. The absurd [Septuagint ]{.class_3759}(which Augustine and Origen thought was "verbally inspired") alters ["age"]{.class_3760} to "generation" (vs. 8), alters ["hypocrite"]{.class_3760} to "ungodly" (vs. 13), and inserts "hast thou not seen such things?" into verse 18 instead of ["I have not seen thee."]{.class_3760}

5\. [Dummelow ]{.class_3759}(in his fool's paradise) says that "everywhere the [RV ]{.class_3759}should be used when reading the Book of Job." Which is the equivalent of saying: "Everywhere [Mein Kampf ]{.class_3759}should be used when reading Luther's [Preface to the Book of Romans.]{.class_3759}" The [RV ]{.class_3759}is about as "reliable" (twentieth century cliché used as a substitute for "authoritative") as the Associated Press.

6\. [Brother Kline ]{.class_3759}([Wycliffe Bible Commentary]{.class_3759}) likes the [RSV ]{.class_3759}(1952) reading for verse 4, which agrees with the Septuagint in getting rid of the expression ["cast them away."]{.class_3760}

7\. [Fausset ]{.class_3759}gets through the chapter without stumbling too many times, but this is only because he ignores the scholars in the majority of cases and gives us a good list of cross-references (Psa. 78:4, for example, as a cross-reference to Job 8:5 and Psalm 73:23). [Umbreit ]{.class_3759}and [Maurer ]{.class_3759}mess enough with the chapter to make a custard pie out of it.

We will say it again, and repeat it again and again "till death do us part," that the [AV ]{.class_3759}text of the Protestant Reformation is always more scientific and authoritative than any set of books (or "bibles") written by any group of Fundamental, Conservative, Neo-orthodox, or Liberal scholars who ever lived, in ANY period of church history. Where four thousand saved scholars agree on correcting the [AV ]{.class_3759}text, you will do quite well to ignore them---completely.

8:20 Behold, God will not cast away a perfect man, neither will he help the evil doers:

21 Till he flll thy mouth with laughing, and thy lips with rejoicing.

22 They that hate thee shall be clothed with shame; and the dwelling place of the wicked shall come to nought.

All is clear in the English text. God does NOT cast away Job, and He does not help the ["evildoers"]{.class_3760} (vs. 1). Furthermore, God DOES fill Job's mouth with laughing and rejoicing (vs. 21), and those who hated him are clothed with shame (vs. 22); and the ["dwelling place of the wicked shall come to nought"]{.class_3760} (vs. 22) in Revelation 19:21, when the Lord Jesus returns.

The acid in Bildad's remarks is apparent, for he has just said that God has ["cast\...away"]{.class_3760} Job's children (vs. 4); if they were what they should have been (vs. 20), God would not have cast them away. This must have been like an ice pick stabbing the diseased sufferer, for he had always taken precautions to guard his children against this very thing (1:5). Job had already "covered them," if they had not been ["perfect"]{.class_3760} (vs. 20). It is a little like working (unsuccessfully) on an automobile for three days, while keeping all the disassembled parts in a clean, oiled pan before reinserting them, and then having some fool come along and tell you that the reason you can't get the thing running is because you have let the parts get dirty.

[]{#IMh58iSMTFKKU0J9qyaeHw865.xhtml}

CHAPTER 9

9:1 Then Job answered and said,

2 I know it is so of a truth: but how should man be just with God?

3 If he will contend with him, he cannot answer him one of a thousand.

4 He is wise in heart, and mighty in strength: who hath hardened himself against him, and hath prospered?

5 Which removeth the mountains, and they know not: which overturneth them in his anger.

6 Which shaketh the earth out of her place, and the pillars thereof tremble.

7 Which commandeth the sun, and it riseth not; and sealeth up the stars.

8 Which alone spreadeth out the heavens, and treadeth upon the waves of the sea.

9 Which maketh Arcturus, Orion, and Pleiades, and the chambers of the south.

10 Which doeth great things past finding out; yea, and wonders without number.

Job has begun to think and consider what has been said (vs. 2), but it is not long before he lapses back into the old refrain (see vss. 12--22). Job begins with ["I know it is of a truth."]{.class_3760} That is, he recognizes that what Bildad said (vss. 9--22) is so. The pre-Deluge people, who danced with the harp and the organ (21:14), told God to "depart" from them, and so Bildad could not help but apply this to Job, because Job had said:[ "How long wilt thou not depart from me"]{.class_3760} (7:19). But Job's infidelity is in an entirely different context. The pre-Deluge sinners wanted God to quit "striving with them" (Gen. 6:1--6) because He was interfering with [race-mixing ]{.class_3759}(Jude 6--7), [group therapy, Satanism]{.class_3759} (2 Pet. 2:4), and [outerspace travel ]{.class_3759}(Luke 10:18; Heb. 1:14; Gen. 6:1--6). Job merely wants God to "get off his back" because he feels that God is killing him by a slow torture.

"[If he will contend with him\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 3) like the Sadducees and Pharisees did (Matt. 21:23!), ["\...he cannot answer him one of a thousand."]{.class_3760} And how true this is; did you ever study the discourses between Christ and the religious celebrities of his day? Did you count how many times He answered them (Mark 2:25, 7:5--8, 10:3--5) and how many times they could not answer Him (Matt. 22:46; Mark 3:4)? When the Bible says that ["every mouth will be stopped"]{.class_3760} and the whole world become ["guilty before God"]{.class_3760} (Rom. 3:19), it is not talking about somebody's "faith." It is stating a scientific and historical fact. You'll have plenty of time at the White Throne Judgment to ask all the questions you like (see Prov. 20:8--9), and it will be YOUR turn to answer some questions. And don't think that simply because a man is a college professor (or nuclear physicist) with an IQ of 150, who has studied "religions" for sixty years, he will be able to answer the first question asked him. The Lord has a way of "taking the wise in their own craftiness" (1 Cor. 1--3).

"[Who hath hardened himself against him, and hath prospered?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 4). Did Cain? Did the ante-Diluvians? Will the Devil (see 41:24)? Could Pharaoh (Exod. 7:3 and comments)? Did Judas? Will YOU? (See Prov. 29:1 and comments.)

Job knows about the power of the Godhead (Rom. 1:20) by the things that are "made" (Psa. 19:1--4), just like any heathen in Africa or Asia (or South Carolina, Alabama, or Minnesota). God:

1\. Removes mountains and overturns them (Rev. 16:20; Isa. 64:1).

2\. Shakes the earth and makes it tremble (Isa. 24:19--21).

3\. Prevents the sun from rising (see Exod. 10:21 and comments).

4\. Seals the stars (Acts 2:19--20).

5\. Spreadeth out the heavens (Heb. 1:10--12, Psa. 19:1--6).

6\. Treads upon the waves of the "sea" (Psa. 135:6, Hab. 3:15), and that ain't the Atlantic or the Mediterranean, bud!

7\. Makes Arcturus, Orion, Pleiades, and the "chambers of the south" (38:32).

8\. Does great things past finding out and without number (Rom. 11:33).

Now these are some of the things that God calls to Job's attention in Chapters 38--41, and it is rather remarkable that God bawls out Job for not knowing these things after Job has just confessed them. But here, again, we run into the familiar "consecration gap" between knowing a thing to be so[ in the head]{.class_3759} and acting upon the knowledge from the [heart ]{.class_3759}(Heb. 11:1--4). Any saved man on this earth knows that God is Omniscient, Omnipresent, Omnipotent, and that His will is going to have to be our first consideration; however, where this will crosses our will (see Matt. 26:42 and comments), we suddenly find ourselves involved in contradictions like this: Couldn't the God of the universe be mistaken about my income? Or my health? Yes, that is how it goes, and although most saved Germans have no trouble in believing that God controls floods, fire, hurricanes, tidal waves, and rainstorms, they have a terrible time believing that God is able to fix washing machines, find tooth brushes, locate lost keys, provide handkerchiefs, and cure headaches!

"[Which maketh Arcturus, Orion and Pleiades, and the chambers of the south"]{.class_3760} (vs. 9). (How did Job know about the ["chambers of the south,"]{.class_3760} which are invisible from Palestine?) Now, the words have been given in a variety of orders by the [Septuagint]{.class_3759}, the [Vulgate]{.class_3759}, the Targums, the Syriac, the Arabic, [Coverdale]{.class_3759}, [Becke]{.class_3759}, and the modern European versions but it is best to stick to the [Authorized Text]{.class_3759}, as always. ["Arcturus"]{.class_3760} is [Ursa Major ]{.class_3759}("The Big Bear") which revolves around the Polar star; it is also commonly called "the Big Dipper," while the Arabians call it, "The three daughters of the Bier"---the other four stars in the constellation make up the "litter" that is being "carried." Brinkley supposes that the stars mentioned here are Taurus and Scorpio, and then he figures that the time of Job (from the appearing of these stars) is about 818 years after the flood; that is, before the time of Abraham (a date of about 2000 B.C.).

"[Orion"]{.class_3760} is a Hebrew translation of "Marduk" ("Marad"---rebellious), and this constellation was called the Mighty Hunter, as it referred directly to Nimrod (from "Nimur"---rebel), who was the thirteenth from Adam. The Arabs call him "the fool." ["Pleiades"]{.class_3760} is the "heap of stars" comprising a Southern constellation. There are six stars which can be seen with the naked eye, and one of them ([Alycone]{.class_3759}) has increased in brightness; it was not even listed in Ptolemy's catalogue of systems. These six stars are sometimes referred to as "the daughters of Atlas." In ancient times, [Pleiades ]{.class_3759}stood for the EAST, [Orion ]{.class_3759}stood for the WEST. [Arcturus ]{.class_3759}for the NORTH and ["Mazzaroth" ]{.class_3759}(Job 38:32) for the SOUTH. (In 2 Kings 23:5 the word is translated as "planets.")

9:11 Lo, he goeth by me, and I see him not: he passeth on also, but I perceive him not.

12 Behold, he taketh away, who can hinder him? who will say unto him, What doest thou?

13 If God will not withdraw his anger, the proud helpers do stoop under him.

14 How much less shall I answer him, and choose out my words to reason with him?

15 Whom, though I were righteous, yet would I not answer, but I would make supplication to my judge.

16 If I had called, and he had answered me; yet would I not believe that he had hearkened unto my voice.

17 For he breaketh me with a tempest, and multiplieth my wounds without cause.

18 He will not suffer me to take my breath, but filleth me with bitterness.

You see, it is terribly hard to keep on dwelling on the "greatness and goodness of God" when everything in your flesh and bones tells you that He either cannot, or will not, deliver YOU. At verse 10, Job gets that far-away look in his eyes, and the first person singular shows up in his conversation immediately: verse 11---["me\...I\...I\...."]{.class_3760} Once we get our eyes off the Lord (Heb. 12:2--3) the flesh reasserts itself (Rom. 6:6--20): ["How much less shall I\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 14); ["Whom, though I\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 15); ["Would I not answer..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 15); ["If I had called\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 16); ["Yet would I not believe\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 16); ["He breaketh me\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 17); ["He will not suffer me\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 18); ["\...filleth me with bitterness"]{.class_3760} (vs. 18). But who could call Job's egotism to his attention without taking their own life in their hands? When your body is one raw, aching mass of running sores and scabs and your sleep is one continual nightmare and your heart has been broken into a thousand pieces and you don't have a sympathetic friend left on earth, are you going to be able to spend all your time preaching sermons about God's greatness and goodness?

In a narrow cell, thirty feet below the ground, Richard Wurmbrand was imprisoned in solitary for three years, and in that time he never saw the sun, the moon, the stars, the snow, the flowers, or the sky. In this condition, he "preached" sermons to the Lord. When Wurmbrand preached a message to God about "God's unjust Laws" and told the Lord what was [wrong ]{.class_3759}with such commandments as "Thou shalt not steal" and "Thou shalt not kill," he was not turning into an Atheist or an unbeliever; he was just trying to humor a broken heart and enjoy a little fellowship with God, while his heart was angry, bitter, and resentful. So Wurmbrand, in his messages, simply "unloads" his heart on the Lord and thus restores their fellowship. Most of the "sermons" preached at this time look like they were taken from Job 10:1--18. When Wurmbrand says, "I have no idea why I have had to suffer so much" ([Stronger Than Prison Walls]{.class_3759}, p. 156), he is telling the truth, and he couldn't have said it any better if he had taken the words right out of Job's mouth.

"[Behold, he taketh away, who can hinder him?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 12).

No one. If God wanted to "take away" your money, health, job, ministry, family, property, or your life this minute, it would be gone. ["Who will say unto him, What doest thou?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 12). Well, almost every scientist, educator, philosopher, artist, writer, politician, and pope who ever lived; but it didn't come to much. When God takes away Babylon (536 B.C.), Persia (330 B.C.), Greece (100 B.C.), Rome (A.D. 400), Spain (sixteenth century), Germany (A.D. 1945), England (same time), or the U.S.A. (A.D. 1990), asking ["What doest thou?"]{.class_3760} won't bring back one country, one dollar, one province, one prince, king, president, deputy, duke, mark, peso, sheriff, bishop, priest, or general. The clay may ask the Potter why He did what He did (Rom. 9:20), but talking won't change the shape of the clay (Jer. 18:6), unless it ruins it. In the final analysis, the counsel of the Lord shall stand (Prov. 16:4, 9, 33 and comments); and nothing the United Nations, the World Council, the Roman Catholic Church, the Federal Government, the HEW, CIA, FBI, or CFR does (or thinks) will alter a whit of it. The Lord took away Zedekiah, and he never got back (Jer. 52). The Lord took away the head of Egypt, and he never got back (Exod. 15). The Lord took the nation of Israel out of the land and made them wander for twenty-five centuries before they could run up their own flag. The Lord took Brian Sternberg's health from him (the 1960's) when Sternberg was a world champion pole vaulter. Brian broke his back doing exercises on a trampoline, and he was never able to pole vault again. However, his calamity led to his salvation, and Brian has been a faithful witness for Jesus Christ since then (Rom. 8:28). When God "took away" Saul of Tarsus, He replaced him with Paul the Apostle, and no one could say, ["What doest thou?"]{.class_3760} And if they had, it wouldn't have altered anything.

"[If God will not withdraw his anger, the proud helpers do stoop under him"]{.class_3760} (vs. 13). For example, 2 Kings 19:13; Jeremiah 52; Daniel 4; and Matthew 23:33. When God's anger is kindled (Matt. 21:41), somebody's town is going to burn (Matt. 22:7). And whether that burning comes from pagans who have been encouraged by the news media to rob, pillage, kill, and steal, or whether it comes from "scientific progress" dumping atom bombs and incendiaries, when the Lord does it ([or allows it]{.class_3759}---don't forget Job 1--2!), it will be "done up brown."

Then Job goes into one of his great declamations, and in what follows (vss. 19--22), the soul of human nature is laid bare for the centuries to behold. No man really "tells it like it is," if he doesn't tell it like Job tells it in the next nine verses. Job admits that he couldn't answer God if God put him on the spot (vs. 15). \[See Job 16:21, 23:4, and 10:15 along the same lines.\] And yet, notice how in the same breath (23:4, 5) Job cries: ["I would order my cause before him, and fill my mouth with arguments. I would know the words which he would answer me, and understand what he would say unto me"]{.class_3760}! But this is only the "self" (the ego) trying every way out to solve the problem of suffering and pain. Gautama Buddha (563--483 B.C.) took a short cut and hypnotized himself out of it by eliminating the very opposites that appear in Job's discourses. But these "opposites" are a part of life, and they are part of "reality." As Wurmbrand has so faithfully stated the matter: "Does anyone know the [whole ]{.class_3759}reality? We lived in a reality apart\...it seems strange to those who live a quiet normal life." You are not going to become "god" or turn into a "god" by pretending that opposites do not exist simply because [God is above them]{.class_3759}, although that is exactly what Buddhism, Unity, Unitarianism, and Christian Science attempt to do. To quote one of these self-deceived egotists: "One may say 'God IS' after one has truly said 'I AM'." (In view of God's revelation in Exod. 3:14, this is the equivalent of saying: "You may allow God to exist after you have claimed to be God yourself." Job is not that stupid.)

"[Though I were righteous, yet would I not answer"]{.class_3760} (vs. 15), because in "answering" you might become [unrighteous]{.class_3759}. ["But I would make supplication"]{.class_3760} (vs. 15). Job knows how frail he is; if he were given a chance to choose words with God, he would plead for mercy (see 7:21), and he winds up doing just that (42:1--4).

"[If I had called, and he had answered me; yet would I not believe that he had hearkened to my voice"]{.class_3760} (vs. 16). Very true; and if you had a dime for every time you prayed and God answered---while you were doubting---you would be able to eat roast beef tonight instead of canned dog food. And Job's case is far worse than yours, for God was breaking Job with a ["tempest"]{.class_3760} (vs. 17) and multiplying his ["wounds without cause"]{.class_3760} (vs. 17: see 2:3). Job cannot speak coherently because his throat and his breath have been affected by his disease (vs. 18). Instead of being filled with air (["my breath"]{.class_3760}), he is filled with ["bitterness"]{.class_3760} (vs. 18).

(Notice how much of this matches the crucifixion passages of Psa. 22 and Isa. 53.)

9:19 If I speak of strength, lo, he is strong: and if of judgment, who shall set me a time to plead?

20 If I justify myself, mine own mouth shall condemn me: if I say, I am perfect, it shall also prove me perverse.

21 Though I were perfect, yet would I not know my soul: I would despise my life.

22 This is one thing, therefore I said it, He destroyeth the perfect and the wicked."

Job says ["If I"]{.class_3760} seven times (vs. 16, 19, 20, 27, 29, and 30), and nearly all of the "conditional clauses" are true. ["If I speak of strength"]{.class_3760} (vs. 19) like Paul Anderson, Charles Atlas, Yuriy Vlasov, M. Schemansky, Rocky Marciano, Max Klein, Koki Noya (Sumo wrestler), Larry Czonka, Masoyama (Karate), or Sandow (weight lifter), ["he is strong"]{.class_3760} (God). The One who flung the constellations and galaxies into space is not going to be impressed with the ["legs of a man"]{.class_3760} (Psa. 147:10). If He decided to send one planet on a collision course with the earth, every line-backer in the NFL and AFL would be screaming for help like a lost child. Job's counterpart (for the Great Tribulation) was the prophet Jeremiah, and it was Jeremiah who said: ["Let not the\...mighty man glory in his might" ]{.class_3760}(Jer. 9:23).

"[If I speak\...of judgment\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 19) who will arrange the hearing? God has already appointed the day (Acts 17:31), and no one can move that day forward or backward. ["If I say, I am perfect\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 20), like a "Pentecostal," Nazarene, or Wesleyan Methodist after "sanctification" (or "eradication," ["it shall also prove me perverse"]{.class_3760} (vs. 20). John repeats the same thing when he says that if any man says that he doesn't have SIN (not "sins"), the truth is not in him (1 John 1:8). You won't get "[eradicated]{.class_3759}" till you get eradicated or [raptured]{.class_3759}. (And don't think that a misinterpretation of 1 John 3:9 will make a Bible scholar out of you anymore than it did Custer, Zodhiates, Panosian, Bell, Nutz, Wuest, Thieme, or Robertson, who all ran to the "Greek" on that verse to prove something that wasn't so.)

Our mouths are the undoing of our souls (see Prov. 19:28, 21:23, and 26:28); so, Eliphaz is right in saying that God will save the poor from their own mouths (see 5:15): this is a "great salvation" indeed. Furthermore, if you were perfect (which you are [not]{.class_3759}), you would not know your soul; like Job, you would "despise" your life.

Now, all of this is doctrinal and practical truth. As soon as a man opens his yap and attempts to justify himself by his works---like any Catholic priest or Campbellite\
elder---this man's own mouth condemns him. Knowing this, Catholic priests and Campbellite elders are very careful never to make any profession of having eternal life as a [present ]{.class_3759}possession. They must carefully avoid this Christian profession in order to pass off as being "humble." Unfortunately, the "Christian" who avoids making this profession is listed in 1 John 5:10--13 as a LIAR. You cannot say that God is a liar (1John 5:10--13) without lying yourself. If you are following James 2:24 into Hell--- pretending that your works can justify you---you will have to make a liar out of God in 1John 5:10--13, or else profess that you do have assurance of eternal life. Campbellites and Catholics can do neither; they would rather burn in a Lake of Fire on James 2:24 than go home to glory on Romans 5:4.

If a man claims to be sinless (by aborting John's writings), his very profession will prove him perverse. Nobody is sinless except the Lord Jesus Christ, and if you profess to be "sinless," your wife or your daughter will undo your profession before anyone has asked them about it for fifteen minutes.

If you were perfect---in the sense of "sinless"---you wouldn't [know ]{.class_3759}it, because the more holy you get the more unclean you know yourself to be. (Read Romans 6--7 about these matters.) Jonathan Edwards (1703--1758) said: "When I look down into my heart and see its desperate wickedness, I believe that it is a pit infinitely deeper than HELL." Jonathan Edwards lived as "clean" a life (by anyone's standards) as any man in this century. A saintly bishop of the Anglican church wrote these words back in 1780: "When I preach, I sin; when I pray, I sin. My very repentance needs to be repented of, and my tears need washing in the blood of Christ!" When a man gets on those grounds, he despises his life (vs. 21), and will even go so far as to renounce it altogether (Gal. 2:20), or seek deliverance from it (Rom. 7:24).

"[This is one thing, therefore I said it, He destroyeth the perfect and the wicked"]{.class_3760} (vs. 22). And Job is brutal but he is right. The theory of Eliphaz and Bildad is that a good man will get out unscathed and a bad man will be destroyed, but this is a half-truth with the exceptions proving the rule. Wicked or righteous (Job 10:15), you may have to undergo serious surgery; wicked or righteous, you may have to pay \$1,500 damages in a collision; wicked or righteous, you may have to go through a divorce court; wicked or righteous, you may have to spend your life in a wheel chair; wicked or righteous, you may get shot on a battlefield; wicked or righteous, you may be wounded in a saturation bombing, and wicked or righteous, you will die, and they will put your body into a hole in the ground. Job is right, and Bildad is wrong, at least on this one point.

"[Therefore I said it\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 22). Job said it by implication when he said Job 3:25 and 6:29.

9:23 If the scourge slay suddenly, he will laugh at the trial of the innocent.

24 The earth is given into the hand of the wicked: he covereth the faces of the judges thereof; if not, where, and who is he?

25 Now my days are swifter than a post: they flee away, they see no good.

26 They are passed away as the swift ships: as the eagle the hasteth to the prey.

When the ["scourge"]{.class_3760} kills someone (see Exod. 12:23), God will laugh at the trial of the innocent. That is exactly the charge that Job makes, and this is apparent by his accusation in Job 10:3, where he says that God shines on ["the counsel of the wicked."]{.class_3760} Umbreit, Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown alter the verse as much as possible, but it still survives correctly in the [AV ]{.class_3759}(1611). Taylor (Dead Bible) has removed half of the verse. The ["scourge"]{.class_3760} and the ["trial"]{.class_3760} take us again into Daniel's Seventieth Week via Isaiah 10:26 and 28:15--18. And Job's remark, in view of Proverbs 1:26, is not as fantastic as it sounds. Although God does not laugh or mock at the trial of the ["innocent"]{.class_3760} (Rev. 6:10, 11), He did promise to laugh and mock at the trial of the self-righteous (Prov. 1:30).

"[The earth is given into the hand of the wicked"]{.class_3760} (vs. 24) as it will be certainly given in Revelation 13. ["He covereth the faces of the judges thereof"]{.class_3760} (vs. 24); that is, God is responsible for the wrong judgments being dealt out. ["If not, where, and who is he?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 24). Who is messing things up if it isn't God?!

Now that is the drift of the accusation, and you cannot get around it with a ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} or a ["Good News for Modern Man"]{.class_3759} or any other substitute for the truth. If it is ["the wicked"]{.class_3760} (vs. 24) who covers the faces of the judges, who is ["the wicked"]{.class_3760}? Somebody gave the earth into the ["hand of the wicked"]{.class_3760} (vs. 24). Who was it? Well, in Job 1 and 2 and Luke 4, it is GOD ALMIGHTY, and you cannot get around that truth with a flying saucer full of Lemurians.
