During a church business meeting in Panama City, Florida (1973), there was a ripple over the fact that the prospective pastor was using a ["New" Scofield Reference Bible]{.class_3759}. When presented with the written facts on that perverse publication, the members of the congregation who wanted a non-Biblical set up said, "Well, it is recommended by Bob Jones III and John R. Rice, so it must be all right." Well now, I don't know about that. Billy Graham may recommend The ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759} as a "bible," but I don't know that that means anything when dealing with the written facts of Scripture.

One of our preacher boys was accosted by a teacher from BJU, during a street meeting in downtown Pensacola. The "doctor" immediately said: "You're a Ruckmanite! You believe the [King James Bible ]{.class_3759}is the word of God, right?"

"That's right," replied the young preacher. "I don't believe it has a mistake in it."

"Ruckman taught you that!" snarled the godly, educated, devoted, fundamental, secondary separationist. "You're following a man!"

"Do YOU believe there are errors in the [King James Bible]{.class_3761}?" asked our preacher boy.

"Of course," said the professor. "Any intelligent Fundamentalist knows that it is not a perfect translation."

"Well," said our young man, "Who taught you that? [What man are YOU following?"]{.class_3761}

Now, there is a question! There is a question that no faculty member on the staff of any university in the world can answer. You see, God didn't show you any mistakes in that Bible when you first believed it. You had to have some sinner like yourself teach you those "mistakes." And in America you never met one of these little man-pleasing, man-following, man-worshipping shrimps in your life who wouldn't accuse a real Bible believer of being a "Ruckmanite."

Well, kiddies, if those who sit at my feet to learn the Bible believe the Holy Bible is infallible and you don't, at whose feet were you sitting when you lost your faith in it and turned against it? The Lord's feet? Who do you think you are trying to bamboozle, your Aunt Sally?

"[He putteth no trust in his saints"]{.class_3760} (vs. 15). If he had trusted Paul to stay away from Jerusalem (Acts 21--27), He sure would have gotten a let-down, wouldn't He? If He had trusted Peter to obey without questioning (Acts 10:14), He sure would have been disappointed. If God has trusted Jonah to preach the first time or Moses to deliver the right message the first time or Balaam to deliver two-thirds of a message, He certainly would have "lost His faith in man," now wouldn't He? Do you think that God Almighty is as stupid as His creatures? God may entrust you with a ministry or a message, but if you think that any of us are one hundred percent trustworthy, you need to spend some time in Romans 1; 1 Corinthians 6; and Galatians 5.

"[Yea, the heavens are not clean in his sight"]{.class_3760} (vs. 15). He who ["created the heaven and the earth"]{.class_3762} (Gen. 1:1) knew all about "dust clouds" and "sun spots" and "dark nebula" before Kepler (1571--1630) or Copernicus (1473--1543) knew the difference between a magnifying glass and a looking glass. Don't you know if God refused to say the heavens were "good" when He created them (Gen. 1:8), it was because He saw something UNCLEAN in them (Eph. 6:9--12)?

15:17 I will shew thee, hear me; and that which I have seen I will declare;

18 Which wise men have told from their fathers, and have not had it:

19 Unto whom alone the earth was given, and no stranger passed among them.

20 The wicked man travaileth with pain all his days, and the number of years is hidden to the oppressor.

21 A dreadful sound is in his ears: in posterity the destroyer shall come upon him.

22 He believeth not that he shall return out of darkness, and he is waited for of the sword.

23 He wandereth abroad for bread, saying, Where is it? he knoweth that the day of darkness is ready at his hand.

24 Trouble and anguish shall make him afraid; they shall prevail against him, as a king ready to the battle.

25 For he stretcheth out his hand against God, and strengtheneth himself against the Almighty.

26 He runneth upon him, even on his neck, upon the thick bosses of his bucklers:

27 Because he covereth his face with his fatness, and maketh collops of fat on his flanks."

28 And he dwelleth in desolate cities, and in houses which no man inhabiteth, which are ready to become heaps.

29 He shall not be rich, neither shall his substance continue, neither shall he prolong the perfection thereof upon the earth.

30 He shall not depart out of darkness; the flame shall dry up his branches, and by the breath of his mouth shall he go away.

31 Let not him that is deceived trust in vanity: for vanity shall be his recompence.

32 It shall be accomplished before his time, and his branch shall not be green.

33 He shall shake off his unripe grape as the vine, and shall cast off his flower as the olive.

34 For the congregation of hypocrites shall be desolate, and fire shall consume the tabernacles of bribery.

35 They conceive mischief, and bring forth vanity, and their belly prepareth deceit.

This long section deals prophetically with the Antichrist, bringing the number of prophetic references in the [Book of Job]{.class_3759} to well over twenty before Job 19:25 can be dealt with. The peculiar blind perverseness of the Conservative and Liberal scholars, which makes them limit verses 19--35 to an indefinite "wicked man" from any group (at any time), is the same perverseness which leads most of the faculty members at Maranatha, Westmount, Whitworth, Easter, Sterling, Tabor, Simpson, Cedarville, Southeastern, and Piedmont to teach that each verse in the Bible has only ONE application. To the contrary, every verse in that Book (including the genealogies in 1 Chronicles if you insist on being obstinate) has [three ]{.class_3759}applications. [Historically]{.class_3759}, Eliphaz is discoursing to Job about wicked men. [Spiritually]{.class_3759}, the content of the passage is a warning to any man reading it, if he decides to major in sin instead of righteousness. And [Doctrinally]{.class_3759}, the passage is aimed at the One-eyed Cyclops (Zech. 11:17, Gen. 6:1--4, Dan. 10:8) of 1990--2000 A.D. about whom more is revealed in the Bible than any other single character with the exception of the Lord Jesus.

"[I will shew thee, hear me\...wise men have told from their fathers\...unto whom alone the earth was given, and no stranger passed among them"]{.class_3760} (vss. 15--19). This is a direct reference to Shem, Ham, and Japheth, and you could not possibly apply it to any other men in any other situation from Genesis 1:2 to Job chapter 15 if you believed any of the Bible at all. The ["fathers"]{.class_3762} are plural (["them"]{.class_3762}), and when the earth was given to ["them"]{.class_3762} the reference could not be to Adam or to Noah. The earth was given to ["them"]{.class_3762} when there was no one on it but THEM (vs. 19). Again, the reference is so clear and so definite that those who displace the date of authorship to the time of Moses (or later) have to do everything short of physical contortions to ignore the marker. Job and his friends have come into the world shortly after the time of the Tower of Babel; therefore the Flood, the fallen angels, Cain, and Adam are as fresh in their minds as "Watergate" was to someone in 1975.

Now, what the ["fathers"]{.class_3760} (vs. 18) passed on to their children is that information found in verses 20--35, and it can be summed up in one short sentence: "Any man who rebels against God and lives wickedly is in for a heap of trouble." The New Testament capsule of the passage says: ["The wages of sin is death"]{.class_3760} (Rom. 6:23).

Eliphaz is obviously pointing the finger at Job. Even though he uses the third person singular throughout (["he"]{.class_3760}---vss. 22--23, 25, 27, 29, 30, 33), Job gets the message (see 16:4) just as good as if Eliphaz had said "YOU."

Well, what about the "wicked man?" (See Job 18, 20.)

To begin with, ["He travaileth with pain all his days"]{.class_3760} (vs. 20) like Job is now. So, the application is a little farfetched. Wicked men travail in pain, but some of them get by with several years of comfort before the axe falls. ["The number of years is hidden to the oppressor"]{.class_3760} (vs. 20). That is, he doesn't know how much time he has to keep on with the devilment, and his conscience keeps telling him that his ["number"]{.class_3760} may be up before the sun comes up the next day. ["A dreadful sound in his ears"]{.class_3760} (vs. 21) which could mean the ["sound"]{.class_3760} of counter revolutionaries seeking to overthrow his regime or the plotting of kidnappers to get money from him or the rumors of assassinations or the planning of kinfolk to get his life insurance money. However, the ["dreadful sound"]{.class_3760} here is probabbly connected with the strange work of Isaiah 28:21 and Matthew 24:6, which see. One must never make the mistake of thinking that the Bible doesn't cover the events of A.D. 1970--2000 with the same precision that it covered the events from 4 B.C. to A.D. 33 (see Nah. 2:3--4 and comments). After all, these two periods of time are dealing with the two Advents.

"[The dreadful sound"]{.class_3760} of the twentieth century is still very much alive in the ears and minds of those who went through World War I, World War II, Korea, and "Nam." There is nothing on earth in the history of man to equal the "sounds" of twentieth-century infantry combat or twentieth-century bombing "runs." To tell the truth of the matter, the most hideous thing about modern warfare is the noise, and if there were some way to put silencers on the 88s, the 75s, the 155s, the rocket launchers, grenades, mortar shells, bombs, rifles, pistols, machine guns, flame throwers, mines, and guided missiles, the terror of making an attack or an assault would be reduced to the point where it would be dangerous for the lives of the [attacking force]{.class_3761}; it would take 85 percent of the fear out of the attack. It is the "NOISE" (see [Private]{.class_3761}, Lester Atwell, 1960) that tears your morale to shreds. Combat infantrymen react to these "sounds" for years after they have left the Theater of Operations, and some of them, to this day (after twenty-five to fifty years), wake up in bed with a cold sweat running down their face: A jet airliner has just passed over their home on the way to the airport. I have known infantrymen (ten years after the war was over) to suddenly jump up and stand upright in their beds and scream. And the nightmares these men have are not "silent movies," they are "talkies."

One of the crewmen who flew the reconnaissance runs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki (Major Claude Eatherly) went insane after the war. After alternate bouts with the Law---arrested for disorderly conduct, released, drunk, jailed, out, arrested for robbery, etc.---Eatherly wound up with no family, no money, no job, and no friends. Before things fell apart completely, Eatherly would wake up at night screaming: "Release it? Release it? Stop it! The children are burning!" Don't you know that the crew of the "Enola Gay" never forgot "the dreadful sound" after the sound waves from that A-bomb rocked their ship at twenty-eight thousand feet?

"[In prosperity the destroyer shall come upon him"]{.class_3760} (vs. 21), just like Cyrus took Belshazzar in the middle of a New Year's Eve wild party and just like God unhorsed Nebuchadnezzar while he was bragging about his posh, split-level, all-electric, air-conditioned, central-heating, four-car garage, suburban "estate" (Dan. 4:33). ["He believeth not that he shall return out of darkness"]{.class_3762} (vs. 22) is a peculiar wording when compared with verse 30: ["He shall not depart out of darkness."]{.class_3762} Now, the obvious meaning of verse 22 is that a wicked man has a hard time believing that he will return home safely after making a trip at night, for ["he is waited for of the sword"]{.class_3762} (vs. 22). That is, an assassin is laying wait to "get him." However, when we lay the passage alongside a "wicked man" who went to the Bottomless Pit (Acts 1:25; John 6:70--71; Rev. 9:11) because he was the "Son of Perdition" (2 Thess. 2:1--3; John 17:12), we find a man who WILL ["return out of darkness"]{.class_3762} and then will return to it "never to depart" (vs. 30). He comes up from the Pit (Rev. 17:8) and goes into ["perdition"]{.class_3762} (Rev. 17:11) because he is the Son of Perdition. And before anyone discards this prophetic application, he should carefully study the same ["wicked man"]{.class_3762} (Psa. 109:6--9) who is Judas Iscariot (Psa. 109:8), according to Acts 1:25. Observe further, the similarity between Psalm 109:6--19 and the material in Job on "the wicked man" (Psa. 52:1--7).

"[He wandereth abroad for bread, saying, Where is it?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 23). With no rain (Rev. 11:6) and no wheat, corn, rye, and barley, there is no bread (1 Kings 17:11--12). No bread? Then God will have to feed His people with Manna (see Mic. 7:14--15, and comments). ["He knoweth that the day of darkness is ready at his hand"]{.class_3762} (vs. 23), which is an exact reference to the Second Advent according to Zephaniah 1:14--15 and Joel 2:2--11. ["Trouble and anguish shall make him afraid"]{.class_3762} (vs. 24) as in Daniel 11:44, and ["they shall prevail against him\..."]{.class_3762} (vs. 24). Trouble and anguish will attack him like a king armed for battle (Job 6:4 and 19:12, for ["he stretcheth out his hand against God\...against the Almighty"]{.class_3762} (vs. 25) like Pharaoh, Jeroboam, Nimrod, Satan, Stalin, and any atheist or dialectical materialist who ever lived. ["He runneth upon him"]{.class_3762} (vs. 26). That is, God attacks the rebel (like Cain, Judas, etc.) and aims at his neck (Hab. 3:13), exposed above his shield (1 Sam. 17:51); this neck is to be cut when the HEAD is bruised (Gen. 3:15; Psa. 7:16, 68:21, 74:13--14; Rev. 13:3, etc.). ["The thick bosses of his bucklers"]{.class_3762} (vs. 26) are the metal studded points which project from a shield (see any picture of Saracen Cavalry or Seljuk Turks---A.D. 1000). The center iron knob about the waist of a soldier is also called a "buckler," and from this we get "belt buckle." Our word "boss" is also kin to the passage, since the word ["bosses"]{.class_3762} (Heb: ["gab"]{.class_3761}) indicates any protuberance which sticks out, or arches above its base. The "boss" is the bigshot.

"[Because he covereth his face with his fatness\...collops of fat"]{.class_3760} (vs. 27) will probably be literal as well as spiritual. Eglon is a ["fat man"]{.class_3762} (Judg. 3:17), and although the reference may be referring to "monetary and physical wealth," we should be careful about a fat face and fat hips being "spiritualized" simply because 99 percent of the commentators don't believe the Bible as it stands.

"[He dwelleth in desolate cities\...ready to become heaps"]{.class_3760} (vs. 28), for as surely as this earth will look like Egypt in Exodus 10:7, the Antichrist's ["seat"]{.class_3762} (Rev. 16:10) will look like Hiroshima did "the day after." Antichrist's ["seat" ]{.class_3762}is connected with the matters in Daniel 12:11; Isaiah 27:10; Jeremiah 7:34, and 48:9.

"[He shall not be rich, neither shall his substance continue\...prolong"]{.class_3760} (vs. 29). The verses match Psalm 52, 58, and a dozen other places in the prophets (Joel, Zephaniah, Obadiah, Isaiah, and Jeremiah). Therefore, to confine Job 15:28 to "wicked men" as a class of people---limited to an ethical discourse on morality---is the height of Bible-rejecting nonsense. ["The flame shall dry up his branches"]{.class_3762} (vs. 30) so that Antichrist is spoken of as a tree (Ezek. 31:8), and ["his branch shall not be green" ]{.class_3762}(vs. 32). (Cf. Isa. 9:14; Ezek. 31:1--3; Psa. 1:3; Jer. 17:5--8, etc.) He loses his [grapes ]{.class_3761}(vs. 33) and his [olives ]{.class_3761}(vs. 33), for his grapes are grapes of [wrath ]{.class_3761}(Deut. 32:32), and his olive oil (type of the Holy Spirit) is the [anointing ]{.class_3761}(Ezek. 28:14) of SATAN. His congregation was made up of hypocrites (vs. 34) who accepted bribes (Dan. 11:32--34) in order to increase their attendance, and they (Heb. 6:8) shall be burned (vs. 34) according to 2 Thessalonians 1:7--8. Ah, the unsearchable riches of the 1611 English. How living it is when compared to the ["Living" Bible.]{.class_3761}

Now, all of what Job says can be applied generally to any wicked man (see vs. 31 for example), but to close down this doctrinal diamond mine on prophecy simply because you lost your tools when you began to alter the [Authorized Text ]{.class_3759}is the height of stupidity and selfishness. If you and your crowd (85 percent of the faculty members of every seminary in the world) cannot mine the diamonds without altering the [Authorized Text ]{.class_3759}with the "original Greek" or the "original Hebrew," how about staying out of the shafts and "lifts" until we get our pockets stuffed? Okay? If you can't find the riches, we can. We have a book that dumps them in our pockets without your help and without your advice. The fact that some of you can't find anything in the Holy Bible without the "originals" is no testimony to your education, training, spirituality, or ability. It is simply a confession that God checked you out years ago, and you weren't smart enough to realize you had been dropped into a crocus sack.

Kenneth Wuest, for example ([The Practical Use of the Greek New Testament]{.class_3759}, Moody Press, 1946), tells us that "certain practical distinctions" in the Bible are "lost" to the student of the English New Testament (p. 51). To illustrate this blatant lie, Kenneth Wuest gives us four samples of "real expositing": he uses Romans 6:13; John 19:30; Ephesians 2:8; and Galatians 4:19. And let it be said (without batting an eyelash) that the "practical applications" of these are so apparent in the [English text ]{.class_3759}of the [AV ]{.class_3759}(1611) that no one could mangle them unless he referred to a Greek text. Telling the gullible student that the "punctiliar action" in John 1:2 and Acts 16:31 "solves another problem which in the English translation could not be solved" is about as truthful as saying that "only one word in a thousand in the Bible is questioned" by Conservative scholars ([Sword of the Lord]{.class_3759}, Oct. 10, 1975). Shut up, you old hypocrites. You ought to be ashamed of yourself.

Kenneth Wuest is very typical of the average Greek teacher in the average "bastion of orthodoxy" today. Setting himself up as the infallible interpreter, Wuest tells us that the student of the [King James Bible ]{.class_3759}"simply cannot cope with the problem" of Hebrews 7:7 (p. 63). He tells us that "the man who knows his Greek Testament" always has more [truth ]{.class_3759}on hand when he has the time and opportunity to give it out. That is, "us Greek scholars" have more truth than you dumb [Bible-believing Christians ]{.class_3759}do! Yeah? Wanna bet? You kiddies that swear on your Greek New Testaments can never find as much truth as a Bible believer because the first requisite for finding truth is a [believing heart ]{.class_3759}and the next requisite for find the truth is a [humble mind]{.class_3759}, and where these two ingredients are missing, sixty-five years of formal education in "the original languages" will no more equip you to teach and preach the truth than sixty-five years in the Army of Red China.

Job 15:20--35 is a reference to the Antichrist, and if your sixty-five years of formal education in Syriac, Coptic, Aramaic, Arabic, Hebrew, and Greek doesn't enable you to discern that, you had better forget your degrees, shelve your books, and start reading the Bible again.

Verses 31 and 35 are self-explanatory. Sow vanity, and you will reap vanity. Conceive mischief (as in Prov. 6:14, 18), and you will bring forth vanity (as in Exod. 2:1). ["Deceit"]{.class_3760} (vs. 35) comes from within a man (Mark 7:22), and the ["belly"]{.class_3760} of verse 35 is used as the word is found in Proverbs 20:27, 30.

Before closing our accounts on Job 15, we shall return to the Scholars Union for a moment and see how they have botched, mangled, and mauled the passage with their Disneyland exegesis. (Look at Wuest \[p. 69\] telling you that the student who uses the Greek Testament of Westcott and Hort has "access to more clearly presented truth" than the student of the English Bible, and therefore, he is "less liable to arrive at erroneous interpretations." ["Interpretations belong to God"]{.class_3760} \[Gen. 40:8\]. Do you ever read the Bible? Which Bible?)

1\. Stumbling blindly along in the dark, Rev. Rawlinson rids himself and his congregation of the reference to the Antichrist by changing ["neither shall he prolong"]{.class_3760} to "neither shall THEIR produce." The gullible [ASV ]{.class_3759}committee (1901) grabs at this alteration like a bargain special and writes: "neither shall THEIR possessions." The lame alibi for this alteration is: "The translation from the singular to the plural is not unusual when it refers to a class (the [Pulpit Commentary]{.class_3759}, p. 281)." Well, look at it this way: The preservation of the singular (["he"]{.class_3760}) to mean ["he"]{.class_3760} is not unusual, and it [can ]{.class_3759}be preserved in the singular since it is [already ]{.class_3759}in the singular. Why not [preserve ]{.class_3759}the truth instead of inventing a lie? You see, there is always "more than one way to look at a thing," isn't there?

Before retiring from the field, Rawlinson feints one more time at verse 19 and says that the reference is to a time when "purity of doctrine remained undisturbed," without telling you what time this was, and this is without telling you that there isn't a case in the history of mankind where "purity of doctrine remained undisturbed." Rawlinson is giving us HPD again in the place of "sound doctrine."

2\. Fausset limits the famine (vs. 23) to only a general reference in the Old Testament, and, again, he has Job interfering with other people's "devotions" in verse 4.

3\. Adam Clarke simply ignores all of the references of the ["flame"]{.class_3760} (vs. 30) and says that it means "his children shall be cut off by sudden judgments." Clarke does cite one Scripture reference for this misinterpretation, but he spiritualizes it so it will not match 2 Thessalonians 2:8; Isaiah 30:33; Daniel 7:10; and Revelation 2:16. And poor brother Clarke (with his Hebrew Old Testament and Greek New Testament) thought that the words of verse 19 referred to Israel entering Canaan in 1450 B.C.

4\. The [Vulgate ]{.class_3759}has mistranslated verse 11 to "can it be a difficult thing for God to comfort thee?"

5\. The [Septuagint ]{.class_3759}has twisted verse 11 into this mess: "Thou hast been scourged lightly for the sins which thou has committed; and thou hast\...blah, blah, blah." This is the text recommended by John R. Rice ([Sword of the Lord,]{.class_3759} Oct. 10, 1975) over the [Authorized Holy Bible]{.class_3759}.

6\. Meredith Kline ([Wycliffe Commentary]{.class_3759}) cannot even attempt to exposit verse 19; so, abandoning the whole Bible momentarily, Kline sails through the passage without making any comment on ["the man of Sin"]{.class_3760} who is called ["that wicked"]{.class_3760} by the Holy Spirit Himself (2 Thess. 2:8).

7\. Taylor has contributed to the cause of liberal theology by quietly erasing the references on the depravity of man (vs. 16) and transferring them to one man only---Job. Taylor (the [Dead Bible]{.class_3759}) has also done this again in verse 14, slyly omitting any reference to anyone ["born of a woman."]{.class_3760} Not even the [New English Bible ]{.class_3759}goes this far in covering up the negative view of man. By comparison with the ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759}, at this point, the [New English Bible ]{.class_3759}is an evangelical, fundamental publication.

8\. ["Collops"]{.class_3760} (vs. 27) should be "lumps" (Bullinger), ["tabernacles"]{.class_3760} (vs. 34) should be "tents" (Bullinger), and ["congregation"]{.class_3760} (vs. 34) should be "assemblies." But why stop there? How about ["earth"]{.class_3760} (vs. 19) should be "land" ([ASV]{.class_3759}, 1901)? ["He knoweth that the day of darkness\..."]{.class_3760} (vs. 23) should be "he is given to be vulture's food" (Peake), verse 29 should not be in the Bible (Dillman, Hitzig), and verse 31 is a "later insertion" (Duhm, Peake, Budde, and Behrman). Or even better. Why not just ignore all of these changes and make up your own bible. You can always sell it because the Bible has always been a "best seller." So just write [your own ]{.class_3759}and make God say what [you ]{.class_3759}want Him to say. Instead of searching the scriptures for the truth, just do it like the other boys do it (write your own list, too; I've listed them long enough to wear out the ribbon). Write down Job 15:17--35 this way:

"I'll show you something, and I've had ten years of college. My dad told me this stuff, and he got it straight out of the Harvard five-foot shelf of classics. There is no such thing as a 'wicked man,' as 'sin' is a relative term and actually means 'failure to realize the best in ourselves and others.' 'Trouble and anguish' are relative terms, and actually they only make us afraid when we fail to overcome our animal heritage and forget to apply the inductive scientific method to unexplainable phenomena. Nobody 'stretches out his hand against God,' because 'God' is a meaningless concept they cannot understand. 'The flame' is not going to burn anybody because you cannot demonstrate that with positivistic empiricism, and people who conceive 'mischief and vanity' are some of the highest paid entertainers on TV today."

And, after all, as far as absolute, infallible TRUTH is concerned, that little lecture is just as good a "bible" as any revision you've seen in the last ninety years; a lie is a lie, and a little leaven ["leaventh the whole lump"]{.class_3760} (Gal. 5:9).

[]{#IMh58iSMTFKKU0J9qyaeHw1393.xhtml}

CHAPTER 16

16:1 Then Job answered and said,

2 I have heard many such things: miserable comforters are ye all.

3 Shall vain words have an end? or what emboldeneth thee that thou answerest?

4 I could also speak as ye do: if your soul were in my soul's stead, I could heap up words against you, and shake mine head at you.

5 But I would strengthen you with my mouth, and the moving of my lips should asswage your grief.

6 Though I speak, my grief is not asswaged: and though I forbear, what am I eased?

Job is adamant. If anybody is wicked, it is the one who is persecuting him (see vss. 9--12). Job's "comforters" are ["miserable comforters"]{.class_3760} (vs. 2), and they are about as comforting as a bastinado administered with iron rods. ["What emboldeneth thee that thou answerest?" ]{.class_3760}(vs. 3). Who asked you to stick your oar in? What do you know about the problem? Did you ever lose your family in one day and your health the next? ["I have heard many such things"]{.class_3760} (vs. 2). Job knows all about the ["destruction of the wicked,"]{.class_3760} since any man a few generations this side of the flood would have it clearly in mind. ["If your soul were in my soul's stead"]{.class_3760} (see Gen. 19:30--34 and comments), ["I could heap up words against you, and shake mine head at you"]{.class_3760} (vs. 4). It is easy to condemn a sick Christian and talk about the judgment of God coming on him; anyone can do it.

Bertha and Martha Siegel lived alone in the tenement district of Baltimore. Bertha was in her forties, and her daughter had been bedridden fifteen years (1942--1957). The worst tormentors that Bertha and Martha Siegel had were Christians who came in and spent hours "comforting" them while suggesting that if they had had enough "faith," Bertha wouldn't have fallen down the cellar stairs and broken her leg, and if they had had enough "faith," etc., etc. The meanest devil this side of hell is the professional healer who blames his failures on his patients (James 5:15) while quoting Hebrews 13:8 in order to filch every sucker in the tent of every dime he has (Mark 2:5).

"[But I would strengthen you with my mouth\...asswage your grief"]{.class_3760} (vs. 5). That is I would be like an iceberg reproving a rose for having a drop of dew on its eyelids; I would encourage you and soothe your troubles. They would melt away like the waters of Genesis 8:1 and evaporate in the wind. ["My grief is not asswaged: and though I forbear, what am I eased?"]{.class_3762} (vs. 6). Job says: "I can talk all night, and it won't change my condition. And if I keep my mouth shut, I am in just as much misery as before." Oh, wretched man!

There is a time when talk ceases to be useful; in man's deepst and direst agonies, words are usually rationed and only come as high priced as the Hope Diamond (\$1,260,000). When the Lord Jesus goes through His brutal agony in Gethsemane, His verbal prayers take up less space than the Twenty--fifth Psalm. "Wrestling" in prayer is more than just telling God what ought to be done, if He knew as much about the situation as you know. Real prayer (Dan. 9:3--4) is the outpouring of the soul, the cry of the broken heart and contrite spirit (Psa. 51) appealing to the Supreme Court of the Universe for relief.

How can you comfort some people? Paul said a minister had to go through plenty (2 Cor. 1:1--4) to qualify as a comforter, and He who sent the "Comforter" went through so much trouble that He was awarded the royal title: ["A man of sorrows, and acquainted with grief"]{.class_3760} (Isa. 53:3).

I talked to a woman in Mobile, Alabama, one time, who had lost two grown sons in one day. She was a Christian herself and had raised these boys right. She had seen them through Whooping Cough, Scarlet Fever, Measles, Mumps, Chicken Pox, etc. She had them both in church, and both of them had received the Lord Jesus as their Saviour. Then God called both of them to preach. When they were twenty and twenty--two years old, they were studying the Bible at a school in Texas. Returning home in the winter, for Christmas vacation, they drove along the Gulf Coast through Biloxi and Pascagoula, Mississippi, on the way to Mobile. A heavy fog---not an uncommon thing on this strip of highway---blanketed the road. Somewhere ahead of them, someone was driving without a tail light. They nearly rammed him before they saw him, and the boys turned off the road to avoid a collision. They hit a cement culvert headon and both of them were killed.

I talked with that Christian mother. I had prayer with her, and I read the Bible to her. But tell me something: Do you think that I could really [comfort ]{.class_3759}her?

I sat up several hours at the bedside of a twelve--year-old girl who was dying of cancer. Her mother and daddy had been living a worldly life, and every time they had tried to get back into church, the church they went to split up in a "business meeting." They had given up finally. Then the twelve-year- old got cancer; she suffered about eight months before she died. I have seen that mother cry when she was sitting by an oxygen tent put over her little girl, while the father stood there with a drawn and somber face. That little girl would paw at the "tent" and say: "Mamma, I don't like this. Mamma, what is this here for?" In about four weeks she was dead. I prayed with that family. I read them the promises. And I hope to God that the ["moving of my lips"]{.class_3760} (vs. 5) helped "assuage their grief," but who knows?

Once (1964) I wrapped the dead body of a little boy up in a blanket (he was about five years old) and carried him out the door to a hearse. I wouldn't let the undertaker's helper do it because when he had carried off the woman's first dead baby (fifteen weeks old), he had picked up the child and thrown him into something that looked like a trumpet case, and then he lugged it out the front door right in the presence of the weeping family. This boy's little brother had died with a hole in his back---a birth defect---and now the older boy had suffered nearly a year with Hodgkin's disease before dying. The poor mother buried two sons within eight months of each other. Do you think that Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar could have ministered to her? Then how under God's heaven could they minister to Job? And do you think that this modern bunch of cool, slick, professional, land-grabbing, money-hugging, educated, pious, "Dale Carnegie" Christian celebrities could really [minister ]{.class_3759}to anybody in real trouble? Many of them aren't "ministers" at all (Matt. 20:28); they are professional motivators and monument builders.

Here is James McGinlay (1950) preaching. His face turns white with pain while he preaches; he flinches at his side when he emphasizes a point. What is the trouble? Well, he only has one kidney left, and a stone is in it. But he must comfort those "in any kind of trouble" (2 Cor. 1:4). [Any volunteers for the "ministry?"]{.class_3759}

I went to the General Hospital (1966) and led a sixty-two-year-old man to Christ. They had picked him up off the street, half dead from a beating someone had given him. He had no one to take him home and no one to take care of his few belongings. So I carried his stuff for him and helped him downstairs and on out to my van. I then drove him "home." "Home" was a second-story room two blocks from the waterfront. Nobody was waiting to meet him; no one was glad to see him return. I let him lean on me as he hobbled upstairs to his "room." He then thanked me for his salvation, and I gave him some tracts. I took one look around the room before I left. He had one bed, unmade, one table with no table cloth, one dresser with the drawers open and dirty underwear hanging out, a broken lamp, two rickety chairs, and a throw-rug that you could have pulled apart with your hands. The windows were open in the summer and had torn screens through which the flies and mosquitoes came in in droves. He had no wife, no children, no friends and, until now, no God.

And I am not going to take up all of your time trying to present myself as a minister. I will close the account by saying that I've had the PRIVILEGE of ministering to scores of men like that one during my work in the rescue missions of Pensacola, Charleston, Savannah, Memphis, St. Petersburg, Atlanta, Mobile, and Blythesville, Arkansas. Some of my "dearly beloved brethren" ([during that time]{.class_3759}) were bellyaching about my "stand" for the [Authorized Version]{.class_3759}, and some of these "dearly beloved brethren" wouldn't be caught dead or alive in the company of anyone who couldn't contribute to their programs, or at least promote them. Well, Job couldn't contribute anything to anyone's ministry or program. He would have been an outcast in the eyes of many ministers today who talk about "worldwide outreaches" and "reaching America for Christ," etc. Job was just like the man living on the road outside Jericho (Luke 10:30); he couldn't get a religious leader to fool with him ten minutes.

It is one thing to build an institution at the expense of [God's authority ]{.class_3759}and the [income of foreign missionaries]{.class_3759}, and it is another thing to "assuage the grief" of those who have been smitten to the ground with poverty, disease, bereavement, and pain.

On one occasion, I led an old gentleman to Christ in the County Home (Pensacola, 1971). The first time I saw him he was crying, and when I talked with him, he said he was crying because he was lonely. Nobody ever came to see him. Left there by a son or daughter (I forget which), he had dragged through the hopeless weeks and months without ever seeing a familiar face. I left him a copy of a color-cartoon book I had written called [Tell It Like It Is]{.class_3759}. A week later, I saw him again. He had been reading the book, but he was still crying. I said, "Are you still lonely?" He said, "No." I then asked him if he was hurting, and he said, "No." I could not resist one more question: "Well, what is wrong? Can I help?" That eighty-four- year-old man looked up at me with eyes that were glistening with tears (and yet shone with a supernatural light) and said, ["I'm crying because I]{.class_3759} [am so happy that I got saved!"]{.class_3759}

16:7 But now he hath made me weary: thou hast made desolate all my company.

8 And thou hast filled me with wrinkles, which is a witness against me: and my leanness rising up in me beareth witness to my face.

9 He teareth me in his wrath, who hateth me: he gnasheth upon me with his teeth; mine enemy sharpeneth his eyes upon me.

10 They have gaped upon me with their mouth; they have smitten me upon the cheek reproachfully; they have gathered themselves together against me.

Job fishes everywhere in the bay to find out who it is that is torpedoing his boat. First it is ["He hath made me weary"]{.class_3760} (vs. 7), and then it is ["thou hast made desolate"]{.class_3760} (vs. 7), then it is[ "they have gaped upon me"]{.class_3760} (vs. 10). ["All these things are against me"]{.class_3760} (Gen. 42:36). But who is wise enough to always discern God's hand in such matters? You can claim Romans 8:28 till you are blue in the face, and you can accept 2 Chronicles 11:4 (["this thing is done of me"]{.class_3760}) till you are white in the face again, but none of us could keep up our prayer and praise 365 days a year in the face of Job's afflictions. I wouldn't even want to attempt to try it in the power of the Holy Spirit with a completed Atonement behind me and a finished Revelation in my hand in front of me. Job attempts it (1:21 and 2:10) [with no Bible and no indwelling Spirit!]{.class_3759} Let those who have been in the crucible of suffering and the refining fires of God's judgments do the preaching at this point: I am an amateur; I have nothing to offer. I could list my real troubles on one hand and still have three fingers left over.

The change of ["he"]{.class_3760} to "thou" in verse 7 is only some more of what we found in Job 14:2, 13. Job has gone from abstract narration into private communication. ["Thou hast made desolate all my company"]{.class_3760} (vs. 7). You did, God; [you ]{.class_3759}did it!

And, strangely enough, there is a great utility in this for the Christian. Even though none of us have a right to accuse God (or the Devil) of doing something He didn't do---and certainly none of us can accuse God of [wrong doing]{.class_3759}, ever---still, some Christians would be 90 percent better off spiritually than they are if they would learn to take a lesson from God as a lesson from God and not an "accident" or a "personality problem" or "the devil using brother so-and-so." No matter how hard God pastes some of His children (Ruth 1:2--5), they never get the message. It is always "Brother Ruckman's fault" or "my wife's fault" or "my husband's fault"; or the "Communists" are to blame or the "environment" is the trouble. I know of a dozen Christians who could be at least half as "godly" as they pretend to be if they would just blame their troubles on God instead of the [Devil ]{.class_3759}and then [submit ]{.class_3759}themselves to God instead of the Devil. One of these good, "godly," etc., Christians (who was always calling people "fanatical nuts" who believed the Bible) accidently fell down a cellar stairway (after [attacking another evangelist ]{.class_3759}through his nationally circulated newspaper), and then he had the gall to say: "The Devil pushed me!" [No he didn't]{.class_3759}. You had your nose stuck so far into other people's business you couldn't see the top of the stairway.

All of Job's ["company"]{.class_3760} (vs. 7) refers to Job 1:13--19. The wrinkles in Job's face are a witness against him (Job 13:26), and they testify to his troubles exactly like Ephraim's gray hairs testify to his troubles (Hos. 7:9--10). Job says that his enemy (whoever it is) has torn him (vs. 9) because he hated him (vs. 9). His enemy gnashes upon him with his teeth (vs. 9) while he is observing him like F. Pindell looking down a rifle barrel. (Pindell fired ten shots through a quarter inch hole at 100 yards: Terre Haute, Indiana, July 1, 1956.)

Verse 10 is aimed right at the Crucifixion of the Lord Jesus Christ (see Psa. 22:13 and Lam. 3:30). And to eliminate this blessed verse from having any prophetic application to the event would be the same as trading your Bible believing birthright for a mess of pottage (i.e., [ASV ]{.class_3759}or [New ASV]{.class_3759}). Adam Clarke makes the ["he"]{.class_3760} of verse 9 refer to Satan and the ["they"]{.class_3760} of verse 20 to be the "demons," but this is a little stretched, unless Job's nightmares (Job 7:14) are pictorial revelations of Satan and demons. Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown overpaint Job's saintliness and say that Job could not have called God his ["enemy"]{.class_3760} (p. 378), nor could he have ascribed "hatred" to God; therefore verse 9 should have read "AS an enemy." But again, this is due to\
a lack of experience, study, common sense, knowledge of the Bible, and knowledge of God. In the first place, if you cannot ascribe "hatred" to God (Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown, p. 378), you are a Bible-rejecting heretic yourself: Note Psalms 5:5, 11:5, 45:7; Proverbs 6:16; Jeremiah 12:8, 44:4, Hosea 9:15; Amos 5:21; Malachi 1:3; Revelation 2:9, etc., etc. This peculiar revulsion which the "majority of Conservative purslane heads" (Job 6:6) have for NEGATIVE TERMS is quite characteristic of Liberal Theology. In the second place, I'll "guarantee you" when Job accuses God of the material found in Job 19:8--13, he blames it directly on God (19:6) and not on the "devil" or "demons" or anything else that any conservative commentator has invented to prove that he is exactly like Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar.

16:11 God hath delivered me to the ungodly, and turned me over into the hands of the wicked.

12 I was at ease, but he hath broken me asunder: he hath also taken me by my neck, and shaken me to pieces, and set me up for his mark.

13 His archers compass me round about, he cleaveth my reins asunder, and doth not spare; he poureth out my gall upon the ground.

14 He breaketh me with breach upon breach, he runneth upon me like a giant.

15 I have sewed sackcloth upon my skin, and defiled my horn in the dust.

16 My face is foul with weeping, and on my eyelids is the shadow of death;

17 Not for any injustice in mine hands: also my prayer is pure.

18 O earth, cover not thou my blood, and let my cry have no place.

19 Also now, behold, my witness is in heaven, and my record is on high.

20 My friends scorn me: but mine eye poureth out tears unto God.

21 O that one might plead for a man with God, as a man pleadeth for his neighbour!

22 When a few years are come, then I shall go the way whence I shall not return.

The first seven words show where Job puts the blame, and Job is right. God HAD delivered him to the ["ungodly,"]{.class_3760} for He had turned him over to Satan ["for the destruction of the flesh"]{.class_3760} (1 Cor. 5:5). Job WAS "in the hands of the wicked" (vs. 11); he had been ["at ease,"]{.class_3760} and God HAD "broken him asunder" (vs. 12). Job likens his position to the position of a POW who has had his legs broken (vs. 12) and then has been strung up by the neck (vs. 12) and shaken before the bowmen gather around him to use him for a target (vs. 13). God is also tearing his skin open with one gash on another (vs. 14) and is stomping him into the ground (vs. 14) like a giant running across a prone body (see Psa. 50:22 and Lam. 2:11). ["I have sewed sackcloth upon my skin, and defiled my horn in the dust"]{.class_3760} (vs. 15). The sackcloth represents a man shorn of his power and authority (Psa. 75:4--5. 92:10. 112: 9; Jer. 48:25, etc.).

"[My face is foul with weeping"]{.class_3760} (vs. 16). It has been drenched with a torrent of tears (Jer. 9:1), and the tears have now dried up without being wiped off. There sits Job, with streaks of dirt and salt lining his face, his skin wrinkled with Elephantiasis, his open sores running pus down into an ash heap where maggots are working their way up on his feet! ["Not for any injustice in mine hands: also my prayer is pure"]{.class_3762} (vs. 17). But there he is: DESTITUTE. His wife is against him; she is down at the Circuit Court trying to get his savings account. His children are dead and buried. His friends are accusing him of sin, and Satan is having a field day with him for "no cause" (Job 2:3 and John 15:25). ["Oh earth, cover not thou my blood, and let my cry have no place"]{.class_3762} (vs. 18).

Job's blood will cry from the ground like Abel's (Heb. 11:4) if his murderer gets away with the deed (Gen. 4:11). ["O earth, cover not thou my blood"]{.class_3760} (vs. 18) ties Deuteronomy 21; Numbers 35; and Matthew 27:25 into one compact unit, seals it, stamps it, and deposits it in the safety vault of the Eternal Bank of God so that no man can open it to "add thereto" or take away "therefrom." Rev. Rawlinson's subtle comment (the [Pulpit Commentary]{.class_3759}, p. 281) that "there was a [widespread belief ]{.class_3759}in the ANCIENT WORLD that innocent blood spilt upon the ground cried to God for vengeance" (without giving one cross-reference for the statement) is just about as theologically close to Harry Emerson Fosdick, Eugene Carson Blake, and Leslie Weatherhead as you can move without getting into the same bed with them.

The allusion to the murder of Abel is so strong this time that not even Adam Clarke misses it, although he still will not move the date of Job's authorship back to 1800--1700 B.C. But most of our "godly, devoted, dedicated commentators," who "stand without apology for Christian principles and Christian practices" with the "Lord Jesus as the center of the etc., etc.," slam right past the verse like the 1901 mail train (Savannah, Florida, and Western) went through Long County, Georgia ---120 miles per hour.

Neither Heavenor ([NBC]{.class_3759}) nor Kline ([WBC]{.class_3759}) nor Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown nor Dummelow, Lange, Ellicott, or Henry can find the New Testament applications for Job's lament. But it is Christ's prayer that is pure, and it is Christ's cry (Matt. 27:25) that has ["no place."]{.class_3760} No injustice was found in His hands. His witness was ["on high"]{.class_3760} (vs. 19). He [did ]{.class_3759}pour out tears unto God (Heb. 5:7), and verse 16 matches Psalm 69:10 and 20 so closely that only a "Christ-exalting" Christian who stood for the "verbally, plenary, inspired originals" could possibly miss the reference to the One who was to be given "the preeminence" in all things. Psalm 69:8--9, 13, 22, 27--28 are so plainly Messianic that you would have to be a Bible-rejecting destructive critic (whose "unquestioned loyalty to the word of God," etc.) to miss the application. So---90 percent of the commentators miss it.

Moral: If you [really ]{.class_3759}want to exalt the Lord Jesus Christ and [magnify ]{.class_3759}Him in your life as a child of God, don't waste five minutes with the men who recommend (or use) the translations published since 1880, especially the [New ASV]{.class_3759} (any edition).

Again, Job is right. There is no injustice in his hands, although there is still self-righteousness in his heart (see Job 31). Job's cry was heard in heaven, and it "had no place" (vs. 18) on this earth to stop and rest. His witness was in heaven (vs. 19), and his record was on high (vs. 19), exactly like yours is (Rev. 20:12).

"[My friends scorn me"]{.class_3760} (vs. 20) is the truth as is ["but mine eye poureth out tears unto God"]{.class_3762} (vs. 20). ["O that one might plead for a man with God, as a man pleadeth for his neighbour!"]{.class_3762} (vs. 21). This is a variation of the plea of 13:18 and 9:33. Job cries for an advocate who can plead a man's case before God exactly as a defense attorney pleads for a criminal. This time, Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown (and others) finally get the Messianic reference through their thick Bible-denying noodles, so that some of them now note that this is a "prefiguring of the Advocacy of Jesus Christ." But how did they find the reference here and miss the one on ["my blood"]{.class_3762} in verse 18?

"[When a few years are come, then I shall go\...I shall not return"]{.class_3760} (vs. 22). See Job 14:10, 12, 20, and 10:21--22.

The ["shadow of death"]{.class_3760} (vs. 16) is on Job's face (see 3:5 and 10:21--22). He is near to death, so he wants just one chance to plead with God (see Isa. 1:18) before departing into the underworld.

The alterations made on the God-honored text (vss. 11--22) are too numerous to mention. The [Septuagint ]{.class_3759}has "spears" all over verse 13 and has changed the number and person of the pronouns in the passage so many times you can't keep track of anything. Job's belly is "parched with wailing" (vs. 17)--- whatever on earth that means---and Job has been "knocked on his knees" in verse 11. The "Living"---saints preserve us!--- [Bible ]{.class_3759}has "daggers" stuck into verse 10, a "forelock" showing up in verse 15, and a "sincere" prayer instead of a "PURE" one (vs. 17); and finally, Job's cheeks are not "foul" with weeping (vs. 16), but are merely "flushed." The [New English Bible ]{.class_3759}doesn't like the unvarnished terminology of verse 16 either so Dodd gets off with "my eyes are red." Adam Clarke reads, "But the years numbered to me are come" for verse 22.

The [New Scofield Reference Bible ]{.class_3759}has substituted [ASV ]{.class_3759}(1901) readings for the Authorized Text in verse 17 (also in 15:34 and 17:16) while professing to be "The Authorized Version." Ho hum. The [Wycliffe Commentator ]{.class_3759}(Meredith Kline) recommends the [ASV ]{.class_3759}(1901) to replace the [Authorized Text ]{.class_3759}in verses 9, 17, 19, and 21, while the changes recommended by Duhm, Budde, Schlottmann, Dillmann, Siegreid, Peake, Delitzsch, and others are about as enlightening as a revision of [Tatian's Diatesseron ]{.class_3759}by Mick Jagger.

The belated recognition of the Advocate (vs. 19), by altering ["record"]{.class_3760} to "He" or "Him," is pitiful in view of the "innocent blood" mentioned in verse 18, without so much as a peep from the major commentators about Matthew 27:25 or Deuteronomy 21:7--8. But such is the foolishness and folly of "Christian Education" where bloated toad frogs sit in judgment of the word of God. Where Bible-rejecting Fundamentalists reject the Bible for the unknown "originals," there is about as much chance of finding the truth as finding a whisper in a whirlwind.

[]{#IMh58iSMTFKKU0J9qyaeHw1453.xhtml}

CHAPTER 17

17:1 My breath is corrupt, my days are extinct, the graves are ready for me.

2 Are there not mockers with me? and doth not mine eye continue in their provocation?

3 Lay down now, put me in a surety with thee; who is he that will strike hands with me?

4 For thou hast hid their heart from understanding: therefore shalt thou not exalt them.

5 He that speaketh flattery to his friends, even the eyes of his children shall fail.

6 He hath made me also a byword of the people; and aforetime I was as a tabret.

7 Mine eye also is dim by reason of sorrow, and all my members are as a shadow.

Job continues with his lament. He says his breath stinks (vs. 1), as it often does in certain diseases. The graves are ready for him (vs. 1). His arms and legs are so skinny they have almost lost their substance (vs. 7), and his eyes are clouded with mist and tears ["by reason of sorrow"]{.class_3760} (vs. 7). Continual weeping has been known to produce blindness in a few rare cases. ["Are there not mockers with me?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 2). Well, the three "comforters" might as well be mocking, for they are accusing him of being either the Antichrist or one of his right hand men.

"[Doth not mine eye continue in their provocation?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 2). The verse is unintelligible in the corrupt [Septuagint]{.class_3761}, and the versions suggested by Good, Calmer, Heath, Duhm, and Budde are not much better. The meaning is quite clear: Job is saying, "Don't I have some people here making fun of me? And don't you think I can't see them? They haven't disappeared from sight because I'm still looking right at them." (For the ["graves"]{.class_3762} see Gen. 35:20, 50:5; Num. 19:16; 2 Sam. 3:32; Psa. 88:5; Nah. 1:14; and 2 Chron. 34:28.)

Verse 3 should be read with Proverbs 6:1; 17:18 and 22:26. Who will bet his life against Job's life? Who will "put up" (vs. 3: ["lay down"]{.class_3760}) the required amount of bail for Job unless God Himself does it? None of Job's friends could match his righteous life; so how could they bail him out or keep the collection agency from "repossessing" everything he had? ["Thou hast hid their heart from understanding"]{.class_3760} (vs. 4) means that God has prevented the three comforters from understanding the situation, and ["therefore thou shalt not exalt them"]{.class_3760} (vs. 4). And God does NOT exalt them; he exalts Job (Job 42:7--8).

"[He that speaketh flattery to his friends"]{.class_3760} (vs. 5). (See Prov. 29:5, 20:19, 26:28; Job 32:21--22; and Psa. 12:3.) In the Tribulation, these eyes (vs. 5) are to be "plucked out by the ravens" (Prov. 30:17) at Armageddon (Rev. 19:17). Job does not want his friends to butter him up and "apple polish" him; he just wants sympathy, which he is not getting. But, since the verse had a Second Advent application, it is torn to shreds by Dummelow, Jamieson, Fausset, and Brown, and the apostate revision committee of 1901 (the [ASV]{.class_3761}). Unable to find the cross references, these Bible-rejecting "Conservatives" (recommended as having superior intelligence by John Rice) have altered the verse to "delivered up his friends," "denounced his friends," and even "promise mischief to his companions." Taylor (the [Dead Bible]{.class_3761}) has stuck the word "bribes" into the text, and the [NEB ]{.class_3761}follows the stinking corruption of 1884 ([RV]{.class_3761}).

But you see, the trouble is that the Bible-rejecting Fundamentalists and Conservatives can't understand WHY Job would suddenly say ["flattery"]{.class_3760} when no one has been flattering him. Therefore! Therefore, the "rule of thumb" established by Origen and Philo comes into play: [If you can't understand it, change it.]{.class_3759}

1\. If you can't understand [Jewish salvation ]{.class_3759}in the Tribulation, use the Greek to alter James and Hebrews to match the Body of Christ in this age.

2\. If you can't understand [Eternal Retribution ]{.class_3759}(Russell and Rutherford), change it to "annihilation."

3\. If you can't understand [assurance of Eternal Life]{.class_3759}, change it to "purgatory" or "Limbo."

4\. If you can't understand a sinless man being born of a woman, then change the woman's "conception" into an "Immaculate Conception."

5\. If you can't understand exactly where the Body of Christ began, pretend it began with [Paul and then get rid of everything Paul wrote about it until Acts 28.]{.class_3759}

6\. If you can't understand [the New Birth]{.class_3759}, change it into a water birth produced by the city water system.

7\. If you can't understand Revelation 22:14 (like John R. Rice and Robert Sumner), change it to match the Catholic Bible of 1582---which matches the corrupt [RV ]{.class_3759}of 1885 and the corrupt [ASV ]{.class_3759}of 1901---and pretend you are a "champion of the faith."

If you can't understand---but why go on? Let's cut the deck and deal the cards out face up. If you can't understand [anything]{.class_3759}, then just go ahead and drag the holy words of God down into the mud where God can condescend to the level of stupidity that [you ]{.class_3759}operate on. Go on, some of you are going to do it anyway. Just bring God down to your educational level where [you ]{.class_3759}can help [Him ]{.class_3759}out with His errors, and then students will think YOU are God, right? Or superior to God, right? Isn't that about it?

And may we say to those of you Christian Educators who are inclined in that direction---with all the charity that we can muster: "If God Almighty doesn't 'OPEN' your stupid understanding (Luke 24:45) so that you can 'understand the Scriptures' (Dan. 2:28--30), don't think for a minute that twenty years of 'Greek and Hebrew' in a 'Christian university' will equip you to understand the Bible any more than it will equip you to explain the Bermuda Triangle."

People did speak flattery to Job (see vs. 6: ["Aforetime I was as a tabret,"]{.class_3760} and compare this with Job 29:8--23), while he did NOT speak flattery to anyone (Job 31:21, 29, 34). Job's speech is perfectly clear to any Christian but the deluded reprobates who revised the ASV of 1901 and the NASV of 1959. Job could have flattered his friends (vss. 8 and 9), but instead, he calls the spade a spade.

"[He hath made me also a byword of the people"]{.class_3760} (vs. 6), and notice how this matches Psalm 69:11 where it deals with the Lord Jesus, and, again, in Deuteronomy 28:37 (and 1 Kings 9:7) where it matches Israel in this dispensation. A ["byword"]{.class_3762} is a short saying about a person or a nation, such as: "The Boche are either kneeling at your feet or standing on your neck" or "The bride at a Polish wedding is the one wearing the clean T-shirt" or "The only thing dumber than a dumb Irishman is a smart Swede," etc. Job's great byword becomes: "Poor as Job's turkey," as Adam's great byword became: "Poor as Adam's housecat." ("The Adam's apple" also figures, but like a man said: "The trouble wasn't the apple in the tree; it was the [pair ]{.class_3761}on the ground.")

Job was as a ["tabret"]{.class_3760} in the sense that he had caused joy to people like a musical instrument (Ezek. 28:13). The tabret set people's feet to tapping (Gen. 31:27; 1 Sam. 10:5, 18:6); it made them "feel real good" (as they say down South). But the Scholars Union will never allow this reading to stand either. Still professing to be printing an [Authorized Version]{.class_3759}, the lying committee of the [New Scofield Reference Bible ]{.class_3759}(p. 583) has torn out[ "as a tabret"]{.class_3760} and added "as one before whom men spit." (And this was done after talking about "improving the REFERENCE [notes]{.class_3759}" of the old [Scofield Bible]{.class_3759}! Ain't we got some "godly" folks on our side? Eh, boy?)

Adam Clarke decides if English, Walvoord, Smith, McClain, Mason, McCrae, Feinberg, Culbertson, and Gaebelein can make up their own "bible," then so can he; so Clarke says: "I shall be as a furnace."

And now that the pace-setters have the pack in line, off they go with a roar! The ["Living" Bible]{.class_3759}: "they spit in my face"; the [New English Bible]{.class_3759}: "a portent for all to see;" Fausset: "an object of disgust"; and so forth and so on. "Step right this way, ladies and gentlemen! Who'll be next! Anyone can write a Bible! You too may have hidden talent. Send in a coupon today with \$15.00 in cash, check, or money order and learn how you too may\...."

Now the thinking of these ding-a-ling expositors is this: The Hebrew word here (["topheth,"]{.class_3759} and ["toph"]{.class_3759}---the Hebrew for "TABRET") was taken from the wrong "root." The "root" should have been ["tuph"]{.class_3760} (Hebrew: Tau, Shureq, Pe) meaning: "to spit out with contempt." However, when we run these briarhoppers down, we find "ROOT NOT USED" ([Analytical Hebrew and Chaldean Lexicon]{.class_3759}, p. 752)! The word for "spitting"---used throughout the Scripture (Num. 12:14; Deut. 25:9; 1 Sam. 21:13; Isa. 50:6; Lev. 15:8)---is anything but ["Tuph."]{.class_3759} Sometimes the Hebrew word is [rir]{.class_3759}, sometimes it is [yaraq]{.class_3759} or [roq]{.class_3759} or [raqaq]{.class_3759}, but never ["Tuph."]{.class_3759} The alteration of the text, therefore, was done to [make it match Psalm 69:11 ]{.class_3759}and to turn it into a Messianic reference. This was entirely unnecessary and peculiarly indecent in view of the fact that the majority of the men who altered the text [missed the Messianic references already ]{.class_3759}given by the Holy Spirit in 16:8 and 16:11.

17:8 Upright men shall be astonied at this, and the innocent shall stir up himself against the hypocrite.

9 The righteous also shall hold on his way, and he that hath clean hands shall be stronger and stronger.

10 But as for you all, do ye return, and come now: for I cannot find one wise man among you.

11 My days are past, my purposes are broken off, even the thoughts of my heart.

12 They change the night into day: the light is short because of darkness.

13 If I wait, the grave is mine house: I have made my bed in the darkness.

14 I have said to corruption, Thou art my father: to the worm, Thou art my mother, and my sister.

15 And where is now my hope? as for my hope, who shall see it?

16 They shall go down to the bars of the pit, when our rest together is in the dust.

Job's sufferings will cause upright men to be astonished, and the old English ["astonied"]{.class_3760} is as good a way to put it as any: Like a stone---petrified. The innocent man (Matt. 27:4), who is suffering without cause (John 15:25), will stir himself up against the hypocrite (Psa. 69:22) because the hypocrite will assume that if he himself does not suffer, it is because he is "godly" (1 Tim. 6:5). The reader will observe that, again, the passage matches the Crucifixion passages on the Lord Jesus Christ (Isa. 52:14), although all of the commentators drop their Messianic references at verse 6. Most of them dropped the references at 16:19.

"[The righteous also shall hold on his way"]{.class_3760} (vs. 9) and even gather courage from Job's afflictions (["shall be stronger and stronger"]{.class_3762}) exactly like Paul's friends were encouraged by his imprisonment (see Phil. 1:14 and comments). What was an ["evident token of perdition"]{.class_3762} to the ungodly (Phil. 1:28) was a token of ["salvation, and that of God"]{.class_3762} (Phil. 1:28) for the truly "godly." A righteous man can take courage in affliction when he sees someone like Job "enduring to the end" (James 5:11), for he knows that Job is right regardless of his condition (see Matt. 27:54 and comments in that Commentary).

"[But as for YOU ALL"]{.class_3760} (vs. 10)---Job turns on his friends in good old Dixie style---["do ye return, and come now: for I cannot find one wise man among you"]{.class_3762} (vs. 10). That is, "go back where you came from and rethink this thing before you come around here dumping all of these 'guilt complexes' on me!"

But after this blast, Job quickly sinks back once more into his diseased suffering and goes on in a lonely soliloquy: "My time is up; I don't even know why I am still alive. I don't have any purpose in living (vs. 11). My imaginations run riot (vs. 11) so that they keep me awake at night (vs. 12), and then darkness comes again before I know it (vs. 12). I don't have any hope (vs. 15). You say, 'Wait and things will turn out all right?' Well, ['If I wait, the grave is mine\...I have said to corruption, Thou art my father: to the worm, Thou art my mother, and my sister']{.class_3760} (vss. 13--14). ['As for my hope, who shall see it?']{.class_3760} (vs. 15)." This verse and preceding it should be read in conjunction with Job 30:29--30; Proverbs 7:4; Psalm 22:4--9; Jonah 2:6; and Isaiah 38:10. The applications to the Lord Jesus are evident: Jonah was a type of Christ in His death, burial, and resurrection (Matt. 27--28). The ["THEY"]{.class_3760} of verse 16 is ["corruption"]{.class_3760} and the ["worm,"]{.class_3760} from verse 14; THEY shall ["go down to the bars of the pit, when OUR rest together is in the dust."]{.class_3760}

Of this last verse, Rev. Rawlinson confesses blankly that "the present commentator must acknowledge that he is unable to attach any satisfactory meaning to the words of the Hebrew text" (p. 296). Being a little more [dishonest ]{.class_3759}than Rev. Rawlinson, the [ASV ]{.class_3759}committee of 1901 simply changes the verse so that it has a "satisfactory meaning" to them. The [NEB ]{.class_3759}makes this last verse [negative ]{.class_3759}since Dodd couldn't understand it either, and so the whole agnostic crew of blind guides (recommended by Bob Jones III and John R. Rice) begin to contribute their share of ignorance, with the closest guess being that both Job and his ["hope"]{.class_3760} (vs. 16: ["THEY shall go down"]{.class_3760}) go somewhere where there are no ["bars of the pit."]{.class_3760} However, in view of the fact that Hell has [gates ]{.class_3759}(Matt. 16:18), bars (Jonah 2:2), and [keys ]{.class_3759}(Rev. 1:18), it is highly presumptuous (if not downright lunatic) to get rid of the ["bars"]{.class_3760} just because you never believed the Bible to start with. An ounce of Christian faith is worthy of a ton of Mickey Mouse scholarship---anytime.

On a bank in Panama City, Florida, will be found three letters: CCC. They are supposed to stand for Care, Concern, and Courtesy. And if there were three things on this earth Job did NOT experience it was these three things. His miserable comforters (16:2) had rather registered Criticism, Complaints, and Condemnation. They were "miserable" saints themselves, without knowing it, and if you want to be as miserable as they were:

1\. Go to prayer meeting with no desire to pray.

2\. Go to hear the word preached without obeying it.

3\. Attend church while you hate and despise your brethren.

4\. Go to a testimony meeting when you don't have any testimony to give.

5\. Spend your life comparing yourself with someone you don't like.

[]{#IMh58iSMTFKKU0J9qyaeHw1506.xhtml}

CHAPTER 18

18:1 "Then answered Bildad the Shuhite, and said,

2 How long will it be ere ye make an end of words? mark, and afterwards we will speak.

3 Wherefore are we counted as beasts, and reputed vile in your sight?

4 He teareth himself in his anger: shall the earth be forsaken for thee? and shall the rock be removed out of his place?

5 Yea, the light of the wicked shall be put out, and the spark of his fire shall not shine.

6 The light shall be dark in his tabernacle, and his candle shall be put out with him.

7 The steps of his strength shall be straitened, and his own counsel shall cast him down.

8 For he is cast into a net by his own feet, and he walketh upon a snare.

9 The gin shall take him by the heel, and the robber shall prevail against him.

10 The snare is laid for him in the ground, and a trap for him in the way.

11 Terrors shall make him afraid on every side, and shall drive him to his feet.

12 His strength shall be hungerbitten, and destruction shall be ready at his side.

13 It shall devour the strength of his skin: even the firstborn of death shall devour his strength.

14 His confidence shall be rooted out of his tabernacle, and it shall bring him to the king of terrors.

15 It shall dwell in his tabernacle, because it is none of his: brimstone shall be scattered upon his habitation.

16 His roots shall be dried up beneath, and above shall his branch be cut off.

17 His remembrance shall perish from the earth, and he shall have no name in the street.

18 He shall be driven from light into darkness, and chased out of the world.

19 He shall neither have son nor nephew among his people, nor any remaining in his dwellings.

20 They that come after him shall be astonied at his day, as they that went before were affrighted.

21 Surely such are the dwellings of the wicked, and this is the place of him that knoweth not God.

There is hardly a break in the thought of the chapter, although the first three verses may serve as introductory remarks; however, the chapter in its entirety is aimed at the ["wicked"]{.class_3760} (vs. 5), so we can place sixteen of the verses right down on that ["wicked one"]{.class_3760} of 2 Thessalonians 2 without consulting Dr. Payne or Dr. Biederwolf (or any of their 400 friends) one time. As we have said before, the verses are spoken with Job in mind---who is not guilty of the things being said---but they are also spoken of any wicked man in any age. However, they go even beyond this into Daniel's Seventieth Week, when the Man of Sin is "bringing in the Kingdom" with the help of the United Nations, the NCC, UNESCO, the RCC, and "THE PEOPLE'S party."

Bildad takes up the gauntlet, and says, ["How long will it be ere ye make an end of words?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 2). Well, Job might have shut up fourteen chapters back if they hadn't provoked him to speak ["unadvisedly with his lips"]{.class_3760} (Psa. 106:33). ["Mark, and afterwards we will speak"]{.class_3760} (vs. 2). That is, "Shut up a minute and get your mind on what WE are going to say, and then we'll say it." Verse 3 is self-explanatory. Dummelow's recommendation on verse 2 ("how long will ye lay snares for words") is from the corrupt [RV ]{.class_3759}of 1885 and the corrupt [ASV ]{.class_3759}of 1901, which have made it to read: "How long will ye HUNT for words." Mr. Good says: "How long will ye plant thorns?" Taylor's paraphrase of the verse is about as much a paraphrase of the Bible as [The Winds of War ]{.class_3759}is a paraphrase of [The Godfather]{.class_3759}. (Why not just "Paraphrase" vss. 2--3 as this: How many months will it be before Flight 675 gets in from Tampa? Check your left rear tire and see if it has thirty pounds. Why did the price of butter go down when Johnny Bench batted .382?" I mean, do it up right son, if you're going to play "God" at all. Go all the way.)

"[He teareth himself in his anger" ]{.class_3760}(vs. 4). This is said to Zophar and Eliphaz while nodding at (or pointing at) Job. The idea is: "God isn't tearing Job up (see 16:9, 14); Job is his own worst enemy." And Job may have been tearing his own clothes or body when these words were spoken, but when pain and exhaustion have reached their "threshold limits" or levels, a man can do some strange things.

Vincent VanGogh (1848--1903) cut off his ear, and before he did that he held his hand over a candle flame till the skin scorched and smoked. Men under shellfire (WWI) tried to dig themselves into the ground with their [teeth]{.class_3759}, and many of them banged their heads against the walls of the dugouts during their terror-stricken panics. Men have bitten their arms nearly in two to keep awake during a perilous situation where [sleep ]{.class_3759}would have cost them their lives. Patients in asylums have gone berserk and had to be straight-jacketed after they tried to embrace hot stoves or radiators that were so hot they would have given them second degree burns. The maniac of Gadara cut himself with [stones ]{.class_3759}(Mark 5:5), and in his demoniac madness, he was no different than the Baalite "fathers" (Judg. 18:18; 1 Kings 18:28) who cut themselves till the blood ["gushed out upon them."]{.class_3760} Pain, frustration, madness, loneliness, fear, and anger can make a man do some terrible things.

"[Shall the earth be forsaken for thee?"]{.class_3760} (vs. 4). Shall God stop His work and take time out for you? Doesn't God have to take care of 2,000,000,000 people daily while He is feeding 40,000,000,000 fish and 60,000,000,000 insects and 20,000,000 mammals, while (at the same time!) He is tending to galaxies, constellations, and nebulae? ["Shall the rock be removed out of his place?"]{.class_3762} (vs. 4). Job had just said (14:18) that the rocks could be moved from their place. Is God obligated to drum up an avalanche or an earthquake for Job's benefit just to prove that He hears Job (Luke 4:10)?

Now, all that remains (vss. 5--21) is self-explanatory with the exception of one or two "difficult passages" which the commentators and revisers were unable to understand; therefore, they followed their habitual practice: They altered the words of God to suit their own deluded, apostate, private fancies.

The ["light of the wicked"]{.class_3760} (vs. 5) shall be put out (Exod. 10:23; Psa. 119:105) according to 1 Kings 11:36. ["The spark of his fire shall not shine"]{.class_3760} (vs. 5), either when his "prophet" calls it down from heaven (Rev. 13:13) or when his followers carry their "sparklers" around on the 5th of May (or the 1st of May). (See Isaiah 50:11 and the work by Hislop, [The Two Babylons]{.class_3759}.)

Verse 8 is more of the same. The wicked man's steps shall be squeezed into a narrow path (Num. 22:22--26) so that he will eventually fall into a trap he himself set on the pathway (vss. 8--10). (See Prov. 1:17--18 and comments.) The trap, the net, the snare, and the ["GIN"]{.class_3760} (Heb: ["pach"]{.class_3759}) will get him like it got Israel in Romans 11:9, 10 and Psalm 69:22. Lions (Ezek. 19:1--6) are taken in this kind of a trap, and since the Devil goes about as a ["roaring lion"]{.class_3760} (1 Pet. 5:8), the direct reference is to the Antichrist again. The ["gin"]{.class_3760} is a small metal trap that can clamp down on an animal's heel (vs. 9). The trap is a large wooden cage with a falling door on the front of it, the snare is a looped rope on the ground, and the "net" is a net spread over a pit and then covered with leaves and underbrush. African lions are captured alive by setting up a long line of drum beaters in a semi-circle near an area which lions inhabit. These drummers than move forward, about twenty yards apart, all beating at the same time. Lions will not attack 100--500 men coming at them like this, so they "skedaddle." Native hunters, who have lived for decades in the area, know the "lion paths" just like a deer hunter in Alabama knows the "deer crossings." Along these paths, the pits have been dug, about seven feet deep and eight feet square, and a large net has been placed over these pits---the diameter of the vine rope is about an inch thick---and then the four corners of the net (see Acts 10:11) are tied with vertical fastenings and attached to a large flexible tree which has been bent almost double and locked into place by a log. Then the whole contraption is covered with grass, leaves, and branches. If any lion crosses the path, down he goes into the pit! And when his weight pulls the bent tree forward a foot or more, the log-lock drops out of place and the tree springs back up---carrying Leo with it. So there he is, hanging three to five feet off the ground, in a net bag that he couldn't chew in half in less than an hour.

It is terror (vs. 11) which drives the ["roaring lion"]{.class_3760} to his feet (vs. 11) and drives him into God's ["net"]{.class_3760} (see Psa. 141:10; 35:8, and Ezek. 12:13). The verses which the Scholars Union finds difficult are verses 12--14 and 19, so we will allow the [Authorized Text ]{.class_3759}to give them the assistance they so sorely need.

Now, the King of the Bottomless Pit is called ["DESTRUCTION"]{.class_3760} (Rev. 9:11), and it is "destruction" who abides by the side of the Antichrist (vs. 12). He will be ["hungerbitten,"]{.class_3760} exactly as a freezing man is "frost-bitten," and everyone understands the expression perfectly. Destruction (["Apollyon"]{.class_3760}---APOLLOS) will devour ["the strength of his skin"]{.class_3760} (vs. 13) as he probably will be a LEPER (2 Chron. 26:21) who spreads leprosy (Rev. 16:2) from a LEOPARD (Rev. 13:2), whose SPOTS (Job 11:15) are those of an Ethiopian (Jer. 13:23), exactly as Cain's MARK.

"[The firstborn\...death"]{.class_3760} (vs. 13) will be the death angel himself (Rev. 9:11) who operates under the Devil (Heb. 2:14) to kill ["the firstborn"]{.class_3762} (Exod. 12:23), exactly as you find it in the "original English" without any reference to any Greek or Hebrew text available or unavailable. That is, the Antichrist is his own destruction, for he operates under the ["king of terrors"]{.class_3762} (vs. 14), and that king will destroy him (Ezek. 31--32) when he is through with him, exactly as he destroyed all of his "seven heads." (See Nimrod, Pharaoh, Sennacherib, Nebuchadnezzar, Cyrus, Alexander the Great, and Caesar in Rev. 17:8--10, with comments.)

The [brimstone ]{.class_3759}is not only in the Lake of Fire (in eternity), but it is also the "habitation" of the Antichrist in the Millennium (Isa. 34:9--10; Matt. 25:41; and Obad. 18). Verses 16--17 will match the Advent passages in Malachi 4:1--3, and the ["nephew" ]{.class_3760}of verse 19 ties the passage right down to Isaiah 14:22 and Psalm 109:10, 12--13. This gives us [sixteen prophetic references ]{.class_3759}in one chapter or more than thirty references in the first eighteen chapters of Job. Therefore, any shallow and superficial work like Dr. Payne's ["Encyclopedia" of Biblical Prophecy]{.class_3759} is to be bypassed as quickly as a washed-out dirt road. If any "Encyclopedia on Prophecy" fails to list a minimum of [fifty prophetic references ]{.class_3759}in the Book of Job, it has been written by a [careless critic ]{.class_3759}of the word of God, not a [real student ]{.class_3759}of the Scripture. Those of us who take our Bible studies "seriously" never waste time with the scholars whose "Christian" education threw them off the deepend and into the morass of infidelity that destroyed America and England between 1900 and 1960.

"[He shall have no name in the street"]{.class_3760} (vs. 17), both in the sense of no one being interested in talking about him anymore and in the sense of no more streets named after him (1 Kings 20:34). If you had ten dollars for every street in America named after a Bible-denying politician, scientist, educator, communist, or military leader, you could bribe the NAACP into setting up "separate" schools.

The Antichrist is driven off this earth (vs. 18) and chased into outer darkness (vs. 18) that is composed of FIRE (vs. 15), and this is exactly how Job puts it. (See Job 20:7, 26, and comments.)

All is in order. The passage has described the destiny of an unsaved man headed for Hell; it described the Son of God delivered to Satan (Isa. 53:9--10) at his death; and it described the earthly end and future state of the Antichrist. Since this mass of material is found in the sixteen verses, we should not be surprised to find that the Scholars Union is having more trouble with the passage than the UN is having with Palestine.

1\. The ["Living"]{.class_3759}---may God forgive us!---[Bible ]{.class_3759}has gotten rid of the ["firstborn"]{.class_3760} (vs. 13), the ["nephew" ]{.class_3760}has been converted to a grandson (vs. 19), and the Antichrist has no ["dwellings"]{.class_3760} (vs. 21) in eternity and no set ["day"]{.class_3760} (vs. 20) in which to reign.

2\. The [New Scofield Reference Bible ]{.class_3759}has sacked the [AV ]{.class_3759}readings in verses 3, 6, 7, 9, 14, 15, 17, and 20. (But you understand you are still to pretend that it is "The [Authorized Version]{.class_3759}" because so-and-so and so-and-so and so-and-so and so-and-so couldn't possibly be deceived because they were "dedicated, devoted, godly, separated, fundamental, soul-winning CROOKS." \[Matt. 16:12\])

3\. Dummelow has altered verses 2, 4, 9, 11, and 17 to match the apocryphal [RV ]{.class_3759}of 1884 which broke the ground for the apocryphal [ASV ]{.class_3759}of 1901.

4\. Adam Clarke is unable to comment on verse 12, so he has given us a Chaldean paraphrase.
